topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Components of Cooperative Robotic Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> In Cooperative Robotics, the **"Components"** refer to the integrated set of hardware, software, communication, and architectural elements that enable multiple robotic agents to perceive, reason, act, and coordinate with each other and the environment to achieve a common goal. These components collectively form a **Multi-Robot System (MRS)**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a **swarm of honeybees** building a hive together:

- Each bee is a single robot (an **agent**).
- Each bee has **eyes** (sensors), **wings** (actuators), and a tiny **brain** (controller).
- Bees communicate through **dances** (communication protocols).
- There is no single "boss bee" giving orders to every bee — instead, the swarm follows **simple local rules** that produce intelligent global behavior (decentralized architecture).

A **cooperative robotic system** is essentially an engineered, programmable version of this swarm — except instead of bees, we use wheeled/underwater/aerial robots, and instead of dances, we use Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, or ROS topics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:**
> The four pillars of any cooperative robotic system are:
> 1. **Perception** (Sensors & Sensing)
> 2. **Action** (Actuators & Locomotion)
> 3. **Computation** (Controllers, Software, Algorithms)
> 4. **Communication** (Inter-robot networks & Protocols)

---

### 1.2 The Four Major Component Categories

| # | Category | Purpose | Real-World Example |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Hardware Components** | Physical body of the robot | Motors, wheels, arms, chassis |
| 2 | **Sensing Components** | Perceive the environment & self-state | LiDAR, IMU, cameras, encoders |
| 3 | **Computational / Software Components** | Decision-making & coordination | ROS, microcontrollers, planners |
| 4 | **Communication Components** | Exchange data between robots | Wi-Fi modules, ZigBee, CAN bus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Board Evaluations:**
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent motions a robot can perform. A typical mobile base has **3 DOF** (x, y, θ), while a 6-DOF arm has 6 independent joints.
> - **Workspace:** The region in 3-D space that a robot's end-effector can reach, measured in **m³** (cubic meters).

---

### 1.3 Why "Components" Matter in Cooperative Robotics

Unlike a single (solitary) robot, a cooperative system must handle:
- **Distributed sensing** — multiple robots observe different parts of the world.
- **Task allocation** — dividing work among robots.
- **Shared situational awareness** — every robot needs a partial picture of the world.
- **Fault tolerance** — if one robot fails, the team must still complete the mission.

Each of these functions maps directly onto specific hardware/software components. A student of cooperative robotics must therefore understand *which component does what* before designing coordination algorithms.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Components – The Physical Body

### 2.1.1 Locomotion / Mobile Base
The mobile base is responsible for moving the robot through its environment. Common locomotion types include:

- **Differential Drive:** Two independently driven wheels + a caster. Simple, low-cost, widely used in research.
- **Ackermann Steering:** Car-like steering. Used in outdoor/cooperative vehicles.
- **Omnidirectional Drive:** Mecanum or Omni wheels allowing motion in any direction without reorientation.
- **Legged / Aerial / Underwater:** Used in heterogeneous cooperative teams.

### 2.1.2 Manipulators
For tasks like grasping, pushing, or assembly, robots are equipped with arms. Cooperative manipulators fall into three classes:

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Single-arm, multi-robot** | N robots, each with 1 arm | Warehouse picking |
| **Multi-arm, single-robot** | 1 robot with multiple arms | Humanoid assistants |
| **Multi-arm, multi-robot** | N robots, M arms each | Heavy-payload lifting |

### 2.1.3 Power Supply
- **Lithium-Polymer (LiPo) batteries:** High energy density, **≈150–250 Wh/kg**.
- **Lead-acid batteries:** Cheap, heavy, used in stationary robots.
- **Solar panels / fuel cells:** For long-duration outdoor missions.

---

## 2.2 Sensing Components – The Robot's "Senses"

Sensors are classified into two broad families:

### (a) Proprioceptive Sensors (Sense Self-State)
These measure internal robot variables.

| Sensor | Measures | Typical Range / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| **Encoder** | Wheel rotation | Pulses per revolution (PPR) |
| **IMU** (Inertial Measurement Unit) | Acceleration, angular velocity, orientation | m/s², rad/s, radians |
| **Gyroscope** | Rotational velocity | rad/s |
| **Accelerometer** | Linear acceleration | m/s² |
| **Potentiometer** | Joint angle | volts or radians |
| **Current Sensor** | Motor current | Amperes (A) |

### (b) Exteroceptive Sensors (Sense the Environment)

| Sensor | Principle | Output |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| **Camera (RGB)** | Computer vision | 2-D image (pixels) |
| **Depth Camera (RGB-D)** | Stereo / ToF / Structured light | 3-D point cloud |
| **LiDAR** | Time-of-Flight laser scanning | 2-D / 3-D point cloud |
| **Sonar / Ultrasonic** | Acoustic echo ranging | Distance (m) |
| **GPS** | Satellite triangulation | Latitude, Longitude (±2.5 m) |
| **Compass (Magnetometer)** | Earth's magnetic field | Heading (°) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensing Range Constraint (KTU high-yield):**
> For ultrasonic sensors, the minimum detectable distance is called the **blind zone** because the transducer is still ringing when the echo arrives.

---

## 2.3 Computational / Software Components – The "Brain"

### 2.3.1 Onboard Computer
- **Microcontrollers (Arduino, STM32):** Low-level motor control, real-time loops.
- **Single-Board Computers (Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson):** High-level vision, planning, communication.

### 2.3.2 Robot Operating System (ROS / ROS 2)
ROS is the **de facto** middleware for cooperative robots. It provides:

- **Nodes:** Independent processes (e.g., one node per sensor).
- **Topics:** Asynchronous publish-subscribe communication.
- **Services:** Synchronous request-reply.
- **Actions:** Long-running tasks with feedback.
- **Parameter Server:** Shared configuration.

### 2.3.3 Algorithms
- **Localization** (Particle Filter, EKF, SLAM).
- **Mapping** (Occupancy Grid, Octomap).
- **Path Planning** (A*, D*, RRT, PRM).
- **Task Allocation** (Hungarian Algorithm, Market-Based, Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm — **CBBA**).
- **Formation Control** (Leader-Follower, Virtual Structure, Behavior-Based).

---

## 2.4 Communication Components – The "Nervous System"

Cooperative robots must exchange information. This is achieved through:

| Medium | Protocol | Range | Bandwidth |
|--------|----------|-------|-----------|
| Wi-Fi | IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac | ≈100 m | up to **1 Gbps** |
| Bluetooth | IEEE 802.15.1 | ≈10 m | ≈2 Mbps |
| ZigBee | IEEE 802.15.4 | ≈100 m | 250 kbps |
| LoRa | LPWAN | up to **10 km** | 0.3–50 kbps |
| CAN bus | ISO 11898 | <40 m | 1 Mbps |
| 5G / LTE | Cellular | kilometers | up to **10 Gbps** |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Board Examination**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Differential-drive forward kinematics (linear) | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(\omega_R + \omega_L\right)$ | m/s |
| 2 | Differential-drive angular velocity | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{r}{L}\left(\omega_R - \omega_L\right)$ | rad/s |
| 3 | General robot pose in 2-D | $\mathbf{q} = \left[x,\ y,\ \theta\right]^{T}$ | m, m, rad |
| 4 | Rotation matrix (planar) | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | Unitless |
| 5 | Number of DOF of a robot | $\text{DOF} = 6 \cdot (n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ | **Grübler's formula** |
| 6 | Ultrasonic range (Time-of-Flight) | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | m (c ≈ 343 m/s) |
| 7 | LiDAR range (Time-of-Flight) | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | m (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s) |
| 8 | Odometry update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \, \Delta t$ | m |
| 9 | Odometry update (Euler) | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \, \Delta t$ | m |
| 10 | Odometry update (Euler) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \dot{\theta}_k \, \Delta t$ | rad |
| 11 | Communication delay (round-trip) | $\tau_{RT} = \dfrac{2D}{c_{prop}} + T_{proc}$ | seconds |
| 12 | Throughput requirement | $R \ge N \cdot f_s \cdot B_s$ | bits/s |

> **Notes for Students:**
> - In Grübler's formula, $n$ = number of links, $c_i$ = number of constraints at joint $i$.
> - For a free 3-D rigid body: DOF = 6; for planar: DOF = 3.
> - Battery sizing: Energy (Wh) = Voltage (V) × Capacity (Ah).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Search & Rescue (SAR):** Heterogeneous teams (UAVs + UGVs) explore disaster zones.
- **Warehouse Automation:** Amazon's Kiva robots coordinate to fetch shelves.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Drone swarms monitor crop health and apply fertilizer.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Platooning:** Trucks drive in cooperative convoys to save fuel.
- **Underwater Exploration:** AUV teams map the ocean floor.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal:**
> The following derivations are the most frequently asked concepts in KTU 2024 Scheme exams. Every algebraic step is written out explicitly with explanatory text. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics

### Statement
A differential-drive mobile robot has two wheels — left (L) and right (R) — each with radius $r$, separated by axle length $L$. Each wheel rotates at angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ respectively. Derive the linear and angular velocity of the robot's center.

### Step 1 — Contact point velocity of each wheel
The contact point of the left wheel with the ground has linear speed:

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

Similarly, for the right wheel:

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

### Step 2 — Velocity of the robot's center (linear)
The center of the robot moves at the average of the two wheel speeds, because the robot's chassis is rigid:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
v = \frac{r \cdot \omega_L + r \cdot \omega_R}{2} = \frac{r}{2}\left(\omega_L + \omega_R\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Angular velocity of the robot
When both wheels rotate at the same speed ($\omega_L = \omega_R$), the robot moves in a straight line, so $\dot{\theta} = 0$. When they differ, the robot rotates about a point called the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**. The angular velocity is derived from the difference in wheel speeds divided by the axle length $L$:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine into matrix form (Euler integration)
Let the robot pose be $\mathbf{q} = \left[x,\ y,\ \theta\right]^{T}$. The forward kinematics can be written as:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix}\dot{x}\\ \dot{y}\\ \dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}v\\ \dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting $v$ and $\dot{\theta}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2}\\[6pt] \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2}\\[6pt] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L}\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}\omega_L\\ \omega_R\end{bmatrix}
$$

This compact form is often called the **Jacobian matrix** of the differential-drive robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If $\omega_L = -\omega_R$, the robot rotates in place with $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{r\omega}{L}$ — useful for tight maneuvers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic / LiDAR Time-of-Fight Range Equation

### Statement
A sensor emits a pulse and measures the time $t$ for the echo to return. Derive the distance to the obstacle.

### Step 1 — Pulse travels to obstacle and back
The total path length traveled by the pulse is:

$$
\text{Path} = c \cdot t
$$

where $c$ is the speed of the wave in the medium.

### Step 2 — Distance is half the total path
Because the pulse goes to the obstacle and comes back, the one-way distance is:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical example
For a sonar (sound in air, $c \approx 343$ m/s) with $t = 10$ ms:

$$
d = \frac{343 \times 0.010}{2} = 1.715 \text{ m}
$$

For a LiDAR (light in air, $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s) with $t = 33.3$ ns:

$$
d = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8})(33.3 \times 10^{-9})}{2} = 4.995 \text{ m} \approx 5 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always state the medium and value of $c$ explicitly in your answer. Examiners award 1 mark for the *correct constant* even before calculation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Grübler's Formula for Degrees of Freedom

### Statement
For a planar (2-D) mechanism, derive the formula for the number of degrees of freedom.

### Step 1 — Free body in the plane
In 2-D, an unconstrained rigid body has **3 DOF** (two translations + one rotation).

### Step 2 — Each joint imposes constraints
Each joint type removes some degrees of freedom:

| Joint Type | DOF Allowed | Constraints |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| Revolute (R) | 1 | 2 |
| Prismatic (P) | 1 | 2 |
| Fixed / Welded | 0 | 3 |

### Step 3 — Sum over the mechanism
For $n$ links (including the ground link) and joint $i$ imposing $c_i$ constraints:

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i
$$

For 3-D mechanisms, the constant becomes 6:

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example
A 3-DOF planar manipulator has 4 links and 3 revolute joints (each imposing 2 constraints):

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(4 - 1) - (2 + 2 + 2) = 9 - 6 = 3
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Components Across Cooperative Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Analysis Table (Board-Favorite Type of Question):**

| Component Aspect | Centralized MRS | Decentralized MRS | Hybrid MRS |
|------------------|-----------------|-------------------|------------|
| **Central Computer** | Yes (single master) | No | Yes (with leaders) |
| **Failure Behavior** | Single point of failure | Robust | Moderately robust |
| **Communication Load** | High (all → master) | Low (peer-to-peer) | Medium |
| **Computation** | Heavy on master | Distributed | Distributed |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Example Algorithm** | Central Hungarian solver | Consensus-based | Leader-Follower |
| **Latency Sensitivity** | High | Low | Medium |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Battery / Energy Budget for a Cooperative Team

### Statement
A team of $N$ homogeneous robots must operate for time $T$ hours. Each robot consumes power $P$ watts. Derive the minimum battery capacity.

### Step 1 — Total energy
Energy is power × time. Per robot:

$$
E_{robot} = P \cdot T \quad \text{[Wh]}
$$

### Step 2 — Total team energy (parallel deployment)

$$
E_{team} = N \cdot P \cdot T
$$

### Step 3 — Battery sizing with safety factor $k$ (typically $k = 1.2$ to 1.5)

$$
E_{battery} \ge k \cdot N \cdot P \cdot T
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example
For $N = 5$, $P = 50$ W, $T = 2$ h, $k = 1.2$:

$$
E_{battery} = 1.2 \times 5 \times 50 \times 2 = 600 \text{ Wh}
$$

At 12 V, this corresponds to capacity:

$$
C = \frac{E}{V} = \frac{600}{12} = 50 \text{ Ah}
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Communication Delay in a Multi-Robot Network

### Statement
A message of size $S$ bits travels between two robots separated by distance $D$ meters through a wireless link of bandwidth $B$ bits/s and propagation speed $c_{prop} \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. The processing delay at each node is $T_{proc}$. Derive the one-way latency.

### Step 1 — Transmission delay (serialization)

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{S}{B}
$$

### Step 2 — Propagation delay

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{D}{c_{prop}}
$$

### Step 3 — Processing delay

$$
T_{proc} = T_{proc}^{(tx)} + T_{proc}^{(rx)}
$$

### Step 4 — Total one-way latency

$$
\tau = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} = \frac{S}{B} + \frac{D}{c_{prop}} + T_{proc}
$$

### Step 5 — Round-trip time

$$
\tau_{RT} = 2\tau = \frac{2S}{B} + \frac{2D}{c_{prop}} + 2T_{proc}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world application:** Cooperative driving at 100 km/h leaves only ≈10 ms to react. With $S = 1$ kbit and $B = 1$ Mbps, transmission alone takes 1 ms. Designers must therefore minimize both $S$ and $D$.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note for KTU 2024:**
> Examiners reward well-labeled block diagrams. Below are the Mermaid block-level architectures most commonly requested in Cooperative Robotics questions on "Components."

---

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Single Cooperative Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[POWER SUBSYSTEM Battery or Fuel Cell]
    PROC[ONBOARD COMPUTER SBC or Microcontroller]
    SEN[SENSING SUBSYSTEM LiDAR Camera IMU Encoders]
    ACT[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM Motors Wheels Arms]
    COM[COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM WiFi ZigBee CAN]
    SW[SOFTWARE MIDDLEWARE ROS ROS2 Nodes]
    ALG[ALGORITHMS SLAM Planning Task Allocation]

    PWR --> PROC
    PWR --> SEN
    PWR --> ACT
    PWR --> COM
    SEN --> PROC
    PROC --> ACT
    PROC --> SW
    SW --> ALG
    COM <--> PROC
    PROC <--> SW

    classDef power fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c,color:#000
    classDef act   fill:#e2fdd5,stroke:#3a3,color:#000
    classDef soft  fill:#fdf5e2,stroke:#c93,color:#000
    class PWR power
    class SEN sense
    class ACT act
    class PROC,COM,SW,ALG soft
```

---

## 4.2 Multi-Robot Cooperative System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[ROBOT 1 Hardware Sensors Actuators]
    R2[ROBOT 2 Hardware Sensors Actuators]
    R3[ROBOT 3 Hardware Sensors Actuators]
    N1[ROBOT N Hardware Sensors Actuators]

    BS[BASE STATION Optional Ground Control]

    NET((WIRELESS NETWORK WiFi or 5G))

    CLOUD[(CLOUD SERVER Optional for Heavy Computation)]

    R1 <--> NET
    R2 <--> NET
    R3 <--> NET
    N1 <--> NET
    BS  <--> NET
    NET <--> CLOUD

    classDef robot fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c
    classDef net   fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33
    classDef ext   fill:#e2fdd5,stroke:#3a3
    class R1,R2,R3,N1 robot
    class NET net
    class BS,CLOUD ext
```

---

## 4.3 Communication Flow in Cooperative Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OBS[OBSERVATION Each Robot Senses Locally]
    SHARE[INFORMATION SHARING Publish to Common Topic]
    FUSE[DATA FUSION Combine Partial Observations]
    PLAN[DISTRIBUTED PLANNING Allocate Tasks and Paths]
    ACT[COORDINATED ACTION Execute Synchronously]
    FEED[FEEDBACK Measure Progress and Replan]

    OBS --> SHARE
    SHARE --> FUSE
    FUSE --> PLAN
    PLAN --> ACT
    ACT --> FEED
    FEED --> OBS

    classDef step fill:#fdf5e2,stroke:#c93
    class OBS,SHARE,FUSE,PLAN,ACT,FEED step
```

---

## 4.4 Sensor Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[SENSORS]

    P[PROPRIOCEPTIVE Sense Self State]
    E[EXTEROCEPTIVE Sense Environment]

    P --> P1[Encoder]
    P --> P2[IMU]
    P --> P3[Gyroscope]
    P --> P4[Accelerometer]
    P --> P5[Current Sensor]

    E --> E1[Camera]
    E --> E2[LiDAR]
    E --> E3[Ultrasonic]
    E --> E4[GPS]
    E --> E5[Compass]

    classDef root fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33
    classDef sub  fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c
    classDef leaf fill:#e2fdd5,stroke:#3a3
    class S root
    class P,E sub
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,E1,E2,E3,E4,E5 leaf
```

---

## 4.5 Task-Allocation Flow inside a Cooperative Team

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[MISSION OBJECTIVE e.g. Cover Area X]
    D[MISSION DECOMPOSITION Subtasks Generated]
    A[TASK ALLOCATION Algorithm CBBA or Hungarian]
    R1[ROBOT 1 Assigned Subtask S1]
    R2[ROBOT 2 Assigned Subtask S2]
    R3[ROBOT 3 Assigned Subtask S3]
    EX[EXECUTION Robots Perform Tasks]
    CK[CHECK Completion Status]
    RP[REPLAN If Failure or Delay]

    M --> D --> A
    A --> R1
    A --> R2
    A --> R3
    R1 --> EX
    R2 --> EX
    R3 --> EX
    EX --> CK
    CK --> RP
    RP --> A

    classDef mission fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33
    classDef algo   fill:#fdf5e2,stroke:#c93
    classDef robot  fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c
    class M,D,CK,RP mission
    class A,EX algo
    class R1,R2,R3 robot
```

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU Board Drawings:** When the question is worth ≥ 7 marks, examiners expect at least **one** block diagram. Always use **rectangular boxes** with **arrows** clearly labeled (data, control, feedback, or power).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 3 marks — direct, short answer.
> - **Part B:** 14 marks — typically sub-part (a) 7 marks + sub-part (b) 7 marks, with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six components of a cooperative robotic system and classify them under hardware, sensing, computational, or communication categories.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **DC Motors / Wheels** — Hardware (Actuation)
2. **LiDAR** — Sensing (Exteroceptive)
3. **IMU** — Sensing (Proprioceptive)
4. **Raspberry Pi / Jetson** — Computational
5. **ROS / ROS 2 Middleware** — Computational (Software)
6. **Wi-Fi Module** — Communication

(Any six correctly classified = full marks. Examiners penalize wrong category mapping.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Sense internal robot state | Sense external environment |
| **Example 1** | Wheel encoder | Camera |
| **Example 2** | IMU (gyro + accelerometer) | GPS receiver |
| **Use Case** | Odometry, joint control | Mapping, obstacle detection |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a cooperative robotic system. Describe the role of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, axle length $L = 0.3$ m, and wheel angular velocities $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s. Compute the linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$, and the new pose after $t = 1$ s, assuming initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 (or similar). Explain each block:

- **Power Subsystem:** Supplies regulated voltage to motors, sensors, and computers. Usually LiPo battery (≈ 11.1 V, 3 cells).
- **Sensing Subsystem:** Provides feedback on internal state (encoders, IMU) and external environment (LiDAR, camera).
- **Onboard Computer:** Runs ROS, performs SLAM, planning, and decision-making.
- **Actuation Subsystem:** DC motors, motor drivers (H-bridge), wheels, and possibly a manipulator arm.
- **Communication Subsystem:** Wi-Fi / Bluetooth / ZigBee modules for peer-to-peer data sharing.
- **Software Middleware:** ROS nodes for modular, distributed software architecture.

(Marks: 2 for diagram + 5 for explanation ≈ 7 marks)

**(b) Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear velocity**

$$
v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R) = \frac{0.05}{2}(4 + 6) = \frac{0.05 \times 10}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity**

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{0.3}(6 - 4) = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.3} = \frac{0.1}{0.3} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Pose update (Euler integration)**

Since $\theta_0 = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$, $\sin\theta = 0$ throughout:

$$
x_1 = x_0 + v \cdot \cos\theta_0 \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.25 \times 1 \times 1 = 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_1 = y_0 + v \cdot \sin\theta_0 \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.25 \times 0 \times 1 = 0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \dot{\theta} \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.333 \times 1 = 0.333 \text{ rad} \approx 19.1^{\circ}
$$

**Final Pose:** $\mathbf{q}_1 = (0.25 \text{ m},\ 0 \text{ m},\ 0.333 \text{ rad})$.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare centralized, decentralized, and hybrid architectures of cooperative robotic systems. Mention one algorithm suitable for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ultrasonic sensor mounted on a cooperative robot measures an echo time of $t = 6$ ms. If the speed of sound in air is $c = 343$ m/s, determine the distance to the obstacle. Also, compute the distance if a LiDAR (using light, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s) measures the same time.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Comparison Table (Marks: 5 for table, 2 for algorithm examples):

| Feature | Centralized | Decentralized | Hybrid |
|---------|-------------|---------------|--------|
| Control | Single master | Distributed | Selected leaders |
| Failure | Vulnerable | Robust | Moderate |
| Computation | Central heavy | Distributed | Mixed |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Example Algorithm | Central Hungarian | Consensus-Based Bundle (CBBA) | Leader-Follower Formation |

**(b) Step-by-step:**

**For ultrasonic sensor:**

$$
d_{US} = \frac{c_{sound} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}
$$

**For LiDAR (light):**

$$
d_{LiDAR} = \frac{c_{light} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \times (6 \times 10^{-3})}{2} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 9 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} = 900 \text{ km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mistakes students make on this topic (loses 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in Time-of-Flight equations — the wave travels to and from the obstacle.
> 2. **Wrong units of $\omega$** — angular velocity in rad/s, not RPM. If given in RPM, multiply by $2\pi/60$ first.
> 3. **Mixing up DOF and constraints** in Grübler's formula — remember: 6 (or 3) minus the constraints.
> 4. **Not stating "centralized" vs "decentralized"** explicitly when describing cooperative systems — examiners are strict about this terminology.
> 5. **Forgetting initial conditions** in pose integration — Euler method needs $\theta_0$ for $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.
> 6. **Confusing sensor types** — calling a GPS an "exteroceptive sensor" is fine; calling it "proprioceptive" loses a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary (Print and Keep):**

- [x] A cooperative robotic system has **4 component categories**: Hardware, Sensing, Computational, Communication.
- [x] **Proprioceptive** sensors measure self-state (encoder, IMU, gyroscope, accelerometer).
- [x] **Exteroceptive** sensors measure environment (camera, LiDAR, GPS, sonar).
- [x] **Differential-drive kinematics:** $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$, $\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- [x] **Time-of-Flight range equation:** $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$. Use $c = 343$ m/s for sound, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s for light.
- [x] **Grübler's formula (planar):** $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - \sum c_i$.
- [x] **Grübler's formula (spatial):** $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - \sum c_i$.
- [x] **ROS** uses **publish-subscribe** (topics) and **request-reply** (services) patterns.
- [x] **Centralized** architectures have a single point of failure; **decentralized** are robust.
- [x] **Communication delay** = transmission + propagation + processing delays.
- [x] **Energy budget:** $E = N \cdot P \cdot T$ (Watts × hours), with safety factor $k \approx 1.2$.
- [x] **Odometry update (Euler):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v \cos\theta_k \Delta t$, similarly for $y$ and $\theta$.
- [x] **LiDAR** uses light (high accuracy, longer range); **ultrasonic** uses sound (cheap, short range, blind zone).
- [x] **CAN bus** is preferred for in-vehicle real-time communication; **Wi-Fi** for high-bandwidth peer-to-peer.
- [x] **Task allocation algorithms:** Hungarian (centralized), CBBA (decentralized market-based), Auction-Based.

topics.title: Odometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Odometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Odometry** is the branch of mobile robotics concerned with the estimation of a robot's incremental change in **pose** (position and orientation) over time, by using data from **proprioceptive sensors** — most commonly rotary **wheel encoders** — to integrate (sum) the measured wheel rotations into a global 2D (or 3D) trajectory.

In co-operative robotics, odometry serves as the **local pose estimation backbone** for every individual agent. Because communication delays and vision occlusions can momentarily prevent absolute localization (e.g., via SLAM, GPS, or inter-robot bearing measurements), each robot must be able to track *where it is right now* using only its own actuators and encoders.

The estimated pose at time $t$ is conventionally written as a 3-vector:
$$p(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ \theta(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the planar position of the robot's reference point in the world frame, and $\theta$ is the **heading angle** measured counter-clockwise from the X-axis.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blindfolded Walker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded in a large empty hall and you have no smartphone or GPS. The only thing you have is a **measuring wheel** (like a surveyor's wheel) and a **compass**. You push the wheel forward 5 meters, turn 30° right, push 3 meters, turn left 60°, and so on. By *adding up* all your own movements, you maintain a rough mental map of your location.

That is exactly what odometry does. The **measuring wheel** corresponds to the **wheel encoder**, the **compass** corresponds to the **gyroscope / heading integrator**, and the **mental map** corresponds to the **robot's estimated pose**. Just as the blindfolded walker accumulates error, a robot's odometric estimate **drifts unboundedly** over time.

## 1.3 Why Odometry Matters in Co-operative Robotics

- **Local Dead Reckoning:** Each member of the swarm must localize between absolute corrections.
- **Initial Guess for Estimation Filters:** The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) and Particle Filter used for multi-robot SLAM need an odometric *prediction* step.
- **Motion Control Feedback:** Trajectory tracking controllers (pure pursuit, MPC) consume odometric updates at high rates (typically **50–200 Hz**).
- **Co-ordination Primitives:** Formation control, leader–follower tracking, and rendezvous algorithms all depend on relative odometry broadcast between agents.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, odometry is the *prerequisite* for understanding **dead reckoning**, **forward kinematics of differential-drive robots**, and the **predict step of the EKF**. Master it here, and the rest of the module becomes straightforward.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Odometric Pipeline — Operational Logic

The dead-reckoning loop runs in four conceptual stages:

1. **Sensor Sampling:** Read encoder tick counts $N_L$, $N_R$ for the left and right drive wheels during a small interval $\Delta t$.
2. **Wheel Displacement Conversion:** Convert ticks to linear wheel travel $\Delta d_L$ and $\Delta d_R$ using the known wheel radius $r$ and encoder resolution $C_e$ (ticks per revolution).
3. **Robot Body Motion Synthesis:** Combine the two wheel displacements into the robot's *forward translation* $\Delta s$ and *rotation* $\Delta \theta$.
4. **Pose Integration:** Update the global pose $(x, y, \theta)$ by composing the local motion with the current heading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order?** Steps 1–2 happen in hardware/firmware at very high frequency. Steps 3–4 are the *kinematic* heart of odometry and what examiners love to test.

## 2.2 Differential-Drive Kinematic Model (The Workhorse)

For a robot with two independently driven wheels separated by axle length $L$ (also called the **track width**), the instantaneous body-frame velocities are:

$$v(t) = \frac{v_R(t) + v_L(t)}{2}$$

$$\omega(t) = \frac{v_R(t) - v_L(t)}{L}$$

where:
- $v_L, v_R$ = linear velocities of left and right wheels (m/s)
- $v$ = forward velocity of the robot's center (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity about the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) (rad/s)

## 2.3 The Two Update Schemes

### A. Approximate (Euler-Integration) Scheme — Easy to remember

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \, \Delta t \cos(\theta_k)$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \, \Delta t \sin(\theta_k)$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \, \Delta t$$

### B. Exact (Arc-Geometry) Scheme — Higher fidelity

When using **displacement** per cycle ($\Delta d_L, \Delta d_R$) instead of velocity:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta d_R + \Delta d_L}{2} \qquad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L}{L}$$

**Case 1 — Curved path** ($\Delta \theta \neq 0$):

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta}\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \Delta \theta) - \sin(\theta_k)\bigr]$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta}\bigl[\cos(\theta_k) - \cos(\theta_k + \Delta \theta)\bigr]$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta$$

**Case 2 — Straight-line motion** ($\Delta \theta = 0$, i.e., $\Delta d_R = \Delta d_L$):

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos(\theta_k)$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin(\theta_k)$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **arc-based formulas** assume the robot moves along a circular arc of radius $R = \Delta s / \Delta \theta$ during the sampling interval. When $\Delta \theta \to 0$, the limit of these expressions is precisely the straight-line case above (verified by L'Hôpital's rule). This duality is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Wheel linear displacement | $\Delta d = \dfrac{N}{C_e} \cdot 2\pi r$ | m | $N$ = ticks, $C_e$ = ticks/rev, $r$ = wheel radius |
| 2 | Robot forward translation | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta d_R + \Delta d_L}{2}$ | m | Average of two wheel arcs |
| 3 | Robot rotation | $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L}{L}$ | rad | $L$ = axle length (track width) |
| 4 | Body linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | m/s | Instantaneous form |
| 5 | Body angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | rad/s | Instantaneous form |
| 6 | Radius of curvature | $R = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | m | Center of rotation lies on axle extension |
| 7 | Pose update (straight) | $x' = x + \Delta s\cos\theta,\ \ y' = y + \Delta s\sin\theta$ | m | $\Delta \theta = 0$ |
| 8 | Pose update (curved) | $x' = x + \tfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta}\bigl[\sin(\theta+\Delta\theta)-\sin\theta\bigr]$ | m | $\Delta \theta \neq 0$ |
| 9 | Pose update (curved) | $y' = y + \tfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta}\bigl[\cos\theta - \cos(\theta+\Delta\theta)\bigr]$ | m | $\Delta \theta \neq 0$ |
| 10 | ICR offset (sign) | Sign$(\omega)$ = Sign$(v_R - v_L)$ | — | Positive $v_R$ ⇒ CCW rotation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How Odometry Is Used |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Autonomous AGVs in Warehouses** | Amazon Kiva robots use wheel odometry + floor fiducials for navigation |
| **Autonomous Cars (Short-term)** | Wheel odometry is fused with IMU in the EKF predict step |
| **Planetary Rovers (NASA)** | Mars rovers rely heavily on visual + wheel odometry over long traverses |
| **Robot Soccer (RoboCup)** | Each player localizes using odometry + landmark vision at 60 Hz |
| **Co-operative Swarms** | Inter-robot relative poses are computed by differencing each agent's odometry |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Encoder Ticks to Wheel Displacement

A wheel encoder produces $C_e$ ticks per revolution. One revolution of a wheel of radius $r$ advances the wheel contact point by a circumference of $2\pi r$. Therefore, the linear distance traveled per tick is $2\pi r / C_e$, and for $N$ ticks:

$$\Delta d = N \cdot \frac{2\pi r}{C_e}$$

**Derivation walk-through:**

**Step 1** — Let $\phi$ be the wheel rotation angle (radians). Encoder pulse count is proportional to angle:
$$N = C_e \cdot \frac{\phi}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the angle:
$$\phi = \frac{2\pi N}{C_e}$$

**Step 3** — Arc length of wheel rolling without slipping equals the linear displacement of the wheel center:
$$\Delta d = r \cdot \phi = r \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{C_e} = \frac{2\pi r N}{C_e}$$

**Step 4** — Apply per wheel:
$$\Delta d_L = \frac{2\pi r N_L}{C_e}, \qquad \Delta d_R = \frac{2\pi r N_R}{C_e}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation assumes the **no-slip rolling condition** — the wheel's contact point is instantaneously at rest. In real systems, slippage is the *primary* source of systematic odometric error.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Forward Kinematics of a Differential-Drive Robot

We now derive the relationship between wheel velocities and the body-frame twist $(v, \omega)$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR).**

For a non-holonomic differential-drive robot, the velocity of the contact point of each wheel is purely longitudinal. The ICR lies on the line passing through both wheel contact points (the axle). Let $R$ be the signed distance from the robot's center to the ICR along the axle.

**Step 2 — Express each wheel's linear velocity using the rigid-body rotation about the ICR.**

For pure rolling, every point on the robot moves with speed $\omega R$ tangential to a circle of radius equal to its distance from the ICR. The right wheel is at distance $R + L/2$ from the ICR; the left wheel is at distance $R - L/2$ (using a sign convention where $R$ is positive on the right):

$$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system for $v$ and $\omega$ in terms of $v_L, v_R$.**

Adding the two equations:
$$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega R = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

But $\omega R$ is precisely the body forward velocity $v$ (the center's tangential speed about the ICR):
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \quad\blacksquare$$

Subtracting the equations:
$$v_R - v_L = \omega L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} \quad\blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two boxed equations are the **single most important result of Module 1**. KTU examiners will either ask for them directly (Part A) or hide them inside a 14-mark derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Exact Pose Update (Arc Geometry)

Assume in a small time interval the robot moves along a circular arc of radius $R = \Delta s / \Delta \theta$.

**Step 1 — Parameterize the arc.**

The center of the arc (the ICR) lies on the line perpendicular to the wheel axle, at the point:
$$C = \begin{bmatrix} x_k - R\sin\theta_k \\ y_k + R\cos\theta_k \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Apply a 2D rotation of angle $\Delta \theta$ about $C$ to the point $(x_k, y_k)$.**

Using the standard rotation matrix $R_{2D}(\Delta \theta)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{k+1} - x_C \\ y_{k+1} - y_C \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\Delta\theta & -\sin\Delta\theta \\ \sin\Delta\theta & \cos\Delta\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_k - x_C \\ y_k - y_C \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and expand.**

Because $x_k - x_C = R\sin\theta_k$ and $y_k - y_C = -R\cos\theta_k$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_C + R\sin\theta_k \cos\Delta\theta + R\cos\theta_k \sin\Delta\theta$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_C - R\sin\theta_k \sin\Delta\theta + R\cos\theta_k \cos\Delta\theta$$

**Step 4 — Insert the expressions for $x_C$ and $y_C$.**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - R\sin\theta_k + R\bigl[\sin\theta_k \cos\Delta\theta + \cos\theta_k \sin\Delta\theta\bigr]$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + R\cos\theta_k + R\bigl[-\sin\theta_k \sin\Delta\theta + \cos\theta_k \cos\Delta\theta\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the sine and cosine addition identities $\sin(\alpha+\beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta$ and $\cos(\alpha+\beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta$:**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + R\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta_k\bigr]$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + R\bigl[\cos\theta_k - \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta)\bigr]$$

**Step 6 — Replace $R$ with $\Delta s / \Delta \theta$:**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta}\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta_k\bigr] \quad\blacksquare$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \theta}\bigl[\cos\theta_k - \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta)\bigr] \quad\blacksquare$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta \quad\blacksquare$$

**Step 7 — Verify the straight-line limit.**

Apply L'Hôpital's rule as $\Delta\theta \to 0$ to the factor $\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta$ in the numerator and $\Delta\theta$ in the denominator. The derivative of the numerator w.r.t. $\Delta\theta$ is $\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta)$, and the derivative of the denominator is $1$:

$$\lim_{\Delta\theta \to 0} \frac{\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta}{\Delta\theta} = \cos\theta$$

Therefore $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s\cos\theta_k$, recovering the straight-line formula. The same applies to $y$.

## 3.4 Analytical Problem Walkthrough — Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-style numerical problem:** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05\ \text{m}$, axle length $L = 0.30\ \text{m}$, and encoder resolution $C_e = 500\ \text{ticks/rev}$. Starting from $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$, the encoders report $N_L = 250$ and $N_R = 750$ ticks in one sampling interval. Compute the new pose using the exact arc method.

**Step 1 — Convert ticks to wheel displacements.**

Using the formula from Section 3.1:
$$\Delta d_L = \frac{2\pi r N_L}{C_e} = \frac{2\pi (0.05)(250)}{500} = \frac{25\pi}{500} = 0.05\pi \approx 0.1571\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta d_R = \frac{2\pi r N_R}{C_e} = \frac{2\pi (0.05)(750)}{500} = \frac{75\pi}{500} = 0.15\pi \approx 0.4712\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute robot translation and rotation.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta d_R + \Delta d_L}{2} = \frac{0.4712 + 0.1571}{2} = 0.3142\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L}{L} = \frac{0.4712 - 0.1571}{0.30} = \frac{0.3142}{0.30} \approx 1.0472\ \text{rad} \;(60°)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exact arc update with $\theta_0 = 0$.**

$$x_1 = 0 + \frac{0.3142}{1.0472}\bigl[\sin(1.0472) - \sin(0)\bigr] = 0.3000 \times 0.8660 \approx 0.2598\ \text{m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 + \frac{0.3142}{1.0472}\bigl[\cos(0) - \cos(1.0472)\bigr] = 0.3000 \times 0.5000 = 0.1500\ \text{m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 1.0472 \approx 1.0472\ \text{rad}\ (60°)$$

**Step 4 — Verification using geometry.** The arc radius is $R = \Delta s / \Delta\theta = 0.30$ m. The center of the arc lies at $(0, 0.30)$. Rotating the start point $(0,0)$ about $(0, 0.30)$ by $60°$ should give $(0.2598, 0.1500)$ — confirmed. ✔

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Odometry Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Encoder L] --> B[Tick Counter]
    C[Wheel Encoder R] --> D[Tick Counter]

    D --> E
    E --> F[Forward Kinematics Block]
    F --> G[Compute Delta s and Delta Theta]
    G --> H{Is Delta Theta approx 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Straight Line Update]
    H -- No --> J[Arc Geometry Update]
    I --> K[New Pose x, y, theta]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Pose Memory Buffer]
    L --> M[EKF Predict Step]
    L --> N[Controller Feedback]
    L --> O[Inter Robot Broadcast]
    M --> P[Multi Robot SLAM]
    N --> Q[Motion Control Law]
    O --> R[Swarm Co ordination Layer]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Differential-Drive ICR Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOT[Differential Drive Robot]
        WL[Left Wheel Contact] --- AXLE[Axle Length L]
        AXLE --- WR[Right Wheel Contact]
        CTR[Robot Center C] --- AXLE
    end
    VECL[Velocity vL leftward along axle] -.-> WL
    VECR[Velocity vR rightward along axle] -.-> WR
    ICR((Instantaneous Center of Rotation ICR))
    ICR --- RADL[Radius R minus L over 2] --- WL

    style ICR fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Error-Source Taxonomy of Odometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Odometric Drift Sources] --> SYS[Systematic Errors]
    ERR --> NONSYS[Non Systematic Errors]
    SYS --> S1[Wheel radius mismatch]
    SYS --> S2[Wheel base L miscalibration]
    SYS --> S3[Encoder quantization]
    SYS --> S4[Unequal wheel contact]
    NONSYS --> N1[Wheel slip on slick floor]
    NONSYS --> N2[Collision with obstacle]
    NONSYS --> N3[Uneven terrain bumps]
    NONSYS --> N4[Carrying payload shift]
    S1 --> MIT[Correct via online calibration]
    S2 --> MIT
    S3 --> FILT[Kalman Filtering with IMU]
    N1 --> FILT
    N2 --> FILT
    style ERR fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style MIT fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style FILT fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Pose Update State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sampling : Timer Tick
    Sampling --> Compute : Encoders Read
    Compute --> CurvedArc : Delta Theta nonzero
    Compute --> StraightLine : Delta Theta zero
    CurvedArc --> PublishPose
    StraightLine --> PublishPose
    PublishPose --> Broadcast : To Swarm
    PublishPose --> Feedback : To Controller
    PublishPose --> EKFPredict : To Filter
    Broadcast --> Idle
    Feedback --> Idle
    EKFPredict --> Idle
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define odometry. Why is it classified as a dead-reckoning method? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Odometry is the process of estimating a mobile robot's change in position and orientation over time by integrating the rotation measurements of its wheel encoders. It is classified as a *dead-reckoning* method because the robot *deduces* its current pose solely from the *cumulative* internal measurement of its own motion, without any external reference such as landmarks, GPS, or a known map. The pose is computed by adding up incremental movements from a known starting configuration, hence the name "deduced reckoning." The limitation of this approach is the **unbounded accumulation of small errors** — both systematic (wheel diameter mismatch, axle length error) and non-systematic (slip, uneven floor) — leading to drift in the pose estimate over long trajectories. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? Why is it important in differential-drive odometry? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) is the unique point in the plane about which a rigid body undergoing planar motion is instantaneously rotating. For a differential-drive robot at any given instant, the ICR lies on the line passing through the two wheel contact points, and the entire robot executes a circular arc about it. Its importance in differential-drive odometry is twofold:

1. **Kinematic Synthesis:** The forward-velocity and angular-velocity equations $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$ are derived directly from the ICR geometry.
2. **Path Modeling:** The exact pose-update equations for arc motion during a sampling interval $\Delta t$ are constructed by rotating about the ICR by angle $\Delta\theta$.

Without invoking the ICR, the elegant closed-form pose update cannot be derived. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics and Exact Pose Update `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the forward-kinematic relations $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$ for a differential-drive mobile robot. Clearly state the assumptions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** The robot moves on a flat, horizontal 2D plane.

**Assumption 2:** Both wheels roll without slipping — the instantaneous velocity of each wheel's contact point with the ground is zero.

**Assumption 3:** The wheel axles are parallel and the robot's center lies on the perpendicular bisector of the axle.

**Step 1 — Define the ICR.** Under non-holonomic constraint (no lateral motion), the robot at any instant is rotating about a point on the axle line. Let $R$ be the signed distance from the robot's center $C$ to the ICR, positive towards the right wheel.

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body rotation about the ICR.** Let the angular velocity of the robot be $\omega$ (positive CCW). The right wheel contact point is at distance $R + L/2$ from the ICR, and the left wheel contact point is at distance $R - L/2$. Their linear speeds are therefore:

$$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right) \qquad v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Add the two equations.**
$$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R$$
But the speed of the robot's center $C$ about the ICR is $\omega R$, which is exactly the body forward velocity $v$. Hence:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two equations.**
$$v_R - v_L = \omega L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Marks Distribution:** Assumptions (1), ICR setup (1), wheel velocity equations (2), final boxed results (2), neatness (1). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a differential-drive robot starting at $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 30°)$, the right and left wheels report displacements of $\Delta d_R = 0.40$ m and $\Delta d_L = 0.20$ m over one sampling interval. The axle length is $L = 0.30$ m. Compute the new pose $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1)$ using the exact arc-based update equations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute translation and rotation increments.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta d_R + \Delta d_L}{2} = \frac{0.40 + 0.20}{2} = 0.30\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L}{L} = \frac{0.40 - 0.20}{0.30} = \frac{0.20}{0.30} = 0.6667\ \text{rad} \approx 38.197°$$

**Step 2 — Identify the applicable formula.** Since $\Delta\theta \neq 0$, use the curved-arc update.

**Step 3 — Compute the new heading.**
$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta = 30° + 38.197° = 68.197° = 1.190\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $x_1$.**

$$x_1 = x_0 + \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta\theta}\bigl[\sin(\theta_0 + \Delta\theta) - \sin(\theta_0)\bigr]$$

$$x_1 = 0 + \frac{0.30}{0.6667}\bigl[\sin(68.197°) - \sin(30°)\bigr]$$

$$x_1 = 0.45 \times [0.9285 - 0.5000] = 0.45 \times 0.4285 = 0.1928\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $y_1$.**

$$y_1 = y_0 + \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta\theta}\bigl[\cos(\theta_0) - \cos(\theta_0 + \Delta\theta)\bigr]$$

$$y_1 = 0 + 0.45 \times [\cos(30°) - \cos(68.197°)] = 0.45 \times [0.8660 - 0.3714]$$

$$y_1 = 0.45 \times 0.4946 = 0.2226\ \text{m}$$

**Final Pose:** $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) = (0.1928\ \text{m},\ 0.2226\ \text{m},\ 1.190\ \text{rad})$. **(7 marks)**

**Marks Distribution:** Increment computation (2), formula selection (1), new heading (1), $x_1$ calculation (1.5), $y_1$ calculation (1.5).

---

### Question B (Internal Choice): Encoder-Based Odometry and Error Sources `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m, encoder resolution $C_e = 1000$ ticks/rev, and axle length $L = 0.25$ m. The encoders report $N_L = 1200$ and $N_R = 1800$ ticks during a sampling period. Starting at $(0, 0, 0)$, determine (i) the wheel displacements, (ii) the robot translation and rotation, and (iii) the new pose using the arc method.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Wheel Displacements.**

$$\Delta d_L = \frac{2\pi r N_L}{C_e} = \frac{2\pi(0.04)(1200)}{1000} = \frac{96\pi}{1000} = 0.3016\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta d_R = \frac{2\pi r N_R}{C_e} = \frac{2\pi(0.04)(1800)}{1000} = \frac{144\pi}{1000} = 0.4524\ \text{m}$$

**(ii) Translation and Rotation.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{0.4524 + 0.3016}{2} = 0.3770\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{0.4524 - 0.3016}{0.25} = \frac{0.1508}{0.25} = 0.6032\ \text{rad}\ (\approx 34.56°)$$

**(iii) New Pose (with $\theta_0 = 0$).**

$$x_1 = 0 + \frac{0.3770}{0.6032}\bigl[\sin(0.6032) - 0\bigr] = 0.625 \times 0.5664 = 0.3540\ \text{m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 + \frac{0.3770}{0.6032}\bigl[1 - \cos(0.6032)\bigr] = 0.625 \times [1 - 0.8241] = 0.625 \times 0.1759 = 0.1099\ \text{m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.6032\ \text{rad} = 0.6032\ \text{rad}$$

**Final Pose:** $(0.3540\ \text{m},\ 0.1099\ \text{m},\ 0.6032\ \text{rad})$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Classify the major sources of error in wheel odometry. Explain how the EKF (Extended Kalman Filter) mitigates these errors in a co-operative robotics context.**

**Model Solution:**

**Error Classification:**

**A. Systematic Errors (deterministic, repeatable):**
1. *Wheel radius mismatch* — Left and right wheels may have slightly different effective radii due to manufacturing tolerance or unequal wear.
2. *Axle length miscalibration* — The track width $L$ in software may differ from the actual mechanical value.
3. *Finite encoder resolution* — Quantization error of order $\pm 1$ tick per wheel.
4. *Wheel deformation under load* — Soft wheels flatten under payload, altering the effective radius.

**B. Non-Systematic Errors (stochastic, environment-dependent):**
1. *Wheel slip* on wet, oily, or polished surfaces.
2. *Bumps and uneven terrain* causing momentary loss of contact.
3. *Unexpected collisions* with obstacles, introducing slip.
4. *Skidding* during fast turns on low-friction surfaces.

**EKF Mitigation in Co-operative Robotics:**

The Extended Kalman Filter addresses odometric error in a two-step loop:

1. **Predict Step:** The current pose estimate $\hat{p}_k$ and its covariance $\Sigma_k$ are propagated using the nonlinear odometry model $f(\cdot)$ and the process-noise covariance $Q$:
$$\hat{p}_{k|k-1} = f(\hat{p}_{k-1}, u_k)$$
$$\Sigma_{k|k-1} = F_k \,\Sigma_{k-1}\, F_k^{T} + Q$$
where $F_k$ is the Jacobian of $f$ w.r.t. the state.

2. **Update Step:** When an absolute measurement becomes available (e.g., GPS, landmark bearing, or *inter-robot relative range/bearing* from a swarm neighbour), the residual $y_k = z_k - h(\hat{p}_{k|k-1})$ and its innovation covariance $S_k$ are used to compute the Kalman gain $K_k = \Sigma_{k|k-1} H_k^{T} S_k^{-1}$ and correct the state:
$$\hat{p}_k = \hat{p}_{k|k-1} + K_k \, y_k$$

**Co-operative benefit:** When one robot in the swarm has a low-drift estimate (e.g., it just saw a landmark), it can broadcast its absolute position. Other agents use this *inter-robot measurement* as an additional update in their EKF, bounding the drift of the entire swarm simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

**Marks Distribution:** Error classification (3), EKF predict equation (2), update equation with co-operative note (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the no-slip assumption** when deriving the kinematic equations. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Using Euler integration in a question that explicitly asks for the *arc* method.** (–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing degrees and radians** silently inside trigonometric functions. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the L'Hôpital / limiting-case justification** when deriving the straight-line formula as a special case. (–1 mark)
> 5. **In encoder problems, students often forget to multiply by $2\pi$** or drop the $r$ in the $\Delta d$ formula. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Failing to label the covariance matrices** $Q$ and $R$ in the EKF answer — examiners explicitly demand notation. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pose vector:** $p = (x, y, \theta)^{T}$ in the world frame.
- **Forward kinematics of differential drive:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- **Encoder to distance:** $\Delta d = 2\pi r N / C_e$.
- **Translation per cycle:** $\Delta s = (\Delta d_R + \Delta d_L)/2$.
- **Rotation per cycle:** $\Delta\theta = (\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L)/L$.
- **Arc-based update (curved):** Use the exact formula with $\sin$ and $\cos$ addition; the radius is $R = \Delta s / \Delta\theta$.
- **Straight-line update:** When $\Delta\theta = 0$, use $x' = x + \Delta s\cos\theta$, $y' = y + \Delta s\sin\theta$.
- **Heading update:** Always $\theta' = \theta + \Delta\theta$, regardless of straight or curved case.
- **Limit case check:** L'Hôpital's rule must connect the curved and straight formulas for full credit.
- **Odometry is dead-reckoning:** No external reference is used; hence error grows without bound.
- **Systematic errors** (deterministic): wheel radius mismatch, axle miscalibration, encoder quantization, wheel deformation.
- **Non-systematic errors** (stochastic): slip, bumps, collision-induced skid.
- **EKF is the standard fusion tool:** Predict with the nonlinear odometry model $f(\cdot)$, update with absolute or inter-robot measurements.
- **Co-operative edge:** Robots in a swarm share absolute fixes, so each agent's drift is bounded *globally*, not just locally.
- **Units:** $x, y, \Delta d$ in **metres**; $\theta, \Delta\theta$ in **radians** (use $\pi/180$ for conversion).
- **Sign convention:** Positive $\omega$ = counter-clockwise rotation; positive $v_R - v_L$ ⇒ right wheel faster ⇒ CCW turn ⇒ $\Delta\theta > 0$.
- **Standard metric:** Encoder resolutions in KTU problems are usually between **100 and 2048 ticks/rev**; typical wheel radii are **3–10 cm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — KINEMATICS IN CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔷 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Co-operative Robot Kinematics** is the branch of multi-body robotics that studies the **geometric relationship between actuator coordinates (joint variables / wheel velocities) and the resulting spatial pose (position + orientation) of a team of inter-connected robots** operating under shared tasks, shared reference frames, and inter-robot geometric constraints (formation shape, relative distance, bearing, or synchronization).

For a single robot, kinematics establishes the **mapping** between:

$$
q \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{FK}} \quad X \in SE(2) \text{ or } SE(3)
$$

For a **team** of $m$ cooperative robots, the cooperative kinematic problem extends this to a **coupled, constrained system**:

$$
Q = [q_1^T, q_2^T, \ldots, q_m^T]^T \quad \xrightarrow{\text{Cooperative FK}} \quad \mathcal{X} = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_m\}
$$

subject to a set of $k$ **cooperative constraints** $\Phi(Q) = 0$.

### 🔷 The Three Layers of Kinematics in Co-operative Robotics

| Layer | Classical Robotics | Co-operative Extension |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Agent Kinematics** | FK / IK of a single manipulator or mobile base | FK / IK of each individual robot in the team |
| **Layer 2 — Relative Kinematics** | Not formally defined | Geometric relationship $X_{i/j} = X_i \ominus X_j$ between any two team members |
| **Layer 3 — Formation Kinematics** | Not defined | Collective motion of the swarm governed by a **formation graph** $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ and a **constraint Jacobian** |

### 🔷 Intuitive Analogy: The Synchronised Dance Troupe 🎭

> [!TIP]
> **Think of cooperative robot kinematics like a synchronised dance troupe.**
>
> 1. **Each dancer (individual robot)** has a personal *body-joint map* — the angle of the elbow determines the position of the hand. That is **agent kinematics**.
> 2. **The hand-to-hand distance** the lead dancer must maintain with the second dancer is a *relative kinematic constraint*.
> 3. **The shape of the entire troupe** (line, triangle, circle) — which dancer is linked to which — is the **formation kinematic structure**.
>
> When the music tempo (control input) changes, every dancer must update *simultaneously* to preserve both their own balance and the shape of the troupe. This simultaneous, constraint-preserving motion is precisely what cooperative kinematics models.

### 🔷 Key Standard Metrics & Symbols (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **D-H Parameters**: $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$ — four geometric descriptors of a serial link.
- **Homogeneous Transformation**: $T \in SE(3)$ — $4 \times 4$ matrix encoding both rotation and translation.
- **Twist (Spatial Velocity)**: $V_b = [v_b^T, \omega_b^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^6$ for manipulators; $\nu = [v_x, v_y, \omega]^T \in \mathbb{R}^3$ for planar mobile robots.
- **Jacobian**: $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ — linear map from joint velocity $\dot{q}$ to end-effector velocity $\dot{X}$.
- **Formation Graph $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$**: $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = m$ robots, $\vert \mathcal{E} \vert = k$ constraints.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: $\rho = nm - k$ for an $m$-robot team with $n$ joints each and $k$ independent constraints.

### 🔷 Types of Robot Platforms in Cooperative Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasises that cooperative teams are **heterogeneous**. The kinematic framework must therefore handle all three platform classes uniformly through the **twist / wrench** formalism:

1. **Fixed-base serial manipulators** (e.g., two KUKA arms on an assembly line).
2. **Wheeled mobile robots** — *Differential drive, Ackermann (car-like), Omnidirectional, Unicycle*.
3. **Aerial / Marine platforms** modelled as 6-DOF rigid bodies (quadrotor, AUV).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forward Kinematic Map — A Single Agent

For a serial manipulator with $n$ revolute joints, the forward kinematics (FK) is the composite product of $n$ homogeneous transforms:

$$
T_n^0(q) = A_1(q_1)\,A_2(q_2)\,\cdots\,A_n(q_n) \in SE(3)
$$

where each **Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) link transform** is:

$$
A_i(q_i) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Why the D-H Parameterisation?

- It uses only **4 parameters** per link (instead of 6 in a full $SE(3)$ description).
- It systematically places frame $i$ on the **common normal** between joint axes $i$ and $i+1$.
- It makes the **Jacobian derivation analytically tractable**.

## 2.2 The Geometric (Inverse) Kinematic Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is the inverse mapping: given a desired end-effector pose $X_d$, find joint coordinates $q_d$ such that $FK(q_d) = X_d$.

For a **2R planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1, l_2$ and end-effector position $(x, y)$:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2} =: C_\theta
$$

This gives **two solutions** (elbow-up / elbow-down) — the classical *kinematic redundancy* phenomenon.

## 2.3 Mobile Robot Kinematics

### Unicycle / Differential Drive

A planar unicycle has configuration $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and control inputs $\nu = [v, \omega]^T$:

$$
\dot{q} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This system is **non-holonomic** — it cannot instantaneously move sideways. The constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is *non-integrable*, which makes IK significantly harder than for holonomic manipulators.

### Bicycle / Ackermann Model

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
$$

where $L$ is the wheelbase and $\phi$ is the steering angle.

## 2.4 Relative Kinematics — The Cooperative Heart

For two robots $i$ and $j$ with poses $X_i, X_j \in SE(2)$, the **relative pose** is:

$$
X_{i/j} = X_j^{-1} X_i = X_i \ominus X_j
$$

For a formation, we collect the relative poses over all edges $\mathcal{E}$:

$$
Z = \mathcal{F}(Q), \quad Z \in \mathbb{R}^{3k}, \quad Q \in \mathbb{R}^{nm}
$$

The map $\mathcal{F}$ is the **cooperative forward kinematic map**.

## 2.5 Formation Graph & Constraint Jacobian

The formation is represented by a **directed graph** $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ where:
- $\mathcal{V} = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ — robot indices
- $\mathcal{E} \subset \mathcal{V} \times \mathcal{V}$ — constraint edges

For each edge $(i, j) \in \mathcal{E}$ with desired relative pose $Z_{ij}^*$:

$$
\Phi_{ij}(Q) = \mathcal{F}_{ij}(Q) - Z_{ij}^* = 0
$$

The **cooperative Jacobian** $J_c \in \mathbb{R}^{3k \times nm}$ maps team joint velocities to constraint velocities:

$$
\dot{\Phi} = J_c(Q)\,\dot{Q}
$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every row of this table — these are the formulas a KTU examiner will test.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Dimensions | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DH link transform | $A_i$ (4×4) above | $4 \times 4$ | Per joint |
| 2 | Full FK | $T_n^0 = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | $4 \times 4$ | Sequential product |
| 3 | Robot Jacobian | $J(q) = \partial T / \partial q$ | $6 \times n$ | For manipulators |
| 4 | Mobile Jacobian | $J(q) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\\sin\theta & 0\\0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 2$ | Unicycle |
| 5 | Relative pose | $X_{i/j} = X_i \ominus X_j$ | $SE(2) / SE(3)$ | Cooperative building block |
| 6 | Formation constraint | $\Phi_{ij}(Q) = \mathcal{F}_{ij}(Q) - Z_{ij}^*$ | $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ | Per edge |
| 7 | Cooperative Jacobian | $\dot{\Phi} = J_c \dot{Q}$ | $3k \times nm$ | Team-level |
| 8 | DOF of team | $\rho = nm - \text{rank}(J_c)$ | scalar | Kinematic mobility |
| 9 | 2R elbow angle | $\cos\theta_2 = (x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2)/(2 l_1 l_2)$ | scalar | Two solutions |
| 10 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta = \dot{y}\cos\theta$ | scalar | Unicycle |
| 11 | Ackermann | $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$ | scalar | Car-like |
| 12 | Skew-symmetric cross product | $[a]_\times b = a \times b$ | $3 \times 3$ | Used in Jacobian |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Cooperative Kinematics Matters |
|---|---|
| **Warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva, AGVs) | Hundreds of mobile robots must maintain safe relative distances while moving — relative kinematics + collision cones |
| **Surgical robotics** (Da Vinci dual-arm) | Two manipulator arms cooperate on the same patient; their *relative Jacobian* determines coordinated suturing precision |
| **Space interferometry** (DARPA Orbital Express) | Two spacecraft must maintain micron-level relative pose — formation kinematics with 6-DOF constraints |
| **Search & rescue UAV swarms** | Quadrotors must maintain line/sphere formations in GPS-denied environments using bearing-only constraints |
| **Agricultural robotics** | Tractor-platoon — Ackermann models coordinated via inter-vehicle distance and bearing constraints |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2R Planar Manipulator (Foundation of the Cooperative Framework)

### Setup
Consider a 2R manipulator in the $xy$-plane with:
- Link 1 length $l_1$, joint angle $\theta_1$ from base $x$-axis
- Link 2 length $l_2$, joint angle $\theta_2$ relative to link 1

The D-H parameters for each link are:

| Link | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $l_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_2$ |

### Step 1 — Frame 1 transform $A_1$

$$
A_1 = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & l_1\cos\theta_1 \\
\sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & l_1\sin\theta_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Frame 2 transform $A_2$

$$
A_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & l_2\cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & l_2\sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply $A_1 A_2$

Multiplying the two matrices and extracting the translation column $(x, y, z)$ of the end-effector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
z &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic of step 3:** The position of the end-effector is the vector sum of link 1's endpoint (at angle $\theta_1$, length $l_1$) and link 2's endpoint (at the *cumulative* angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$, length $l_2$). This is the classical **trigonometric form of FK**.

### Step 4 — Compose the full homogeneous transform

$$
T_2^0 =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics of the 2R Arm

### Step 1 — Square and add the position equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 + y^2 &= l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 \\
\Rightarrow \cos\theta_2 &= \frac{x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\theta_2$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\left( \frac{x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2} \right)
$$

The **$\pm$** gives the elbow-up / elbow-down dual solutions.

### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_1$ using the atan2 function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}\bigl(l_2\sin\theta_2,\; l_1 + l_2\cos\theta_2\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic of step 3:** $\text{atan2}(y, x)$ gives the angle from the base to the *wrist point* $(x, y)$. The second $\text{atan2}$ term subtracts the angle subtended by link 2 at the wrist. Using $\text{atan2}$ (not $\arctan$) avoids quadrant ambiguity — a KTU marker note.

## 3.3 Derivation: Geometric Jacobian of the 2R Arm

For a revolute joint $i$, the $i$-th column of the Jacobian is:

$$
J_i =
\begin{bmatrix}
z_{i-1} \times (p_{end} - p_{i-1}) \\
z_{i-1}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute $z_0$ and $p_0$ (base frame)
$z_0 = [0, 0, 1]^T$, $p_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $p_{end} = [x, y, 0]^T$.

### Step 2 — Compute $z_1$ and $p_1$
After joint 1, $z_1 = R_1^0 z_0 = [0, 0, 1]^T$ (rotation about $z$), and $p_1 = [l_1\cos\theta_1, l_1\sin\theta_1, 0]^T$.

### Step 3 — First column of $J$

$$
J_1 = 
\begin{bmatrix}
z_0 \times (p_{end} - p_0) \\
z_0
\end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-y \\ x \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Second column of $J$

$$
J_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
z_1 \times (p_{end} - p_1) \\
z_1
\end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
0 \\
0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble

$$
J(q) =
\begin{bmatrix}
-y & -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
x & l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 \\
1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Cooperative Jacobian for a Two-Robot Formation

Consider two differential-drive robots $R_1$ and $R_2$ with configurations $q_1 = [x_1, y_1, \theta_1]^T$, $q_2 = [x_2, y_2, \theta_2]^T$. The desired **relative range and bearing** constraint is:

$$
Z^* = [d^*, \beta^*, \alpha^*]^T
$$

where $d$ is inter-robot distance, $\beta$ is bearing from $R_2$ to $R_1$, and $\alpha$ is the relative heading.

### Step 1 — Express the relative constraint $\Phi = \mathcal{F}(q_1, q_2) - Z^*$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2} \\
\beta &= \text{atan2}(y_1 - y_2,\; x_1 - x_2) - \theta_2 \\
\alpha &= \theta_1 - \theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate each constraint

$$
\dot{d} = \frac{(x_1 - x_2)(\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) + (y_1 - y_2)(\dot{y}_1 - \dot{y}_2)}{d}
$$

$$
\dot{\beta} = \frac{-(y_1 - y_2)(\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) + (x_1 - x_2)(\dot{y}_1 - \dot{y}_2)}{d^2} - \dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\dot{\alpha} = \dot{\theta}_1 - \dot{\theta}_2
$$

### Step 3 — Express in cooperative Jacobian form $\dot{\Phi} = J_c \dot{Q}$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{d} \\ \dot{\beta} \\ \dot{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\gamma & \sin\gamma & 0 & -\cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\
-\sin\gamma/d & \cos\gamma/d & -1 & \sin\gamma/d & -\cos\gamma/d & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{y}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{y}_2 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\gamma = \beta + \theta_2$ is the absolute bearing from the global frame.

> **Logic of step 3:** The cooperative Jacobian has a **block structure** — the left $3 \times 3$ block acts on $\dot{q}_1$, the right $3 \times 3$ block (with sign reversal on positional parts) acts on $\dot{q}_2$. This block-banded pattern is the **hallmark of cooperative kinematics** and is what KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Quick-Reference Matrix

| Problem Type | Method to Apply | Pitfall to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| FK of single arm | D-H product | Forgetting the order of multiplication — $A_1 A_2 \neq A_2 A_1$ |
| IK of 2R | Cosine law + atan2 | Using $\arctan$ instead of $\text{atan2}$ — loses quadrant info |
| IK of 3R | Geometric decoupling | Sign ambiguity at wrist |
| Single mobile robot FK | Integration of unicycle | Treating non-holonomic systems as holonomic |
| 2-robot cooperative FK | Block-banded Jacobian | Forgetting the sign-flip in the second robot block |
| Formation DOF | $\rho = nm - \text{rank}(J_c)$ | Mistaking rank deficiency for redundancy |
| Constraint tracking | Inverse $J_c$ via pseudo-inverse | Pseudo-inverse is minimum-norm but not collision-free |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Layer Kinematic Architecture of a Cooperative System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1  AGENT KINEMATICS  n per robot"]
    L1A["Manipulator FK via D-H product"]
    L1B["Mobile FK via twist integration"]
    L1C["Aerial FK via 6 DOF rigid body"]

    L2["LAYER 2  RELATIVE KINEMATICS  pairwise"]
    L2A["Relative pose X sub i over j"]
    L2B["Bearing and range constraints"]
    L2C["Orientation alignment error"]

    L3["LAYER 3  FORMATION KINEMATICS  team level"]
    L3A["Formation graph G V E"]
    L3B["Cooperative Jacobian J sub c"]
    L3C["Team DOF rho equals n times m minus rank J sub c"]

    L1 --> L1A
    L1 --> L1B
    L1 --> L1C
    L1A --> L2
    L1B --> L2
    L1C --> L2
    L2 --> L2A
    L2 --> L2B
    L2 --> L2C
    L2A --> L3
    L2B --> L3
    L2C --> L3
    L3 --> L3A
    L3 --> L3B
    L3 --> L3C
```

## 4.2 DH Frame Assignment Schematic (2R Cooperative Arm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE["BASE FRAME 0  fixed at robot base"]
    J1["JOINT 1 REVOLUTE  axis z0  angle theta 1"]
    L1["LINK 1  length l1  along x1"]
    J2["JOINT 2 REVOLUTE  axis z1  angle theta 2"]
    L2["LINK 2  length l2  along x2"]
    EE["END EFFECTOR FRAME 2  tool point"]

    BASE --> J1
    J1 --> L1
    L1 --> J2
    J2 --> L2
    L2 --> EE
```

## 4.3 Formation Graph Topology (5 Robots, Triangle + Tail)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1["ROBOT 1 LEADER"]
    R2["ROBOT 2"]
    R3["ROBOT 3"]
    R4["ROBOT 4"]
    R5["ROBOT 5"]

    R1 ---|"d12 star"| R2
    R1 ---|"d13 star"| R3
    R2 ---|"d23 star"| R3
    R3 ---|"d34 star"| R4
    R4 ---|"d45 star"| R5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The labels `d12 star`, `d13 star`, etc. denote the **desired relative distance constraints** on the corresponding edges of the formation graph. The graph is **directed** in general (each edge $(i, j)$ has a *parent* $i$ and *child* $j$), but for distance constraints, the edge is undirected.

## 4.4 Reference Frame Hierarchy in a Cooperative System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W["WORLD FRAME W  inertial global"]
    R1F["ROBOT 1 BODY FRAME B1"]
    R2F["ROBOT 2 BODY FRAME B2"]
    R3F["ROBOT 3 BODY FRAME B3"]
    T1F["TOOL FRAME T1  on robot 1"]
    T2F["TOOL FRAME T2  on robot 2"]
    S1F["SENSOR FRAME S1  on robot 1"]
    OBJ["OBJECT FRAME O  shared manipulated object"]

    W --> R1F
    W --> R2F
    W --> R3F
    R1F --> T1F
    R2F --> T2F
    R1F --> S1F
    T1F --> OBJ
    T2F --> OBJ
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this hierarchy matters:** The cooperative kinematic map $\mathcal{F}$ is a *composition* of transforms across these frames. For example, the position of object $O$ as seen by robot 1's sensor is $X_{O}^{S_1} = (X_{W}^{S_1})^{-1} X_W^O$. **Mark loss zone** in KTU valuation: students often confuse the *direction* of transform composition (left- vs right-multiplication).

## 4.5 Cooperative Jacobian Block Structure Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q["Q DOT TEAM VELOCITY"]
        Q1["q1 dot  robot 1  3 by 1"]
        Q2["q2 dot  robot 2  3 by 1"]
        Q3["qm dot  robot m  3 by 1"]
    end

    subgraph JC["COOPERATIVE JACOBIAN J sub c  3k by nm"]
        B11["J11  3 by 3  d Phi 1 over d q1"]
        B12["J12  3 by 3  d Phi 1 over d q2"]
        B21["J21  3 by 3  d Phi 2 over d q1"]
        B22["J22  3 by 3  d Phi 2 over d q2"]
    end

    subgraph PHI["PHI DOT CONSTRAINT VELOCITY  3k by 1"]
        P1["Phi 1 dot  edge 1 to 2"]
        P2["Phi 2 dot  edge 2 to 3"]
        PK["Phi k dot  edge k"]
    end

    Q1 --> B11
    Q1 --> B21
    Q2 --> B12
    Q2 --> B22
    B11 --> P1
    B12 --> P1
    B21 --> P2
    B22 --> P2
```
