# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **Differentiate between forward kinematics and inverse kinematics of a serial manipulator. Why is IK generally considered harder than FK?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Given joint variables $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$, compute the end-effector pose $X \in SE(3)$ via $X = FK(q) = \prod A_i(q_i)$. It is a *unique*, *well-defined* function. (1 mark)
2. **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** Given a desired $X_d$, find $q$ such that $FK(q) = X_d$. The map $X \to q$ is generally *non-unique* (multiple configurations can reach the same pose) and may have *no solution* (out of workspace). (1 mark)
3. **Why harder:** The map is non-linear transcendental; for 6-DOF arms, solving IK requires either closed-form geometric decoupling (Piper / spheroidal-wrist methods) or iterative numerical solvers (Newton–Raphson, Jacobian pseudo-inverse, damped least-squares). FK, in contrast, is a one-pass matrix product. (1 mark)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series 1]`

> **Explain the concept of a non-holonomic constraint in the context of cooperative mobile robots. Give an example from a differential-drive system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A non-holonomic constraint is a velocity-level constraint that **cannot be integrated** into a configuration-level constraint. (1 mark)

For a unicycle / differential-drive robot, the wheel rolling-without-slipping condition yields:

$$
-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

(1 mark)

This is non-integrable: the robot can reach **any** $(x, y, \theta)$ in its configuration space (assuming no obstacles), yet it cannot move instantaneously sideways. This has profound implications for cooperative path planning: inter-robot collision avoidance must use **velocity obstacles** rather than static configuration-space obstacles. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of the D-H convention, derive the forward kinematic model of a 2R planar manipulator. Clearly state the D-H parameter table.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** For the same manipulator, derive the inverse kinematic solution for a desired end-effector position $(x_d, y_d)$. Show both the elbow-up and elbow-down configurations.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — D-H Table (2 marks)**

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $l_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

**Part (a) — Link Transforms (2 marks)**

$$
A_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}
c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & l_1 c_1 \\
s_1 & c_1 & 0 & l_1 s_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
A_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & l_2 c_2 \\
s_2 & c_2 & 0 & l_2 s_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Part (a) — FK product (3 marks)**

$$
T_2^0 = A_1 A_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad
\begin{aligned}
x &= l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} \\
y &= l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12} \\
z &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.

**Part (b) — Cosine Law (3 marks)**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2}
$$

The discriminant condition $x_d^2 + y_d^2 \le (l_1 + l_2)^2$ must hold (workspace reachability).

**Part (b) — Two solutions (2 marks)**

$$
\theta_2^{(1)} = +\arccos(\cdot), \quad \theta_2^{(2)} = -\arccos(\cdot)
$$

corresponding to **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** respectively.

**Part (b) — Solve for $\theta_1$ (2 marks)**

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_d, x_d) - \text{atan2}(l_2\sin\theta_2,\; l_1 + l_2\cos\theta_2)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B-A**
> 1. **Order of multiplication** — $A_1 A_2$ is **not** commutative. Many students write $A_2 A_1$ in the FK product, which represents a *physically different* robot. **Penalty: -2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $\arctan$ instead of $\text{atan2}$** — loses quadrant information, leading to wrong configurations. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting the second (elbow-down) solution** — IK has *multiple* solutions; a complete answer must enumerate them. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
> 4. **Not verifying the workspace condition** — if $(x_d, y_d)$ is unreachable, no solution exists. **Penalty: -1 mark.**

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Two differential-drive robots $R_1$ and $R_2$ must maintain a constant inter-robot distance $d^*$. Formulate this as a cooperative constraint $\Phi(q_1, q_2) = 0$ and derive the **cooperative Jacobian** $J_c$ such that $\dot{\Phi} = J_c \dot{Q}$.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** If the formation has three such distance constraints forming a triangle, compute the **team degrees of freedom** $\rho$ and comment on its physical meaning.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Distance constraint (2 marks)**

$$
\Phi(q_1, q_2) = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2} - d^* = 0
$$

**Part (a) — Differentiate (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{\Phi} = \frac{(x_1 - x_2)(\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) + (y_1 - y_2)(\dot{y}_1 - \dot{y}_2)}{\sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2 + (y_1-y_2)^2}}
$$

Let $d = $ current distance, $\gamma = \text{atan2}(y_1 - y_2,\; x_1 - x_2)$ be the bearing. Then:

$$
\dot{\Phi} = \cos\gamma\,(\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) + \sin\gamma\,(\dot{y}_1 - \dot{y}_2)
$$

**Part (a) — Cooperative Jacobian (3 marks)**

$$
J_c =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\gamma & \sin\gamma & 0 & -\cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0
\end{bmatrix}_{1 \times 6}
$$

This is a **$1 \times 6$ row vector** because we have a single scalar distance constraint and a team configuration of dimension $nm = 2 \times 3 = 6$.

**Part (b) — Team DOF for a triangle (4 marks)**

For a triangular formation with 3 distance constraints, the cooperative Jacobian is $J_c \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 6}$ (3 constraints $\times$ 6 team DOF). Generically, the 3 rows are linearly independent, so $\text{rank}(J_c) = 3$.

$$
\rho = nm - \text{rank}(J_c) = 6 - 3 = 3
$$

**Part (b) — Physical meaning (3 marks)**

The triangle has $\rho = 3$ internal degrees of freedom. These correspond to the **2-DOF planar translation of the centroid** of the triangle plus the **1-DOF in-plane rotation** of the entire formation. The internal shape (the three side lengths) is fully *constrained* by the 3 distance equations — the triangle is *rigid*. To control the team, one specifies the centroid's $(x, y)$ trajectory and the heading $\psi(t)$, yielding 3 control inputs — exactly matching the team DOF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B-B**
> 1. **Forgetting the time-varying bearing $\gamma$** — $J_c$ is **not** constant; it depends on the current configuration. Evaluators look for explicit dependence on $(x_i, y_i)$. **Penalty: -2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong sign on the $R_2$ block** — the Jacobian's second block must be **negative** of the symmetric form (because the constraint involves $q_1 - q_2$, so derivative w.r.t. $\dot{q}_2$ flips sign). **Penalty: -2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing team DOF with single-robot DOF** — students often write $\rho = 6$ (which is the unconstrained team DOF) instead of $\rho = 3$. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
> 4. **Ignoring non-holonomy** — if robots are non-holonomic, the *controllable* team DOF is *less* than the *kinematic* team DOF. Comment on this for full marks. **Penalty: -1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Kinematics**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- [x] Cooperative kinematics is the **geometric study of coupled multi-robot motion under constraints**.
- [x] Three layers: **Agent** → **Relative** → **Formation**.
- [x] The team configuration $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{nm}$ is mapped to the constraint vector $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{3k}$ via the **cooperative Jacobian** $J_c$.

### 📌 Forward Kinematics
- [x] **D-H convention** uses $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$ — 4 params per link.
- [x] FK = product $\prod A_i$; **order matters** ($A_i A_{i+1} \neq A_{i+1} A_i$).
- [x] For 2R: $x = l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12}$, $y = l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12}$.

### 📌 Inverse Kinematics
- [x] **Always use atan2**, never arctan.
- [x] **Multiple solutions** exist (elbow-up/down, wrist-flip).
- [x] **Workspace check**: $d_{reach} \le \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \le l_1 + l_2$.

### 📌 Mobile Robots
- [x] **Unicycle** is non-holonomic: $\dot{x}\sin\theta = \dot{y}\cos\theta$.
- [x] **Ackermann**: $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$.
- [x] Non-holonomy makes both IK and trajectory planning *harder*.

### 📌 Cooperative Specifics
- [x] **Formation graph** $\mathcal{G}(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ encodes which robots constrain which.
- [x] **Cooperative Jacobian** has a **block-banded** structure — one block per robot.
- [x] **Team DOF** $\rho = nm - \text{rank}(J_c)$.
- [x] A **rigid triangular formation** of 2D robots has $\rho = 3$ (2 translation + 1 rotation).

### 📌 KTU Examination Hall Tips
- [x] Always **draw the coordinate frames** before writing the D-H table.
- [x] Always **state the workspace condition** when solving IK.
- [x] Always **comment on the physical meaning** of team DOF — it is the single most under-answered question.
- [x] Always **distinguish holonomic vs non-holonomic** when discussing controllability of the team.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Cooperative Robotics**, the term **"Control"** refers to the systematic methodology, architectural framework, and mathematical algorithms employed to govern the behaviour, motion, and interaction of multiple robotic agents such that they collectively accomplish a shared mission or task. Control spans three hierarchical layers:

1. **Individual Robot Control (Low-Level Control):** Joint-level and wheel-level servo control using feedback loops (e.g., PID) to track desired trajectories for a single agent.
2. **Coordination Control (Mid-Level Control):** Control laws that synchronize the actions of multiple agents — e.g., leader–follower formation, consensus, and swarm aggregation.
3. **Mission/Swarm Control (High-Level Control):** Task allocation, role assignment, and decision-making logic that determines *what* each robot should do.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** As per the PERAT862 syllabus, Module 1 establishes the foundational *control taxonomy* — the distinction between **Centralized, Decentralized, and Distributed** control architectures is a *high-weightage* area in university examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Geese V-Formation**
>
> Imagine a flock of geese flying south for the winter in a **V-formation**:
> - Each goose (robot) flaps its own wings (low-level joint control).
> - The lead goose sets the pace, and every other goose adjusts its position to stay in the updraft of the one in front (coordination control).
> - The flock as a whole "decides" when to rest, when to change altitude, and which path to take (mission control).
>
> **No single goose acts as a "central commander" writing instructions to every other goose.** Instead, each goose reacts to its **neighbours** — this is precisely **decentralized control** in cooperative robotics.
>
> In contrast, a **military parade drill** where a commanding officer shouts orders to every soldier represents **centralized control** — a single master node dictates the motion of all.

This analogy immediately clarifies *why* distributed/decentralized control is preferred for large robot swarms: it is **scalable, fault-tolerant, and robust** to the failure of any single agent.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology of Control (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Agent** | An autonomous robot capable of sensing, computing, and acting. |
| **Centralized Control** | A single master controller computes actions for all agents. |
| **Decentralized Control** | Each agent computes its own action using only local information. |
| **Distributed Control** | Agents share information with neighbours; no global supervisor. |
| **Reference Trajectory** | The desired time-parameterized path the robot must follow. |
| **Feedback Loop** | A control structure where measured output is compared to a reference to generate corrective action. |
| **Open-Loop Control** | Control without measurement of the actual output (no feedback). |
| **Closed-Loop Control** | Control with continuous measurement and correction (with feedback). |
| **PID Controller** | Proportional–Integral–Derivative controller; the workhorse of low-level robot control. |
| **Consensus** | Agreement among agents on a common state value via local interactions. |
| **Formation** | A geometric spatial arrangement maintained by a group of robots. |
| **Swarm** | A large population of simple agents exhibiting emergent cooperative behaviour. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The difference between *Decentralized* and *Distributed* control is subtle but exam-relevant.
> - **Decentralized** = each agent has its **own independent controller** (no sharing required).
> - **Distributed** = agents **explicitly share information** over a communication graph to achieve a common objective.
> - All distributed systems are decentralized, but not all decentralized systems are distributed.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used to evaluate the quality of a control system in cooperative robotics:

- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time for the system output to go from **10 % to 90 %** of the final value. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time for the output to stay within **±2 % (or ±5 %)** of the final value. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** The persistent error between desired and actual output as $t \to \infty$. Dimensionless or in same units as the controlled variable.
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** Maximum peak value above the steady-state, expressed as a percentage.
- **Bandwidth ($\omega_b$):** The frequency range over which the system can faithfully track a sinusoidal input. Unit: **rad/s**.
- **Stability Margin:** A measure of "how far" the system is from instability (gain margin in dB, phase margin in degrees).
- **Communication Range ($R_c$):** Maximum distance over which two agents can exchange data. Unit: **metres (m)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Universal Standard:** The Nyquist Sampling Theorem requires the sampling frequency $f_s$ to satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the signal). For practical robotic control, $f_s$ is typically chosen as **5× to 10×** the closed-loop bandwidth for adequate phase margin — this is the famous **practical engineering rule of thumb** often tested in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Control Architecture for Multi-Robot Systems

A cooperative robotic system has **three control layers**, each operating at a different time-scale:

| Layer | Time-Scale | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Level (Mission / Task)** | Seconds to Minutes | Decide *what* to do | Auction-based task allocation |
| **Mid-Level (Coordination)** | 100 ms to 1 s | Decide *how* to interact | Formation keeping, consensus |
| **Low-Level (Motion / Servo)** | 1 ms to 10 ms | Execute the motion | PID on each wheel motor |

This hierarchy is sometimes called the **"3-Tier Control Stack"** and is a *direct syllabus topic* in Module 1.

---

## 2.2 Centralized vs. Decentralized vs. Distributed Control

### A. Centralized Control
A single high-performance processor (the *central controller*) has **global knowledge** of all agents' states, the environment, and the task. It computes and transmits the control command $u_i(t)$ to every agent $i$ at each time step.

**Advantages:** Globally optimal solutions, simple agent hardware.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, communication bottleneck, poor scalability, intolerant to dynamic agent loss.

### B. Decentralized Control
Each agent computes its own control input $u_i(t)$ using **only its own local sensor data**. There is no inter-agent communication required for the control law itself.

**Advantages:** Inherently robust, scalable, no communication dependency.
**Disadvantages:** Suboptimal global behaviour, possible conflicts between agents.

### C. Distributed Control
A hybrid: each agent computes $u_i(t)$ using its **own state** plus the states of its **immediate neighbours** as defined by a **communication graph** $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$.

**Advantages:** Combines robustness of decentralization with coordination quality of centralization.
**Disadvantages:** Requires a reliable communication network; more complex analysis.

---

## 2.3 The Generic Closed-Loop Control Block

The fundamental block diagram that governs every robotic control loop is:

$$
R(s) \rightarrow \bigoplus \rightarrow [E(s)] \rightarrow [C(s)] \rightarrow [G(s)] \rightarrow [H(s)] \rightarrow \bigoplus
$$

Where:
- $R(s)$ = **Reference input** (Laplace domain) — the desired state.
- $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ = **Error signal** (controller input).
- $C(s)$ = **Controller transfer function** (e.g., PID).
- $G(s)$ = **Plant transfer function** (the robot's physical dynamics).
- $H(s)$ = **Feedback sensor transfer function** (often = 1 for unity feedback).
- $Y(s)$ = **Output** (actual measured state).

The **closed-loop transfer function** (with $H(s) = 1$) is:

$$
T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)\,G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation**. The roots of this equation are the **closed-loop poles**, and their location in the complex $s$-plane **determines stability**. If any pole has a positive real part, the system is **unstable**.

---

## 2.4 The PID Controller — Detailed Form

The **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID)** controller is the canonical low-level control law in robotics:

$$
u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

In the Laplace domain:

$$
C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\, s
$$

| Term | Laplace Form | Physical Meaning | Effect of Increasing $K$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P — Proportional** | $K_p$ | Reacts to *current* error | Faster response, but more overshoot and steady-state error reduction only |
| **I — Integral** | $K_i / s$ | Reacts to *accumulated past* error | Eliminates steady-state error, but can cause oscillations |
| **D — Derivative** | $K_d s$ | Reacts to *rate of change* of error | Damps oscillations, improves stability, sensitive to noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A *pure* P-controller will always have a non-zero steady-state error for a step input in a Type-0 system. Only the **I-action** can drive the steady-state error to zero.

---

## 2.5 Consensus-Based Distributed Control (Cooperative Robotics Specific)

For a multi-agent system with $N$ agents, the **consensus protocol** is the most fundamental distributed control law:

$$
\dot{x}_i(t) = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij} \left( x_j(t) - x_i(t) \right)
$$

Where:
- $x_i(t)$ is the state (position, opinion, etc.) of agent $i$.
- $\mathcal{N}_i$ is the set of neighbours of agent $i$ as defined by the communication graph.
- $a_{ij} \geq 0$ is the **adjacency weight** of edge $(i, j)$ in the graph.
- $(x_j - x_i)$ is the **disagreement** that drives agent $i$ towards agent $j$.

In matrix form for the entire network:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = -L\, \mathbf{x}(t)
$$

Where $L$ is the **graph Laplacian matrix** of the communication graph, defined as:

$$
L_{ii} = \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij}, \quad L_{ij} = -a_{ij} \;\; (i \neq j)
$$

**Stability Theorem:** Consensus is achieved ($\lim_{t \to \infty} \vert x_i(t) - x_j(t) \vert = 0$ for all $i, j$) **if and only if** the communication graph $\mathcal{G}$ is **connected** (for undirected) or contains a **directed spanning tree** (for directed). The convergence rate is governed by the **algebraic connectivity** $\lambda_2(L)$ — the second-smallest eigenvalue of $L$.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed-loop TF | $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | Stability: poles of $T(s)$ in LHP |
| 2 | Characteristic Eq. | $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ | Roots = closed-loop poles |
| 3 | PID (time) | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Real-time domain |
| 4 | PID (Laplace) | $C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | For block diagram analysis |
| 5 | Consensus Law | $\dot{x}_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)$ | Distributed, local info only |
| 6 | Consensus in Matrix Form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = -L \mathbf{x}$ | $L$ = graph Laplacian |
| 7 | Laplacian Entry | $L_{ii} = \sum_j a_{ij}, \; L_{ij} = -a_{ij}$ | Symmetric if undirected |
| 8 | Consensus Convergence | $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ | Algebraic connectivity of undirected graph |
| 9 | Unicycle Kinematics | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \; \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \; \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Standard mobile robot model |
| 10 | Lyapunov Stability | $\dot{V}(x) < 0 \;\forall x \neq 0$ | $V$ positive definite, $\dot V$ negative definite |
| 11 | Steady-State Error (step) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ (Type-0, P-only) | Only PI/PID eliminate |
| 12 | Nyquist Criterion | $Z = N + P$ | Encirclements test for stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute values, norms, and determinants in this sheet are written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x_i - x_j \vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cooperative control is the **backbone** of every modern multi-robot application:

| Application | Control Type Used | Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Warehouse Automation (Amazon Kiva)** | Centralized task allocation + Decentralized motion | Logistics |
| **Agricultural Drone Swarms** | Distributed consensus for area coverage | Agriculture |
| **Autonomous Vehicle Platooning** | Leader–follower formation control | Transportation |
| **Search and Rescue (DARPA SubT)** | Distributed multi-agent SLAM | Defence |
| **Surgical Robot Teams** | Centralized high-level + redundant low-level safety | Medical |
| **Self-Healing Power Grids** | Distributed consensus on voltage/frequency | Energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Algebraic Connectivity** $\lambda_2(L)$ is the *single most important scalar* in distributed multi-agent control. It quantifies how "well-connected" the communication network is, and its reciprocal $\dfrac{1}{\lambda_2(L)}$ gives a lower bound on consensus convergence time. KTU examiners love asking: *"If you add a new communication link, what happens to convergence speed?"* — the answer: it **increases** $\lambda_2(L)$, hence **faster convergence**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function from Block Diagram

**Goal:** Starting from the basic feedback block diagram, derive the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$.

**Step 1 — Define the error signal.**

The summing junction computes the difference between reference and feedback:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the controller and plant in series.**

The forward path multiplies the error by $C(s)$ and then by $G(s)$:

$$
Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s)\, E(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $E(s)$.**

$$
Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s)\, \big[ R(s) - H(s)\, Y(s) \big]
$$

**Step 4 — Expand.**

$$
Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s)\, R(s) - C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)\, Y(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Collect all $Y(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$
Y(s) + C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)\, Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s)\, R(s)
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $Y(s)$.**

$$
Y(s) \big[ 1 + C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s) \big] = C(s)\, G(s)\, R(s)
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the ratio $Y(s)/R(s)$.**

$$
T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)\, G(s)}{1 + C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case:** For **unity feedback** $H(s) = 1$, this reduces to the familiar $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Controller Output Response to a Step Input

**Goal:** Derive the time-domain response of a PID controller to a unit step input $r(t) = 1$.

**Step 1 — The step input has constant value, so the error is:**

$$
e(t) = 1 \quad \text{(for } t \geq 0 \text{ in a simplified Type-1 system)}
$$

**Step 2 — The proportional term gives an instantaneous response:**

$$
u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) = K_p
$$

**Step 3 — The integral term accumulates the error over time:**

$$
u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau = K_i \int_0^t 1\, d\tau = K_i\, t
$$

**Step 4 — The derivative term, for a constant error, is zero:**

$$
u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} = K_d \cdot 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Total control output is the sum:**

$$
u(t) = K_p + K_i\, t + 0 = K_p + K_i\, t
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because the integral term grows **linearly and unbounded** with time, the controller output $u(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$. This is precisely *why* the integral action **eliminates** steady-state error — the controller keeps "pushing" harder and harder until the error becomes zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Consensus Convergence Using the Graph Laplacian

**Goal:** Show that for the consensus law $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = -L \mathbf{x}$, all agents converge to a common value if the graph is connected.

**Step 1 — Write the system as a linear ODE:**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = -L\, \mathbf{x}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — The solution is the matrix exponential:**

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{-L t}\, \mathbf{x}(0)
$$

**Step 3 — Diagonalize the Laplacian** (for a symmetric $L$, $L = U \Lambda U^T$ with $U$ orthogonal and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_N)$):

$$
e^{-L t} = U\, e^{-\Lambda t}\, U^T = U\, \text{diag}(e^{-\lambda_1 t}, e^{-\lambda_2 t}, \dots, e^{-\lambda_N t})\, U^T
$$

**Step 4 — Recall the eigenvalues of the Laplacian of a connected graph:**

$$
0 = \lambda_1 < \lambda_2 \leq \lambda_3 \leq \dots \leq \lambda_N
$$

**Step 5 — In the limit $t \to \infty$:**

$$
e^{-\lambda_1 t} = e^{0} = 1, \quad e^{-\lambda_i t} \to 0 \;\; \text{for } i \geq 2
$$

**Step 6 — Therefore the limiting state is:**

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbf{x}(t) = U \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix} U^T \mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{1}\, \bar{x}
$$

Where $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_i x_i(0)$ is the **average** of the initial states (the "consensus value").

**Step 7 — Disagreement vector decays at rate $\lambda_2$:**

$$
\vert x_i(t) - x_j(t) \vert \leq \vert x_i(0) - x_j(0) \vert\, e^{-\lambda_2(L) t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This bound is the **engineering justification** for the importance of $\lambda_2(L)$ — it is the *exact exponential rate* of consensus convergence.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Control Architectures

| Property | Centralized | Decentralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Global knowledge required?** | Yes | No | No (only local neighbours) |
| **Communication needed?** | To all agents | None | To neighbours only |
| **Scalability** | Poor (computation $O(N^3)$ typical) | Excellent ($O(1)$ per agent) | Good ($O(\text{deg})$ per agent) |
| **Fault tolerance** | Low (single point of failure) | Highest | High |
| **Optimality** | Globally optimal | Locally greedy | Near-optimal if well-connected |
| **Implementation cost** | High (powerful central CPU) | Low (simple on-board) | Moderate (comm hardware) |
| **Mathematical framework** | Single optimization problem | Independent local laws | Consensus on graphs |
| **KTU example** | Server-based fleet | Roomba vacuums | Drone swarm light show |
| **Convergence guarantee** | Always (if well-posed) | No general guarantee | Guaranteed if $\lambda_2 > 0$ |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Consensus on a 3-Robot Line

**Setup:** Three robots A, B, C in a line, with communication edges A–B and B–C. Initial positions:

$$
x_A(0) = 0, \quad x_B(0) = 10, \quad x_C(0) = 20
$$

**Step 1 — Build the adjacency matrix** (symmetric, unweighted):

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the degree matrix $D$:**

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Laplacian $L = D - A$:**

$$
L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the eigenvalues of $L$:**

$$
\lambda_1 = 0, \quad \lambda_2 = 1, \quad \lambda_3 = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Consensus value is the average of initial states:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{0 + 10 + 20}{3} = 10
$$

**Step 6 — As $t \to \infty$, all three robots converge to position 10** (the centroid). The convergence rate is governed by $\lambda_2 = 1$, so the disagreement decays as $e^{-t}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The **algebraic connectivity** $\lambda_2 = 1$ of this 3-node line graph is small, so consensus is *slow*. If we added a direct edge A–C (forming a triangle), $\lambda_2$ would jump to **2** (for a complete graph on 3 nodes, $\lambda_2 = N = 3$), giving **3× faster consensus**. This is why KTU papers often ask about "which topology is best" — **denser graphs = faster consensus**, but at the cost of communication overhead.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Closed-Loop Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R of s] --> SUM1{Subtract}
    SUM1 --> E[Error Signal E of s]
    E --> C[Controller C of s]
    C --> G[Plant G of s - Robot Dynamics]
    G --> Y[Output Y of s - Actual State]
    Y --> SUM1
    Y --> OUT[Measured Feedback to Sensors]
    OUT -.-> SUM1
```

**Reading Guide:**
- $R(s)$ enters from the left.
- $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ is the **error** (the "driving force" of the controller).
- $C(s)$ is the controller block — implement PID here.
- $G(s)$ is the **plant** — the robot's physical dynamics (motors, wheels, body).
- $Y(s)$ is the actual measured state, fed back to the summing junction.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: 3-Tier Cooperative Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH LEVEL - MISSION CONTROL"]
        M1[Task Allocation]
        M2[Role Assignment]
        M3[Mission Planner]
    end
    subgraph MID["MID LEVEL - COORDINATION CONTROL"]
        C1[Consensus Law]
        C2[Formation Control]
        C3[Collision Avoidance]
    end
    subgraph LOW["LOW LEVEL - MOTION CONTROL"]
        L1[PID on Wheels]
        L2[Joint Servo]
        L3[Trajectory Tracker]
    end
    HIGH --> MID
    MID --> LOW
    LOW -->|Sensor Feedback| MID
    MID -->|Task Status| HIGH
```

**Reading Guide:** Information flows **downward** (commands) and **upward** (feedback). The timescales are: HIGH = seconds-to-minutes, MID = 100 ms, LOW = 1–10 ms.

---

## 4.3 Communication Graph for a 4-Agent Swarm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Agent 1] --- A2[Agent 2]
    A2 --- A3[Agent 3]
    A3 --- A4[Agent 4]
    A1 --- A3
    A2 --- A4
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Nodes** = agents (robots).
- **Edges** = bidirectional communication links.
- This graph has $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ (it is connected), so consensus **is** guaranteed.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Consensus Control Loop on a Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X_I[x_i - own state] --> SUM_C{Subtract}
    X_J[x_j - neighbour state] --> SUM_C
    SUM_C --> GAIN[Weight a_ij]
    GAIN --> SUM_T{Sum over neighbours}
    SUM_T --> INT[Integrator]
    INT --> U_I[u_i - control input for agent i]
    U_I --> ROBOT_I[Agent i Dynamics]
    ROBOT_I --> X_I
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the per-agent implementation of the consensus law. Each agent maintains a state $x_i$, compares it to each neighbour's $x_j$, weights the difference by $a_{ij}$, sums over all neighbours, and integrates to get the control input $u_i$.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Flowchart: When to Use Each Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Control Architecture] --> Q1{Number of Agents N}
    Q1 -->|N is small, less than 10| Q2{Task is safety critical}
    Q1 -->|N is large, greater than 50| DEC[Decentralized]
    Q1 -->|N is medium, 10 to 50| DIS[Distributed]
    Q2 -->|Yes| CEN[Centralized - for global guarantees]
    Q2 -->|No| DIS
    DEC --> DONE[Implementation: local sensors only]
    DIS --> DONE2[Implementation: local comms with neighbours]
    CEN --> DONE3[Implementation: master controller broadcasts]
```

**Reading Guide:** This decision tree is a *direct exam-ready summary* of when to pick which architecture. A typical KTU question is: *"For a 100-robot warehouse, which architecture would you recommend and why?"* — Answer: **Distributed**, because centralized is unscalable and decentralized cannot achieve coordinated behaviour.

---

## 4.6 State-Space Block: From Block Diagram to Modern Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Input u of t] --> B[Matrix B]
    B --> INT[Integrator]
    X[State x of t] --> A[Matrix A]
    A --> ADD{Add}
    ADD --> INT
    INT --> X
    X --> C[Matrix C]
    C --> Y[Output y of t]
```

**Reading Guide:** Modern control uses state-space representation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x}$. This is the foundation for **LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)** and **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** used in advanced cooperative robotics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between centralized and decentralized control in multi-robot systems. State two advantages of decentralized control.**

**Model Answer:**

**Centralized control** uses a single master controller that has global knowledge of all robot states and the environment. It computes and transmits the control command to every agent. In contrast, **decentralized control** allows each robot to compute its own control action using **only its local on-board sensor data**, without requiring a central supervisor or inter-agent communication.

**Two advantages of decentralized control:**

1. **Fault tolerance:** If one robot fails, the rest of the system continues to function — there is no single point of failure.
2. **Scalability:** The computational and communication burden does not grow with the number of agents, making it suitable for large swarms.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the algebraic connectivity of a graph. Why is it important in consensus-based multi-robot control?**

**Model Answer:**

The **algebraic connectivity** $\lambda_2(L)$ is defined as the **second-smallest eigenvalue** of the graph Laplacian matrix $L$ of the communication network.

It is important in consensus control because:

1. **Convergence guarantee:** Consensus is achieved if and only if $\lambda_2(L) > 0$, i.e., the graph is connected.
2. **Convergence speed:** The disagreement between agents decays exponentially as $e^{-\lambda_2(L) t}$, so a larger $\lambda_2(L)$ directly implies faster consensus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "first eigenvalue" instead of "second-smallest." The smallest eigenvalue of $L$ is *always* zero for any graph. The correct term is **"second-smallest"** or **"Fiedler value"** (named after mathematician Miroslav Fiedler).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the basic structure of a closed-loop control system. Derive the expression for the closed-loop transfer function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the consensus-based distributed control law for a multi-robot system. Using the graph Laplacian, show that consensus is achieved when the graph is connected. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Block Diagram:**

```
R(s) → (+) → E(s) → [C(s)] → [G(s)] → Y(s) → Output
         (-)                              |
         ↑                                |
         └──── H(s) ←────────────────────┘
```

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The error signal at the summing junction is:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\, Y(s)
$$

**Step 2:** The output $Y(s)$ is obtained by cascading the controller and plant:

$$
Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s)\, E(s)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s) \big[ R(s) - H(s)\, Y(s) \big]
$$

**Step 4:** Expand and collect $Y(s)$ terms on the left:

$$
Y(s) + C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)\, Y(s) = C(s)\, G(s)\, R(s)
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $Y(s)$ and solve for the ratio $Y(s)/R(s)$:

$$
T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)\, G(s)}{1 + C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)}
$$

For **unity feedback** ($H(s) = 1$):

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)\, G(s)}{1 + C(s)\, G(s)}
$$

**Step 6:** The denominator $1 + C(s)G(s)H(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation**, whose roots are the closed-loop poles. For BIBO stability, all poles must lie in the **open Left-Half Plane** (LHP), i.e., have negative real parts.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**The Consensus Law:**

Consider $N$ agents, with the state of agent $i$ denoted $x_i(t) \in \mathbb{R}$. The **consensus protocol** is:

$$
\dot{x}_i(t) = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij} \big( x_j(t) - x_i(t) \big)
$$

Here, $\mathcal{N}_i$ is the set of neighbours of agent $i$, and $a_{ij} \geq 0$ is the weight of the communication edge $(i, j)$.

**Matrix Form:**

Stacking the states into a vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N]^T$, the system can be written as:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = -L\, \mathbf{x}(t)
$$

where $L$ is the **graph Laplacian** with entries $L_{ii} = \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij}$ and $L_{ij} = -a_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$.

**Proof of Convergence:**

The Laplacian $L$ is a real symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. For a connected undirected graph, its eigenvalues satisfy:

$$
0 = \lambda_1 < \lambda_2 \leq \lambda_3 \leq \dots \leq \lambda_N
$$

The solution of the ODE is $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{-L t}\, \mathbf{x}(0)$. Diagonalizing $L = U \Lambda U^T$:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = U \begin{bmatrix} e^{-\lambda_1 t} & 0 & \cdots \\ 0 & e^{-\lambda_2 t} & \cdots \\ \vdots & & \ddots \end{bmatrix} U^T \mathbf{x}(0)
$$

As $t \to \infty$, only the $\lambda_1 = 0$ term survives (all other exponentials decay to zero):

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{1}\, \bar{x}
$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i x_i(0)$ is the **average** of the initial states. Thus, **all agents converge to a common value** — this is the definition of consensus.

**Key Condition:** This convergence requires $\lambda_2 > 0$, which is guaranteed if and only if the graph is **connected**.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the PID controller in detail. Discuss the effect of increasing each of $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ on the closed-loop response. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the three-tier cooperative control architecture for multi-robot systems. Compare centralized, decentralized, and distributed control. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**PID Controller Definition:**

The Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) controller computes a control signal $u(t)$ as a weighted sum of three terms based on the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$:

$$
u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Laplace domain form:**

$$
C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\, s = \frac{K_d\, s^2 + K_p\, s + K_i}{s}
$$

**Effect of Each Gain on Closed-Loop Response:**

| Gain Increased | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | Small change | Decreases (but not zero) | Degrades |
| $K_i \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | Increases | **Eliminates** | Degrades significantly |
| $K_d \uparrow$ | Minor change | **Decreases** | Decreases | Minor effect | Improves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Summary:** P-action provides *speed*, I-action provides *accuracy* (zero steady-state error), D-action provides *damping* and *stability*. A well-tuned PID balances all three.

**Ziegler–Nichols Tuning (commonly tested):**
- **Step 1:** Set $K_i = 0$, $K_d = 0$. Increase $K_p$ until the system oscillates with constant amplitude. Record this as $K_u$ (ultimate gain) and the oscillation period as $T_u$.
- **Step 2:** Set $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 1.2 K_u / T_u$, $K_d = 0.075 K_u T_u$.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Three-Tier Cooperative Control Architecture:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  HIGH LEVEL — Mission / Task Control    │
│  (Time-scale: seconds to minutes)       │
│  • Task allocation                      │
│  • Role assignment                      │
│  • Mission planning                     │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               │ (Task description)
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MID LEVEL — Coordination Control       │
│  (Time-scale: 100 ms to 1 s)            │
│  • Consensus protocols                  │
│  • Formation keeping                    │
│  • Collision avoidance                  │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               │ (Desired trajectories)
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LOW LEVEL — Motion / Servo Control     │
│  (Time-scale: 1 ms to 10 ms)            │
│  • PID on each motor                    │
│  • Joint servo control                  │
│  • Trajectory tracking                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Centralized | Decentralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Global knowledge?** | Yes | No | No |
| **Communication?** | To all | None | To neighbours only |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Fault tolerance** | Low | High | High |
| **Optimality** | Global optimum | Local greedy | Near-optimal |
| **Example** | Server-controlled fleet | Independent vacuum robots | Drone light show |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to confuse *decentralized* and *distributed*. In **decentralized** control, agents act entirely alone. In **distributed** control, agents **explicitly communicate with neighbours** to achieve a common goal. Always clarify this distinction in your exam answer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Control" in Cooperative Robotics:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the consensus law** — The correct form is $\dot{x}_i = \sum a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)$ with the difference in that order. Writing $x_i - x_j$ will make the system **diverge**.
> 2. **Confusing "smallest" and "second-smallest" eigenvalue** — Always write **"second-smallest eigenvalue $\lambda_2(L)$"** for algebraic connectivity, never just "eigenvalue of $L$."
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram** — A question worth 7 marks on closed-loop systems **requires** a neat, labeled block diagram. Drawing it earns at least 2 marks before you even start writing equations.
> 4. **Mixing time-domain and Laplace-domain PID** — Pick one and stay consistent. If you write $K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ in the time-domain, the marker will mark you down.
> 5. **Not specifying graph connectivity** — Any consensus-related answer must explicitly state the condition "**the graph must be connected**" (or "must contain a spanning tree" for directed graphs). Omitting this loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultra-High-Density Revision — Module 1: Control**
>
> ✅ **Three Layers of Control:** Mission (high) → Coordination (mid) → Motion (low). Each operates at a different time-scale.
>
> ✅ **Three Architectures:** Centralized (one master), Decentralized (no comm), Distributed (neighbour comm via graph).
>
> ✅ **Closed-Loop TF:** $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$ — characteristic equation in denominator.
>
> ✅ **Stability:** All closed-loop poles must lie in the **Left-Half Plane** (LHP) of the $s$-plane.
>
> ✅ **PID:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot e$. P = speed, I = zero steady-state error, D = damping.
>
> ✅ **Consensus Law:** $\dot{x}_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)$, with matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = -L\mathbf{x}$.
>
> ✅ **Laplacian:** $L_{ii} = \sum_j a_{ij}$, $L_{ij} = -a_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$. Always **symmetric** for undirected graphs.
>
> ✅ **Convergence Condition:** $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ ⇔ graph is connected ⇔ consensus is achieved.
>
> ✅ **Convergence Rate:** Disagreement decays as $e^{-\lambda_2(L) t}$. **Denser graph = faster consensus.**
>
> ✅ **Final Consensus Value:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_i x_i(0)$ (the average of initial states).
>
> ✅ **Steady-State Error:** Only **integral action** can drive $e_{ss} \to 0$ for a step input on a Type-0 plant.
>
> ✅ **Unicycle Model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \; \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \; \dot\theta = \omega$ — the standard mobile robot kinematic model.
>
> ✅ **Nyquist Sampling:** Sample at least **5× to 10×** the closed-loop bandwidth for adequate phase margin in practice.
>
> ✅ **Ziegler–Nichols PID Tuning:** Find ultimate gain $K_u$ and period $T_u$, then set $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 1.2 K_u / T_u$, $K_d = 0.075 K_u T_u$.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Amazon Kiva (centralized + decentralized motion), drone light shows (distributed consensus), autonomous platoons (leader–follower formation).
>
> ✅ **Key Insight:** $\lambda_2(L)$ is the **single most important scalar** in distributed cooperative control — it governs both *whether* consensus is possible and *how fast* it happens.

---

*End of Module 1 — Control Notes | PERAT862 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite State Machines as Robot Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Finite State Machines as Robot Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Finite State Machine (FSM)** is a mathematical model of computation that describes a system by a finite number of discrete states, transitions between those states, and the actions or outputs associated with each transition. In the context of **robotics**, an FSM is employed as a high-level behavioral controller that partitions the robot's continuous task execution into a sequence of well-defined discrete operational states, each governed by a deterministic set of sensor inputs and actuator outputs.

Formally, an FSM used as a robot controller is represented as a **6-tuple transducer**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, \lambda, q_0)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Finite, non-empty set of robot behavioral states
- $\Sigma$ = Finite input alphabet (sensor events, perception triggers)
- $\Delta$ = Finite output alphabet (actuator commands, motion primitives)
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ = State transition function
- $\lambda : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ = Output function
- $q_0 \in Q$ = Initial state of the robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In cooperative robotics, FSMs form the **behavioral backbone** of individual agents, providing a modular, verifiable, and computationally efficient alternative to monolithic control architectures such as neural networks or fuzzy logic systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **domestic floor-cleaning robot** (like a Roomba). The robot does not "think" continuously; instead, it operates in a small set of behavioral **modes**:
1. **IDLE** – Waiting on the dock, fully charged.
2. **NAVIGATING** – Moving through the room, avoiding obstacles.
3. **EDGE_DETECTED** – Found a stair edge; backs up and rotates.
4. **DOCKING** – Returning to the charging station.
5. **ERROR** – Wheel stuck; emits alert sound.

The robot switches from one mode to another only when a **specific event** occurs (battery low, obstacle hit, edge detected). This discrete switching is exactly what an **FSM models**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Think of an FSM as a "**mood ring**" for a robot. The robot has only a few moods (states), and its mood changes only when something meaningful happens in the world (input/event). It then expresses that mood through actions (outputs).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in FSM-Based Robot Control

- **Sampling period** $T_s$: typically **10 ms to 100 ms** for mobile robots.
- **State transition latency**: must be bounded by the **real-time deadline** of the robot task.
- **State space cardinality** $\vert Q \vert$: typically **5 to 50 states** for cooperative robotic tasks.
- **Hard real-time guarantee**: worst-case execution time (WCET) of the transition logic must be **< $T_s$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FSMs in Cooperative Robotics?** Cooperative systems involve multiple agents (robots) that must communicate their **state** to each other. Since states are *discrete and enumerable*, inter-robot coordination becomes a simple handshake protocol — far easier than negotiating continuous trajectories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: Step-by-Step Logic

An FSM-based robot controller executes the following **control loop** at every sampling instant $kT_s$:

1. **Read** the current state $q(k) \in Q$ from the state register.
2. **Acquire** the sensory input vector $s(k) \in \Sigma$ from sensors (LiDAR, encoders, IMU, vision).
3. **Evaluate** the transition function $\delta(q(k), s(k))$ to compute the next state $q(k+1)$.
4. **Evaluate** the output function $\lambda(q(k), s(k))$ to determine the actuator command $a(k) \in \Delta$.
5. **Dispatch** the actuator command to the low-level motor drivers.
6. **Update** the state register: $q(k) \leftarrow q(k+1)$.
7. **Repeat** at the next sampling instant $(k+1)T_s$.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Read State):** The FSM is a *memory-based* system; without remembering $q(k)$, it cannot make decisions.
- **Step 2 (Sensor Read):** Transitions are *event-driven*; without fresh sensor data, the robot cannot perceive the world.
- **Step 3 (Transition):** This is the **decision-making core** — implemented in hardware as a lookup table or in software as a `switch-case` block.
- **Step 4 (Output):** The FSM *commits* to an action. In Moore machines, this depends only on the state; in Mealy machines, on both state and input.
- **Step 5 (Dispatch):** The actuator command reaches the wheels, gripper, or arm.
- **Step 6 (Update):** The robot "moves" to a new behavioral regime.
- **Step 7 (Loop):** The FSM is **cyclic** and runs at a fixed rate.

## 2.2 Moore Machine vs. Mealy Machine

| Property | Moore Machine | Mealy Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Output depends on | State only | State **and** Input |
| Output function | $\lambda(q)$ | $\lambda(q, \sigma)$ |
| Number of states | Usually more | Usually fewer |
| Output stability | Outputs stable for entire state duration | Outputs can change mid-state |
| Suitability for robotics | Trajectory control, steady behaviors | Reactive behaviors, sensor-driven control |
| Example in robot | "Moving" state (output: constant velocity) | "Avoiding obstacle" (output: turn left/right based on sensor) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Set of robot states | $\vert Q \vert = 5$ to $50$ (dimensionless) |
| $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet (sensor events) | $\vert \Sigma \vert = 2^n$ for $n$ binary sensors |
| $\Delta$ | Output alphabet (motor commands) | $\vert \Delta \vert = 2^m$ for $m$ binary actuators |
| $\delta$ | Transition function | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ |
| $\lambda$ | Output function | $\lambda: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ (Mealy) or $\lambda: Q \rightarrow \Delta$ (Moore) |
| $q_0$ | Initial state | $q_0 \in Q$ |
| $T_s$ | Sampling period | $10$ ms to $100$ ms |
| $f_c$ | Control loop frequency | $f_c = \frac{1}{T_s}$ Hz |
| $N_{trans}$ | Total transitions | $N_{trans} = \vert Q \vert \times \vert \Sigma \vert$ (worst case) |
| $t_{WCET}$ | Worst-case execution time | $t_{WCET} < T_s$ for hard real-time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In **autonomous warehouse robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva)**, FSMs drive the behavior of hundreds of robots. Each robot is in a state such as *AwaitingTask*, *MovingToShelf*, *LiftingShelf*, *Transporting*, *DroppingPod*, *ReturningToDock*. The **discreteness** ensures predictable coordination and easy debugging — critical when 500 robots are working in the same space.

## 2.4 Determinism and Completeness in Robot FSMs

For an FSM to be a **valid robot controller**, it must satisfy two mathematical properties:

**Determinism:**
$$\forall q \in Q, \forall \sigma \in \Sigma : \vert \delta(q, \sigma) \vert = 1$$

At every state, for every possible input, there is exactly **one** next state. This ensures the robot never "freezes" in an undefined condition.

**Completeness:**
$$\forall q \in Q, \forall \sigma \in \Sigma : \delta(q, \sigma) \text{ is defined}$$

The transition function must be **total**. Undefined transitions in robotics are dangerous — they can cause the robot to enter an undefined state, leading to crashes or unsafe behavior. Engineering practice adds a **default `ERROR` or `SAFE_STOP` state** to handle unanticipated sensor inputs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example: Pick-and-Place Robot FSM

Consider a simple **single-arm pick-and-place robot** for a cooperative manufacturing cell. The robot has the following states:

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5\}$$

Where:
- $q_0$ = `IDLE`
- $q_1$ = `MOVING_TO_PICK`
- $q_2$ = `PICKING`
- $q_3$ = `MOVING_TO_PLACE`
- $q_4$ = `PLACING`
- $q_5$ = `EMERGENCY_STOP`

The input alphabet represents sensor events:

$$\Sigma = \{\text{START}, \text{AT\_PICK}, \text{GRIPPED}, \text{AT\_PLACE}, \text{RELEASED}, \text{ESTOP}\}$$

The output alphabet represents actuator commands:

$$\Delta = \{\text{STOP}, \text{MOVE\_PICK}, \text{CLOSE\_GRIPPER}, \text{MOVE\_PLACE}, \text{OPEN\_GRIPPER}, \text{BRAKE}\}$$

### 3.1.1 State Transition Table (Mealy Form)

| Current State $q$ | Input $\sigma$ | Next State $\delta(q,\sigma)$ | Output $\lambda(q,\sigma)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ (IDLE) | START | $q_1$ | MOVE_PICK |
| $q_0$ (IDLE) | ESTOP | $q_5$ | BRAKE |
| $q_1$ (MOVING_TO_PICK) | AT_PICK | $q_2$ | STOP |
| $q_1$ (MOVING_TO_PICK) | ESTOP | $q_5$ | BRAKE |
| $q_2$ (PICKING) | GRIPPED | $q_3$ | MOVE_PLACE |
| $q_2$ (PICKING) | ESTOP | $q_5$ | BRAKE |
| $q_3$ (MOVING_TO_PLACE) | AT_PLACE | $q_4$ | STOP |
| $q_3$ (MOVING_TO_PLACE) | ESTOP | $q_5$ | BRAKE |
| $q_4$ (PLACING) | RELEASED | $q_0$ | OPEN_GRIPPER |
| $q_4$ (PLACING) | ESTOP | $q_5$ | BRAKE |
| $q_5$ (EMERGENCY_STOP) | RESET | $q_0$ | STOP |

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Completeness Check

For completeness, we must verify that for every state $q \in Q$, every input $\sigma \in \Sigma$ is handled.

**State $q_0$ coverage:** $\Sigma$ has 6 events. Rows for $q_0$ cover `START` and `ESTOP`. The remaining 4 events (`AT_PICK`, `GRIPPED`, `AT_PLACE`, `RELEASED`) are **not expected** in IDLE, so by the engineering convention they are routed to a "self-loop with safe output":

$$q_0 \xrightarrow{\text{AT\_PICK/GRIPPED/AT\_PLACE/RELEASED}} q_0 \text{ with } \lambda = \text{STOP}$$

**State $q_5$ (EMERGENCY_STOP) coverage:** Only the `RESET` event is meaningful; all other events produce a self-loop with $\lambda = \text{BRAKE}$.

After adding these implicit transitions, the FSM is **complete**:

$$\forall q \in Q, \forall \sigma \in \Sigma : \delta(q, \sigma) \text{ is defined}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation: Number of Transitions

The maximum possible number of transitions in a complete Mealy FSM is:

$$N_{trans}^{\max} = \vert Q \vert \times \vert \Sigma \vert$$

Substituting our values:

$$N_{trans}^{\max} = 6 \times 6 = 36 \text{ transitions}$$

The actual number of explicit transitions in the table is $11$. The remaining $25$ are **implicit "self-loop on safe action"** transitions — a common engineering simplification.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix: State Reachability

We verify that all states are reachable from $q_0$ (initial state):

$$q_0 \xrightarrow{\text{START}} q_1 \xrightarrow{\text{AT\_PICK}} q_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GRIPPED}} q_3 \xrightarrow{\text{AT\_PLACE}} q_4 \xrightarrow{\text{RELEASED}} q_0$$

$$q_0 \xrightarrow{\text{ESTOP}} q_5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reachable set} \quad R(q_0) &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5\} \\
&= Q
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R(q_0) = Q$, the FSM is **fully reachable** — no "dead states" exist.

### 3.1.5 Moore Equivalence (Conversion)

To convert a Mealy machine to an equivalent Moore machine, each Mealy state is split so that outputs become state-dependent. The new state set becomes:

$$Q_{Moore} = Q \times \Delta_{subset}$$

For the simple FSM above, the Moore equivalent has more states (one per unique output per state). The conversion formula for a state producing $k$ distinct outputs is:

$$\vert Q_{Moore} \vert = \sum_{q \in Q} \text{card}(\lambda(q, \cdot))$$

For our example, each state produces a single output, so:

$$\vert Q_{Moore} \vert = 6 = \vert Q \vert$$

(Conversion is identity in this case. In general, the Moore machine may have more states.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FSM-Based Robot Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sensor Layer (LiDAR, IMU, Encoders, Camera)"] --> B["Input Encoder (sigma in Sigma)"]
    B --> C["Current State Register (q_k)"]
    C --> D["Transition Logic (delta)"]
    D --> E["Next State Register (q_k plus 1)"]
    E --> C
    D --> F["Output Logic (lambda)"]
    F --> G["Actuator Command (a in Delta)"]
    G --> H["Actuator Layer (Motors, Gripper, Wheels)"]
    I["Real-Time Clock (T_s)"] --> D
    I --> F
    J["Safety Watchdog"] -.-> D
    J -.-> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Sensor Layer** is the robot's perception.
- The **Input Encoder** discretizes continuous sensor data into finite events (e.g., a LiDAR distance is converted into `NEAR` / `MID` / `FAR`).
- The **Current State Register** stores the present state $q(k)$.
- The **Transition Logic** computes the next state.
- The **Output Logic** computes the actuator command.
- The **Real-Time Clock** triggers the FSM at period $T_s$.
- The **Safety Watchdog** forces a transition to `EMERGENCY_STOP` if a deadline is missed.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram: Pick-and-Place Robot

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0_IDLE
    q0_IDLE --> q1_MOVING_TO_PICK : START / MOVE_PICK
    q1_MOVING_TO_PICK --> q2_PICKING : AT_PICK / STOP
    q2_PICKING --> q3_MOVING_TO_PLACE : GRIPPED / MOVE_PLACE
    q3_MOVING_TO_PLACE --> q4_PLACING : AT_PLACE / STOP
    q4_PLACING --> q0_IDLE : RELEASED / OPEN_GRIPPER
    q0_IDLE --> q5_EMERGENCY_STOP : ESTOP / BRAKE
    q1_MOVING_TO_PICK --> q5_EMERGENCY_STOP : ESTOP / BRAKE
    q2_PICKING --> q5_EMERGENCY_STOP : ESTOP / BRAKE
    q3_MOVING_TO_PLACE --> q5_EMERGENCY_STOP : ESTOP / BRAKE
    q4_PLACING --> q5_EMERGENCY_STOP : ESTOP / BRAKE
    q5_EMERGENCY_STOP --> q0_IDLE : RESET / STOP
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each arrow is labeled in the form `INPUT / OUTPUT`.
- All operational states ($q_0$ to $q_4$) have an emergency transition to $q_5$ on the `ESTOP` event.
- The cycle $q_0 \rightarrow q_1 \rightarrow q_2 \rightarrow q_3 \rightarrow q_4 \rightarrow q_0$ represents one full pick-and-place cycle.
- The double circles convention of automata theory is omitted here for clarity; the `[*]` indicates the start node.

## 4.3 Cooperating FSMs: Multi-Robot Handshake

In a cooperative robotics scenario, two robots (Robot A and Robot B) synchronize their FSMs through shared events:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RA["Robot A FSM"] -- "REQ_PICK (event)" --> RB["Robot B FSM"]
    RB -- "ACK_PICK (event)" --> RA
    RA -- "PICK_DONE (event)" --> RB
    RB -- "REQ_PLACE (event)" --> RA
    RA -- "ACK_PLACE (event)" --> RB
    SH["Shared Blackboard (Memory or Bus)"] -.-> RA
    SH -.-> RB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each robot's FSM is **decoupled**, communicating only through discrete events.
- The **Shared Blackboard** stores the most recent event and both robots read it.
- This is the foundation of **Behavior-Based Cooperative Robotics** as taught in PERAT862.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define a Finite State Machine (FSM) used as a robot controller. List its six components.**

**Model Answer:**
A Finite State Machine (FSM) used as a robot controller is a mathematical model that describes the robot's behavior as a set of discrete states and transitions between them. Formally, it is a 6-tuple transducer:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, \lambda, q_0)$$

The six components are:
1. $Q$ — finite set of robot states (e.g., IDLE, MOVING, GRIPPING).
2. $\Sigma$ — finite set of input symbols (sensor events).
3. $\Delta$ — finite set of output symbols (actuator commands).
4. $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ — state transition function.
5. $\lambda : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ — output function.
6. $q_0 \in Q$ — initial state of the robot.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between a Moore machine and a Mealy machine with one robotics example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Moore Machine | Mealy Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Output dependency | State only | State + Input |
| Output function | $\lambda(q)$ | $\lambda(q, \sigma)$ |
| Robotics example | A robot in `MOVING` state producing a constant velocity command | A robot in `AVOIDING` state producing `TURN_LEFT` or `TURN_RIGHT` based on obstacle side sensor |

In a **Moore** machine, the output is associated with the state itself, making it stable for the entire state duration. In a **Mealy** machine, the output is associated with the transition, making it more responsive to inputs but possibly less stable.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a complete FSM for a **floor-cleaning mobile robot** with the following behavior:
- The robot has 4 states: `IDLE`, `CLEANING`, `DOCKING`, `ERROR`.
- Inputs: `START`, `BATTERY_LOW`, `DOCK_REACHED`, `OBSTACLE_STUCK`, `RESET`.
- Outputs: `STOP`, `MOVE_FORWARD`, `MOVE_TO_DOCK`, `TURN`, `ALARM`.
- Draw the **state transition table** and verify **determinism** and **completeness**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the FSM in part (a) into an equivalent **Moore machine** and show the modified state diagram. Explain one advantage and one disadvantage of the Moore formulation for this specific robot.

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — FSM Design for Floor-Cleaning Robot

**State Set:**
$$Q = \{q_0 \text{ (IDLE)}, q_1 \text{ (CLEANING)}, q_2 \text{ (DOCKING)}, q_3 \text{ (ERROR)}\}$$

**Input Alphabet:**
$$\Sigma = \{\text{START}, \text{BATTERY\_LOW}, \text{DOCK\_REACHED}, \text{OBSTACLE\_STUCK}, \text{RESET}\}$$

**Output Alphabet:**
$$\Delta = \{\text{STOP}, \text{MOVE\_FORWARD}, \text{MOVE\_TO\_DOCK}, \text{TURN}, \text{ALARM}\}$$

**State Transition Table (Mealy Form):**

| Current State $q$ | Input $\sigma$ | Next State $\delta(q,\sigma)$ | Output $\lambda(q,\sigma)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ (IDLE) | START | $q_1$ | MOVE_FORWARD |
| $q_0$ (IDLE) | OBSTACLE_STUCK | $q_3$ | ALARM |
| $q_0$ (IDLE) | Others | $q_0$ | STOP |
| $q_1$ (CLEANING) | BATTERY_LOW | $q_2$ | MOVE_TO_DOCK |
| $q_1$ (CLEANING) | OBSTACLE_STUCK | $q_1$ | TURN |
| $q_1$ (CLEANING) | START | $q_1$ | MOVE_FORWARD |
| $q_1$ (CLEANING) | Others | $q_1$ | MOVE_FORWARD |
| $q_2$ (DOCKING) | DOCK_REACHED | $q_0$ | STOP |
| $q_2$ (DOCKING) | OBSTACLE_STUCK | $q_3$ | ALARM |
| $q_2$ (DOCKING) | Others | $q_2$ | MOVE_TO_DOCK |
| $q_3$ (ERROR) | RESET | $q_0$ | STOP |
| $q_3$ (ERROR) | Others | $q_3$ | ALARM |

**Determinism Check:**
$$\forall q \in Q, \forall \sigma \in \Sigma : \vert \delta(q, \sigma) \vert = 1$$

Each row of the table has exactly one defined next state for each input. ✓ Deterministic.

**Completeness Check:**
$$\forall q \in Q, \forall \sigma \in \Sigma : \delta(q, \sigma) \text{ is defined}$$

Each row of the table covers all 5 inputs (either explicitly or via the "Others" default). ✓ Complete.

**Reachability Check:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R(q_0) &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\} \\
&= Q
\end{aligned}$$

All states are reachable. ✓

---

### Part (b) — Moore Equivalent

In a **Moore machine**, the output depends only on the state. We split each state by its unique output to create a Moore state for each (state, output) pair.

**Moore State Set:**
$$Q_{Moore} = \{p_0, p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4, p_5, p_6\}$$

Where:
- $p_0 = (q_0, \text{STOP})$
- $p_1 = (q_1, \text{MOVE\_FORWARD})$
- $p_2 = (q_1, \text{TURN})$
- $p_3 = (q_2, \text{MOVE\_TO\_DOCK})$
- $p_4 = (q_3, \text{ALARM})$
- $p_5 = (q_3, \text{STOP})$ (after RESET but before action)

The Moore transition table assigns outputs to states:

| Moore State | Output $\lambda(q)$ |
|---|---|
| $p_0$ | STOP |
| $p_1$ | MOVE_FORWARD |
| $p_2$ | TURN |
| $p_3$ | MOVE_TO_DOCK |
| $p_4$ | ALARM |
| $p_5$ | STOP |

**Advantage of Moore Form:** Outputs are **stable for the entire state duration**, so the actuator command is glitch-free. This is safer for a cleaning robot's motors.

**Disadvantage of Moore Form:** The Moore machine requires **more states** ($\vert Q_{Moore} \vert = 6$) than the Mealy machine ($\vert Q \vert = 4$), increasing memory and complexity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of FSMs in cooperative robotics**. With a neat diagram, describe how two robot arms synchronize their pick-and-place tasks using **event-based FSM coordination**. Define the six-tuple for each robot.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cooperative mobile robot team consists of **two robots**: a *leader* and a *follower*. The leader has states: `IDLE`, `EXPLORING`, `SIGNALING_HELP`, `WAITING`. The follower has states: `IDLE`, `FOLLOWING`, `APPROACHING_LEADER`, `ASSISTING`. Write the **synchronization events** between them and draw the combined state diagram showing the **valid cooperative configurations**.

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — FSMs in Cooperative Robotics

In **cooperative (multi-agent) robotics**, multiple robots must coordinate their actions to achieve a common goal. FSMs are ideal for this because:

1. **Discreteness:** Each robot's behavior is enumerable and communicable in a compact form (state ID).
2. **Verifiability:** Coordination protocols can be formally verified using model checking.
3. **Modularity:** Each robot's FSM is designed independently and integrated via shared events.
4. **Predictability:** Discrete state transitions make real-time guarantees easier to satisfy.

**Six-tuple for Robot A (Arm 1):**
$$M_A = (Q_A, \Sigma_A, \Delta_A, \delta_A, \lambda_A, q_{0,A})$$

**Six-tuple for Robot B (Arm 2):**
$$M_B = (Q_B, \Sigma_B, \Delta_B, \delta_B, \lambda_B, q_{0,B})$$

**Shared Event Channel:**
$$\Sigma_{shared} = \{\text{HANDOVER\_READY}, \text{HANDOVER\_DONE}, \text{RELEASE\_OK}\}$$

**Synchronization Logic:**

| Robot A State | Event Emitted | Robot B State | Event Expected |
|---|---|---|---|
| `HOLDING` (q_A2) | `HANDOVER_READY` | `WAITING` (q_B0) | — |
| `WAITING_ACK` (q_A3) | — | `RECEIVING` (q_B1) | `HANDOVER_READY` |
| `RELEASED` (q_A4) | `HANDOVER_DONE` | `HOLDING` (q_B2) | — |

The two FSMs **handshake** via discrete events on a shared bus. This is the **Petri-Net-Mealy hybrid** style of coordination widely used in modern cooperative cells.

---

### Part (b) — Leader-Follower Synchronization

**Synchronization Events:**
- `LEADER_NEEDS_HELP` (Leader → Follower)
- `FOLLOWER_AT_LEADER` (Follower → Leader)
- `TASK_COMPLETE` (Leader → Follower)
- `RESUME_EXPLORATION` (Leader → Follower)

**Valid Cooperative Configurations:**
A configuration is a pair $(q_L, q_F)$. Valid configurations satisfy the protocol:

$$(IDLE, IDLE), (IDLE, FOLLOWING), (EXPLORING, FOLLOWING),$$
$$(SIGNALING\_HELP, APPROACHING\_LEADER), (WAITING, ASSISTING)$$

**Invalid configurations** (forbidden by the protocol) include:
- $(EXPLORING, IDLE)$ — Leader is moving but follower has not started following.
- $(SIGNALING\_HELP, FOLLOWING)$ — Follower is following instead of approaching.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1a: Leader IDLE
    s1b: Leader EXPLORING
    s1c: Leader SIGNALING_HELP
    s1d: Leader WAITING
    s2a: Follower IDLE
    s2b: Follower FOLLOWING
    s2c: Follower APPROACHING_LEADER
    s2d: Follower ASSISTING
    s1a --> s1b : START_EXPLORE
    s1b --> s1c : LEADER_NEEDS_HELP
    s1c --> s1d : FOLLOWER_AT_LEADER
    s1d --> s1b : TASK_COMPLETE
    s2a --> s2b : LEADER_STARTED
    s2b --> s2c : LEADER_NEEDS_HELP
    s2c --> s2d : FOLLOWER_AT_LEADER
    s2d --> s2b : TASK_COMPLETE
```

**Reading the diagram:** The two FSMs evolve in **lockstep** via shared events. The leader's `SIGNALING_HELP` event triggers the follower's `APPROACHING_LEADER` state. The follower's `FOLLOWER_AT_LEADER` event moves the leader to `WAITING`. This is a **synchronous handshake protocol** guaranteed to be deadlock-free if the event ordering is respected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the state set and the input set** — many students write `Q` when they mean `Σ`. Always label clearly.
> 2. **In Moore machines, the output is attached to the state, NOT to the transition arrow.** Drawing outputs on arrows will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Completeness is mandatory** — if even one $(q, \sigma)$ pair is undefined, the FSM is *not* a valid robot controller and full marks will not be awarded.
> 4. **For cooperative FSMs, you MUST show the synchronization events** explicitly. Stating "they communicate" without listing the events loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **State reachability is often asked** — students forget to prove $R(q_0) = Q$. Always include a reachability check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Finite State Machines as Robot Controllers":**

- An FSM is a **6-tuple** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, \lambda, q_0)$.
- $Q$ = states, $\Sigma$ = inputs, $\Delta$ = outputs, $\delta$ = transition function, $\lambda$ = output function, $q_0$ = initial state.
- **Moore machine:** output depends only on the **current state** — $\lambda(q)$.
- **Mealy machine:** output depends on **state and input** — $\lambda(q, \sigma)$.
- A robot-control FSM must be **deterministic** AND **complete**.
- **Determinism:** every $(q, \sigma)$ pair maps to exactly one next state.
- **Completeness:** every $(q, \sigma)$ pair is defined; undefined pairs are filled with a safe default (e.g., self-loop on `STOP` or transition to `ERROR`).
- The **state transition table** is the engineering artifact used to implement the FSM in code or hardware.
- **Sampling period** $T_s$ is critical: the transition function's WCET must satisfy $t_{WCET} < T_s$.
- **Cooperative robotics** uses FSMs with shared events on a **blackboard** or bus for inter-agent coordination.
- **Reachability** of all states from $q_0$ is a basic verification check.
- **Moore-to-Mealy (and vice versa) conversion** changes the number of states but preserves the input-output behavior.
- FSMs are **superior to neural networks** for safety-critical robotic tasks because they are **formally verifiable** and **deterministic**.
- Common robotic states include: `IDLE`, `MOVING`, `PICKING`, `PLACING`, `DOCKING`, `ERROR`, `EMERGENCY_STOP`.
- The **emergency stop** transition must be present from **every operational state** to a safe `EMERGENCY_STOP` state.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Transitions Based on Robot-Robot Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** State Transitions Based on Robot-Robot Interactions # Module 1 — State Transitions Based on Robot-Robot Interactions ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition** > *State transitions based on robot-robot interactions* refer to the formal mechanism by which a robot in a multi-robot system changes its operational, behavioral, or task state in response to events, signals, observations, or communications originating from one or more peer robots in the same workspace. The transition is governed by a deterministic or stochastic transition function that maps the current state and the interaction input to a successor state. In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme discrete-event systems, the transition is mathematically described as: $$\delta : S \times I \rightarrow S$$ where $S$ is the set of local states of a robot, and $I$ is the set of possible interaction inputs (messages, sensor events, contact forces, etc.). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PERAT862, Module 1)** > The interaction-triggered transition is the *fundamental primitive* of every cooperative behavior — formation keeping, flocking, coverage, search-and-rescue, and swarm aggregation. Without a well-defined state-transition model, no cooperative algorithm can be implemented, simulated, or verified. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Driving Intuition Imagine a fleet of self-driving cars approaching a narrow bridge: - **Car A** is cruising alone → state = `CRUISE`. - It **detects** Car B behind it through a camera (an *interaction*) → it may transition to `FOLLOW`. - It **receives a wireless message** from Car C (another *interaction*) saying "Bridge is clear, proceed" → it may transition to `MERGE`. - It **physically touches** the curb of another lane (a *physical interaction*) → it transitions to `CORRECT`. Each event — sight, signal, contact — is a **robot-robot interaction**, and the rule that maps the event to a new state is the **state transition**. The entire cooperative choreography of the fleet is just a sequence of such transitions, fired in parallel by every vehicle. > [!TIP] > The three principal **interaction modalities** in KTU cooperative robotics are: > 1. **Communication-based** (explicit message passing) > 2. **Perception-based** (exteroceptive sensing of peers) > 3. **Physical / implicit** (contact, occlusion, or observed action) ### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Multi-Robot State Transition Design - **Communication Range** $R_c$ — typically measured in **meters (m)**. - **Detection Probability** $P_d \in [0,1]$ — dimensionless. - **False Alarm Rate** $\lambda_{FA}$ — measured in **events per second**. - **State Transition Latency** $\tau_t$ — measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. - **Update Rate** $f_s$ — measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. > [!NOTE] > The KTU valuation key frequently tests whether a student correctly identifies the *trigger*, the *guard condition*, and the *effect* of a transition. Always list these three explicitly in exam answers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Robot-Robot Interaction-Driven Transition A transition is a **4-tuple**: $$\text{Transition} = (S_{src},\ \text{Trigger},\ \text{Guard},\ S_{dst},\ \text{Effect})$$ - **$S_{src}$** — the source state the robot is currently in. - **Trigger** — the interaction event (message arrival, peer detection, contact). - **Guard** — a Boolean predicate over local and interaction data (range check, time check, ID check). - **$S_{dst}$** — the destination state. - **Effect** — side-effect actions (memory update, broadcast, motor command). ### 2.2 The Transition Function (Deterministic Case) For a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) extended to a multi-robot team: $$\delta_i(s_i,\, m_{ji}) = s_i'$$ Here, $s_i$ is the local state of robot $i$, $m_{ji}$ is the message received from peer robot $j$, and $s_i'$ is the new state of robot $i$. ### 2.3 The Stochastic Transition Function (MDP Case) When the interaction is unreliable, the transition becomes probabilistic. The multi-robot system is then modeled as a **Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP)**: $$P(s_i'\,\vert\,s_i,\, a_i,\, o_{ji}) = \Pr[\text{next state} = s_i' \mid \text{current} = s_i,\ a_i,\ \text{peer observation } o_{ji}]$$ ### 2.4 Consensus-Based Transition (Continuous-Time) For flocking and formation control, the transition dynamics are continuous and governed by consensus: $$\dot{x}_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)$$ where $a_{ij}$ is the adjacency weight, $a_{ij} > 0$ iff robot $j$ is inside robot $i$'s interaction neighborhood $\mathcal{N}_i$. ### 2.5 Interaction Bandwidth and Latency Constraint The latency to receive a peer's message over wireless medium is bounded by: $$\tau_{comm} = \frac{d_{ij}}{c} + \tau_{proc}$$ where $d_{ij}$ is the inter-robot distance, $c$ is the speed of light $\approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8\ m/s}$, and $\tau_{proc}$ is the protocol processing delay. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit / Range | |---|---|---| | $S$ | Set of local states of a robot | discrete set | | $I$ | Set of interaction inputs | discrete set | | $\delta$ | Transition function | mapping | | $R_c$ | Communication / sensing range | **meters (m)** | | $P_d$ | Peer detection probability | $[0, 1]$ | | $\lambda_{FA}$ | False-alarm rate | **events/s** | | $\tau_t$ | Transition latency | **ms** | | $f_s$ | Sensor / comm update rate | **Hz** | | $a_{ij}$ | Consensus adjacency weight | $[0, 1]$ | | $\dot{x}_i$ | Continuous state derivative | **m/s** or **rad/s** | | $\tau_{comm}$ | Wireless round-trip delay | **s** | | $\mathcal{N}_i$ | Interaction neighborhood of robot $i$ | set of indices | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it matters in industry** > State-transition-based coordination is the *backbone* of warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), agricultural swarms (XAG drones), and surgical multi-arm systems (Da Vinci). The reliability of every one of these commercial systems rests on rigorous transition modeling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Consensus Convergence Under Robot-Robot Interaction The KTU board expects the student to be able to derive that under proper interaction, all robots converge to a common state value. **Step 1 — Define the team objective.** We have $N$ robots, each with a state $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
