### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between centralized and decentralized multi-robot systems in terms of fault tolerance and communication overhead.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a **centralized** system, a single leader robot plans and controls all other agents. Communication overhead grows as **O(N²)** because every agent must report to and receive commands from the leader. Fault tolerance is **0%** because leader failure halts the mission.

In a **decentralized** system, there is no leader. Each robot decides using local sensing and limited peer communication, with overhead of **O(N·k)** where $k$ is the neighbor count. Fault tolerance is **up to (N-1)/N × 100%**, since loss of any agent does not collapse the system.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**List and briefly explain any three current design approaches used in co-operative robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Behavior-Based Approach:** Decomposes control into parallel reactive behaviors such as obstacle avoidance, goal seeking, and flocking; outputs are fused via subsumption or weighted averaging.
2. **Market-Based Approach:** Uses auction mechanisms where robots bid for tasks; the lowest-cost bidder wins, enabling self-organized task allocation.
3. **Swarm Approach:** Employs many simple robots following 2–3 local rules (separation, alignment, cohesion) to produce emergent collective behavior.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Hierarchical Approach** to multi-robot coordination. Discuss its three layers and the direction of information flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A warehouse uses **N = 20** robots. Compare the communication overhead of a centralized architecture versus a decentralized architecture where each robot communicates with **k = 4** neighbors. Calculate the percentage reduction in messages. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The **Hierarchical Approach** organizes control into **three distinct abstraction layers**, mirroring real-world organizational structures.

**Layer 1 — Strategic Layer (Top):**
- Defines long-term mission goals
- Performs global resource estimation
- Generates high-level objectives (e.g., "deliver 500 parcels in 8 hours")

**Layer 2 — Tactical Layer (Middle):**
- Decomposes strategic goals into tasks
- Allocates tasks to robot sub-teams
- Performs global path planning and scheduling

**Layer 3 — Execution Layer (Bottom):**
- Handles low-level motor control
- Manages sensor acquisition
- Executes communication handshakes

**Information Flow:**

- **Top-down:** Commands cascade from Strategic → Tactical → Execution → Robots
- **Bottom-up:** Status and sensor data flow from Robots → Execution → Tactical → Strategic

**Key Advantage:** Each layer abstracts complexity for the layer above, simplifying software engineering and verification.

**Key Limitation:** Communication latency between layers can cause slow response in dynamic environments.

*(Reference block diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** N = 20, k = 4

**Step 1 — Centralized message count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{central}^{total} \;&= \; N(N-1) \\
&= \; 20 \times 19 \\
&= \; 380 \; \text{messages per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decentralized message count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{decent}^{total} \;&= \; N \cdot k \\
&= \; 20 \times 4 \\
&= \; 80 \; \text{messages per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute reduction:**

$$
\Delta M \;=\; 380 - 80 \;=\; 300 \; \text{messages}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{reduction} \;&= \; \frac{300}{380} \times 100\% \\
&= \; 78.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The decentralized approach reduces communication overhead by **78.95%** for N = 20, k = 4. The warehouse operator benefits from dramatically reduced bandwidth requirements, lower power consumption on each robot's radio, and improved scalability as more units are added.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Market-Based Approach** for multi-robot task allocation. Describe the auction protocol with a sequence diagram and state two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, explain the **Behavior-Based Approach**. A mobile robot has three behaviors with weights $w_1 = 0.6$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.1$ and outputs $b_1 = -2$, $b_2 = +1$, $b_3 = +3$. Compute the final actuator command. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The **Market-Based Approach** treats task allocation as an economic auction. Robots are *self-interested agents* that bid for tasks based on their internal cost function (e.g., distance, energy, time).

**Auction Protocol Steps:**
1. **Auctioneer** (could be one robot or external server) announces a task T with a reward R.
2. All robots in the team compute their bid cost $c_i$.
3. Robots submit bids to the auctioneer.
4. Auctioneer selects the **lowest bidder** and awards the task.
5. Winning robot executes the task and reports completion.

**Common Variants:**
- **Combinatorial Auctions:** Bid on bundles of tasks.
- **Sequential Auctions:** One task at a time.
- **Continuous Auctions:** Tasks re-auctioned dynamically.

**Real-World Applications (any two for 2 marks):**
1. **Amazon Kiva Robots** in warehouses — robots bid for shelf transport tasks.
2. **DARPA Swarm Challenges** — UAVs auction reconnaissance targets.

*(Reference sequence diagram from Section 4.3.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

The **Behavior-Based Approach** decomposes robot control into **parallel reactive modules** (behaviors). Each behavior maps sensor input directly to actuator output. The final command is the **weighted fusion** of all active behaviors:

$$
u_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_{ij} \cdot b_{ij}, \quad \text{with} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_{ij} = 1
$$

**Given:**
- $w_1 = 0.6$, $b_1 = -2$ (avoid-obstacle — turn left)
- $w_2 = 0.3$, $b_2 = +1$ (go-to-goal — move forward)
- $w_3 = 0.1$, $b_3 = +3$ (explore — move forward strongly)

**Step 1 — Verify weight normalization:**

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 \;=\; 0.6 + 0.3 + 0.1 \;=\; 1.0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_i \;&= \; (0.6)(-2) + (0.3)(+1) + (0.1)(+3) \\
&= \; -1.2 + 0.3 + 0.3 \\
&= \; -0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since $u_i = -0.6$ is **negative**, the dominant behavior is *avoid-obstacle*. The robot will execute a left-turn command with magnitude 0.6 (out of a normalized range of $-1$ to $+1$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Module 2 questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up *centralized vs decentralized* with *hierarchical*.** Hierarchical is about *layers of abstraction*, not about centralization. Many students confuse these — examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals.** Always write `messages per cycle`, `seconds`, `%`, etc. Missing units = **0.5 to 1 mark deducted**.
> 3. **Not verifying $\sum w_{ij} = 1$** in behavior-based problems. Examiners award partial marks only if this normalization is checked.
> 4. **Wrong order of complexity.** Writing $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for centralized when it is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ is a frequent blunder — practice the derivation until it is automatic.
> 5. **Sketching without labels.** When asked for a block diagram, every block MUST be labeled; arrows MUST indicate direction of data or command flow.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Centralized:** Single leader, $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ comms, 0% fault tolerance, global optimum.
- **Decentralized:** No leader, $\mathcal{O}(Nk)$ comms, up to (N-1)/N × 100% fault tolerance, local optimum.
- **Hierarchical:** Three layers (Strategic / Tactical / Execution), abstraction reduces complexity, vertical command flow.
- **Behavior-Based:** Parallel reactive behaviors, weighted fusion $u_i = \sum w_{ij} b_{ij}$, subsumption arbitration.
- **Market-Based:** Auction protocol, lowest bid wins, used in Amazon warehouses.
- **Blackboard:** Shared memory pool, any agent can read/write, needs conflict resolution.
- **Swarm:** Large N (≥ 50), local rules (separation / alignment / cohesion), emergent intelligence.
- **Communication overhead formula:** Centralized $N(N-1)$, Decentralized $Nk$.
- **Fault tolerance formula:** Centralized 0%, Decentralized $(N-1)/N \times 100\%$.
- **Always show weight normalization check** in behavior-based numerics.
- **Always include units** in every numerical answer.
- **Block diagrams must have labeled blocks and directional arrows.**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision tip:** Memorize the Analytical Comparison Matrix (Section 3.4) — it answers 80% of "compare and contrast" Part B questions in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottom-Up Design Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bottom-Up Design Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Co-operative Robotics**, a **Bottom-Up Design Approach** is a distributed architectural paradigm in which the global mission or collective behavior of a multi-robot system (MRS) is **not explicitly programmed** at the system level. Instead, it **emerges** from the local interaction of many simple, autonomous, and often homogeneous robotic agents, each following a small set of reactive rules based on local sensory information and communication with immediate neighbors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *Bottom-Up Design* is an agent-centric methodology where the designer specifies **local behaviors, control laws, and inter-agent interaction protocols** at the *individual robot level*, and the desired **global group behavior emerges** as a macroscopic consequence of these microscopic rules — analogous to how an ant colony forages or a flock of birds navigates.

The governing equation of the philosophy can be symbolically expressed as:

$$
\mathcal{G} \;=\; \mathcal{F}\bigl(\,B_1,\,B_2,\,\dots,\,B_n,\,I_{local},\,\Pi_{comm}\,\bigr)
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{G}$ = **Global emergent behavior** of the swarm
- $B_i$ = **Local behavior** of the $i$-th agent
- $I_{local}$ = **Local sensory input** (limited range)
- $\Pi_{comm}$ = **Inter-agent communication / interaction protocol**
- $\mathcal{F}$ = The (often non-linear and stochastic) **emergence function**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bird Flock

Imagine you are watching a flock of **starlings** perform a mesmerizing aerial ballet at sunset. No single bird is the "leader" with a global map. No central choreographer issues "turn left at coordinate $(x, y)$." Each bird obeys only three simple rules based on what its **seven nearest neighbors** are doing:

| Rule | Local Question Asked | Bird's Response |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Separation** | "Am I too close to anyone?" | Steer gently away |
| **Alignment** | "Which way are my neighbors heading?" | Match their average heading |
| **Cohesion** | "Where is the local center of my group?" | Steer toward it |

> [!NOTE]
> Out of three ridiculously simple rules per bird, you get the **breathtaking, fluid murmuration** seen in nature. **That is Bottom-Up Design in one sentence.** The complexity is *not* in the brain of the bird; it is in the **interaction field** between the birds.

In a robotic swarm, we replace the bird's brain with a microcontroller, and the visual perception with a **LiDAR, camera, or IR sensor**, but the design philosophy is identical: *design the local rule, harvest the global behavior.*

## 1.3 Why Bottom-Up? The KTU Justification

| Property | Top-Down (Centralized) | Bottom-Up (Distributed) |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Scalability** | Poor — single point of control | Excellent — $O(n)$ agents easily added |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Fragile — master failure collapses system | Robust — loss of a few agents negligible |
| **Communication** | Heavy global bandwidth | Sparse, local-only |
| **Computational Load** | Concentrated on central planner | Distributed across agents |
| **Predictability** | High (deterministic) | Lower (stochastic, emergent) |
| **Engineering Cost** | High upfront | Lower per agent, tunable in aggregate |
| **Best Suited For** | Small, structured teams | Large swarms, unknown environments |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, the examiner expects students to clearly distinguish **Behavior-Based**, **Swarm-Intelligence**, and **Reactive** approaches as the *three principal sub-families* of Bottom-Up Design. The acronym **B-S-R** is a useful mnemonic.

## 1.4 The Three Sub-Families of Bottom-Up Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bottom-Up Design Approaches] --> B[1. Behavior-Based Robotics]
    A --> C[2. Swarm Intelligence]
    A --> D[3. Reactive Control]

    B --> B1[Subsumption Architecture - Brooks 1986]
    B --> B2[Motor Schemas - Arkin 1987]

    C --> C1[Stigmergy - Grassé 1959]
    C --> C2[Pheromone Models - ACO]
    C --> C3[Probabilistic Finit]]

    D --> D1[Reactive Layer Only]
    D --> D2[Hybrid Deliberative-Reactive]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Pillars of Bottom-Up Design

A bottom-up co-operative robotic system is engineered by stacking **five conceptual pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — The Agent (The Building Brick)
Every bottom-up system starts with defining a single agent. A KTU-compliant agent specification must include:
1. **Sensing capability** — what can it perceive? (range, modality)
2. **Actuation capability** — what can it do? (locomotion, manipulation)
3. **Onboard computation** — microcontroller, FPGA, edge-AI chip
4. **Communication interface** — Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, IR, acoustic
5. **Local state variables** — pose, velocity, internal timer, energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The agent is intentionally kept **computationally weak** and **sensing-limited**. The brilliance of bottom-up design is that *aggregate intelligence overcomes individual stupidity.*

### Pillar 2 — The Local Behavior Set
A **behavior** $B_i$ is a mapping from sensed inputs to actuator commands:

$$
B_i : S_{local} \longrightarrow A_{local}
$$

Typical behaviors used in cooperative robotics:

- **Avoid-obstacle** — repulsive potential from sensed obstacles
- **Go-to-goal** — attractive potential to a target
- **Follow-leader** — track a designated agent
- **Aggregate** — cluster around neighbors
- **Disperse** — spread out to cover area
- **Forage** — search, find, retrieve pattern

### Pillar 3 — The Coordination Mechanism
How do individual behaviors combine into a coherent agent action? The three classical mechanisms are:

| Mechanism | Formula Type | Inventor |
|-----------|--------------|----------|
| **Subsumption** | Hard arbitration, layered inhibition | Rodney Brooks (1986) |
| **Motor Schemas** | Weighted vector summation | Ronald Arkin (1987) |
| **Probabilistic Finite State Machine (PFSM)** | Stochastic transitions between states | Krieger & Billeter (2000) |

### Pillar 4 — The Interaction Protocol
Defines how agents exchange information. Three classes:
- **Direct** — explicit message passing (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth)
- **Indirect / Stigmergic** — environment-mediated (pheromone trails, RFID tags on floor)
- **Implicit** — passive observation of peers' positions via onboard sensors

### Pillar 5 — The Emergent Global Behavior
The *desired* macroscopic outcome — flocking, foraging, coverage, sorting, construction. The designer's job is to **reverse-engineer the local rules** that produce this global behavior.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — Reynolds' Boids Model

Craig Reynolds (1987) formulated the canonical **boids** model, which is the textbook example of bottom-up design. Each agent computes three steering vectors per time step:

### Vector 1 — Separation

$$
\vec{v}_{sep,i} \;=\; -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\lVert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \rVert^2}
$$

where $\mathcal{N}_i$ = set of neighbors within radius $r_{sep}$.

### Vector 2 — Alignment

$$
\vec{v}_{align,i} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lVert \mathcal{N}_i \rVert}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i
$$

### Vector 3 — Cohesion

$$
\vec{v}_{coh,i} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lVert \mathcal{N}_i \rVert}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i
$$

### Final Steering Command

$$
\vec{v}_{total,i} \;=\; w_1 \vec{v}_{sep,i} + w_2 \vec{v}_{align,i} + w_3 \vec{v}_{coh,i}
$$

where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are non-negative scalar weights (typically tuned empirically).

## 2.3 The Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)

Brooks' radical insight was to **abandon world models** entirely. He proposed organizing control as **layers of finite-state machines (augmented finite-state machines, AFSMs)** arranged in a strict priority order. Higher layers can *suppress* (inhibit) or *replace* (override) the outputs of lower layers.

**Layer Hierarchy (from lowest to highest priority):**

| Layer | Behavior | Purpose |
|-------|----------|---------|
| 0 | `avoid` | Don't hit things |
| 1 | `wander` | Move around aimlessly |
| 2 | `follow` | Track a moving object |
| 3 | `build_map` | Construct a world model |
| 4 | `notice_change` | Detect novelty |
| 5 | `reason` | Plan, deliberate |
| 6 | `plan_motion` | Long-horizon path planning |

The **suppression** and **inhibition** wires are the key innovation:

$$
\text{Layer}_k \, \text{output} \;=\; \begin{cases} O_k & \text{if no higher layer is active} \\ O_j & \text{if layer } j > k \text{ suppresses it} \end{cases}
$$

## 2.4 Motor Schemas (Arkin, 1987)

Arkin's **schema theory** proposes that each behavior produces a **motor vector** in 2D space, and the final actuator command is the **normalized vector sum** of all active schemas:

$$
\vec{V}_{final} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} \gamma_k \, \vec{V}_k}{\left\Vert \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} \gamma_k \, \vec{V}_k \right\Vert}
$$

where $\gamma_k \in [0, 1]$ is the *gain* (or perceptual attention weight) of schema $k$.

Unlike subsumption, schemas **cooperate** rather than compete — they are additive, producing smooth emergent trajectories.

## 2.5 Probabilistic Finite State Machine (PFSM) for Swarm Control

Krieger & Billeter's PFSM framework models each agent as a stochastic state machine. The probability of transitioning from state $S_i$ to $S_j$ is $p_{ij}$, with the constraint:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{M} p_{ij} \;=\; 1 \quad \forall \, i \in \{1, 2, \dots, M\}
$$

PFSMs are extremely efficient for **foraging, sorting, and clustering** tasks because they allow asynchronous, decentralized decision-making.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{F}(B_i, I_{local}, \Pi_{comm})$ | Emergent global behavior | Conceptual basis |
| 2 | $\vec{v}_{sep,i} = -\sum_{j} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\lVert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \rVert^2}$ | Separation vector | Boids collision avoidance |
| 3 | $\vec{v}_{align,i} = \bar{\vec{v}}_{neighbors} - \vec{v}_i$ | Velocity matching | Boids alignment |
| 4 | $\vec{v}_{coh,i} = \bar{\vec{r}}_{neighbors} - \vec{r}_i$ | Centering vector | Boids flocking |
| 5 | $\vec{v}_{total,i} = \sum_k w_k \vec{v}_k$ | Weighted blending | All weighted-vector schemes |
| 6 | $L_i(t) = L_0 - \alpha \cdot t$ | Pheromone evaporation | Ant Colony Optimization |
| 7 | $P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum [\tau]^\alpha [\eta]^\beta}$ | ACO transition prob. | Path planning swarms |
| 8 | $\sum_j p_{ij} = 1$ | PFSM stochastic constraint | Stochastic swarm control |
| 9 | $\vec{V}_{final} = \frac{\sum \gamma_k \vec{V}_k}{\Vert \cdot \Vert}$ | Motor schema fusion | Arkin-style architectures |
| 10 | $r_{comm} \ll r_{arena}$ | Locality constraint | Scalability guarantee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Note:** Bottom-up equations (boids, ACO, PFSM) are deployed in real-world applications including **warehouse robotics (Kiva/Amazon)**, **agricultural weeding swarms**, **Mars rover formation flying**, and **disaster-response rubble exploration**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Emergence of Flocking from Three Local Rules

We now derive **why** three purely local boids rules produce stable flocking. This is a standard KTU long-answer derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Agent's State Vector

Each agent $i$ at time $t$ has:
- Position $\vec{r}_i(t) \in \mathbb{R}^2$
- Velocity $\vec{v}_i(t) \in \mathbb{R}^2$
- Acceleration bound $\lVert \vec{a}_i \rVert \leq a_{max}$

### Step 2 — Define the Neighborhood

Agent $j$ is a neighbor of $i$ if it lies within a disk of radius $R_{sense}$:

$$
\mathcal{N}_i(t) \;=\; \left\{j \,:\, j \neq i \text{ and } \lVert \vec{r}_j(t) - \vec{r}_i(t) \rVert \leq R_{sense}\right\}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Three Component Vectors

Using the formulas from Section 2.2:

$$
\vec{v}_{sep,i}(t) = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i(t) - \vec{r}_j(t)}{\lVert \vec{r}_i(t) - \vec{r}_j(t) \rVert^2}
$$

$$
\vec{v}_{align,i}(t) = \frac{1}{\lVert \mathcal{N}_i \rVert}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j(t) - \vec{v}_i(t)
$$

$$
\vec{v}_{coh,i}(t) = \frac{1}{\lVert \mathcal{N}_i \rVert}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{r}_j(t) - \vec{r}_i(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Linearly Combine the Vectors

$$
\vec{v}_{desired,i}(t) = w_1 \vec{v}_{sep,i}(t) + w_2 \vec{v}_{align,i}(t) + w_3 \vec{v}_{coh,i}(t)
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Saturation Constraint

Real robots have a maximum velocity $v_{max}$. We must clip:

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = \frac{\vec{v}_{desired,i}(t)}{\max\left(1, \frac{\lVert \vec{v}_{desired,i}(t) \rVert}{v_{max}}\right)}
$$

### Step 6 — Update the Position (Discrete-Time Dynamics)

$$
\vec{r}_i(t+1) = \vec{r}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1) \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 7 — Equilibrium Analysis (Why Flocking is Stable)

At flock equilibrium, **all agents move with the same velocity** $\vec{v}^\star$. Set $\vec{v}_{align,i} = \vec{0}$:

$$
\vec{v}_i = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j
$$

This is satisfied when all $\vec{v}_i$ are equal, proving equilibrium. Similarly, at centroid equilibrium:

$$
\vec{v}_{coh,i} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i = \vec{0}
$$

which is satisfied when all $\vec{r}_i$ are equal — i.e., agents are **co-located**.

The **separation term** prevents this collapse, so the steady state is a **bounded cluster moving coherently**, which is exactly what we observe in nature.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Local Rule vs. Global Behavior Mapping

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU table** that examiners love to test:

| Local Rule Combination | Resulting Global Behavior | Real-World Analogue |
|------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Separation only | Dispersion, uniform coverage | Gas molecules spreading |
| Alignment only | Parallel motion lanes | Highway traffic |
| Cohesion only | Aggregation, clustering | Droplet formation |
| Sep + Align | Swarm with internal order | Fish schools |
| Sep + Coh | Static clusters | Cell colonies |
| Align + Coh | Loose flocks | Bird murmurations |
| **Sep + Align + Coh** | **Stable flocking** | **Starlings, sardines** |
| Go-to-goal + Avoid | Reactive navigation | Single mobile robot |
| Go-to-goal + Avoid + Follow | Leader-follower convoy | Truck platoons |
| Wander + Aggregate | Self-organized clustering | Slime mold behavior |
| Forage + Return + Pheromone | Adaptive path optimization | Ant colony foraging |

## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical Example — Tuning Boids Weights

**Problem (Typical 7-Mark Question):** *Three agents A, B, C are initially at positions $\vec{r}_A = (0,0)$, $\vec{r}_B = (2,0)$, $\vec{r}_C = (4,0)$ with velocities $\vec{v}_A = (1,0)$, $\vec{v}_B = (1,0)$, $\vec{v}_C = (1,0)$. Compute the new velocity of agent A using $w_1 = 2$, $w_2 = 1$, $w_3 = 1$, with sensing radius $R_{sense} = 5$.*

**Solution — Step 1: Identify A's neighbors.** Both B and C are within $R_{sense}=5$, so $\mathcal{N}_A = \{B, C\}$.

**Step 2: Compute Separation Vector:**

$$
\vec{v}_{sep,A} = -\left[\frac{\vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_B}{\lVert \vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_B \rVert^2} + \frac{\vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_C}{\lVert \vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_C \rVert^2}\right]
$$

Calculate denominators:
- $\lVert \vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_B \rVert^2 = 2^2 = 4$
- $\lVert \vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_C \rVert^2 = 4^2 = 16$

Therefore:

$$
\vec{v}_{sep,A} = -\left[\frac{(-2, 0)}{4} + \frac{(-4, 0)}{16}\right] = -\left[(-0.5, 0) + (-0.25, 0)\right] = (0.75, 0)
$$

**Step 3: Compute Alignment Vector:**

$$
\vec{v}_{align,A} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\vec{v}_B + \vec{v}_C\right] - \vec{v}_A = \frac{1}{2}\left[(1,0)+(1,0)\right] - (1,0) = (0,0)
$$

Agents are already aligned, so $\vec{v}_{align,A} = (0,0)$.

**Step 4: Compute Cohesion Vector:**

$$
\vec{v}_{coh,A} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\vec{r}_B + \vec{r}_C\right] - \vec{r}_A = \frac{1}{2}\left[(2,0)+(4,0)\right] - (0,0) = (3, 0)
$$

**Step 5: Combine with Weights:**

$$
\vec{v}_{desired,A} = w_1 \vec{v}_{sep,A} + w_2 \vec{v}_{align,A} + w_3 \vec{v}_{coh,A}
$$

$$
\vec{v}_{desired,A} = 2(0.75, 0) + 1(0, 0) + 1(3, 0) = (1.5 + 0 + 3, \; 0) = (4.5, 0)
$$

**Step 6: Apply Saturation** (assume $v_{max} = 3$):

$$
\lVert \vec{v}_{desired,A} \rVert = 4.5 > v_{max} = 3
$$

$$
\vec{v}_A^{new} = \frac{(4.5, 0)}{4.5/3} = \frac{(4.5, 0)}{1.5} = (3, 0)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\vec{v}_A^{new} = (3, 0)$ m/s. Agent A now moves at maximum speed directly away from the dense cluster — the *separation* term dominates, as the weights prescribed.

This example is a **gold standard KTU long-answer** template. Memorize the structure.

## 3.4 Convergence Proof Sketch — PFSM to Stable Distribution

For a Probabilistic Finite State Machine, the **steady-state probability** $\pi_j$ of being in state $j$ satisfies the global balance equation:

$$
\pi_j = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \pi_i \, p_{ij}
$$

This can be written in matrix form:

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^T = \boldsymbol{\pi}^T \mathbf{P}
$$

where $\mathbf{P}$ is the $M \times M$ transition matrix. The unique stationary distribution exists if the Markov chain is **irreducible** and **aperiodic** (Perron-Frobenius theorem). For bottom-up swarm design, we must **guarantee** these conditions to ensure predictable global behavior.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Top-Down Centralized]
        CP[Central Planner] --> A1[Agent 1]
        CP --> A2[Agent 2]
        CP --> A3[Agent 3]
        CP --> A4[Agent 4]
    end
    style CP fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000
    style A3 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000
    style A4 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000

    subgraph BU[Bottom-Up Distributed]
        L1[Agent 1] <--> L2[Agent 2]
        L2 <--> L3[Agent 3]
        L3 <--> L4[Agent 4]
        L4 <--> L1
        L1 <--> L3
    end

    style L2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L4 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** In Top-Down, the central planner is a *single point of failure*; in Bottom-Up, every agent is both an executor and a partial planner, and the swarm is fault-tolerant.

## 4.2 Subsumption Architecture — Layered Control (Brooks)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L6[Layer 6 - Plan Motion] --> SUP6[Suppression Wires]
    L5[Layer 5 - Reason] --> SUP5[Suppression Wires]
    L4[Layer 4 - Notice Change] --> SUP4[Suppression Wires]
    L3[Layer 3 - Build Map] --> SUP3[Suppression Wires]
    L2[Layer 2 - Follow] --> SUP2[Suppression Wires]
    L1[Layer 1 - Wander] --> SUP1[Suppression Wires]
    L0[Layer 0 - Avoid Obstacle] --> ACT[Actuators]

    SUP6 --> ACT
    SUP5 --> ACT
    SUP4 --> ACT
    SUP3 --> ACT
    SUP2 --> ACT
    SUP1 --> ACT

    style L6 fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style L5 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style L4 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style L3 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style L2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style L1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000
    style L0 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style ACT fill:#70ad47,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** Higher layers (top) can *inhibit* or *override* the outputs of lower layers (bottom). Layer 0 (avoid) is the only layer that can *never* be overridden — a robot must always avoid collisions.

## 4.3 Motor Schema Fusion — Vector Summation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Schema: Avoid Obstacle] -->|V1, gamma1| SUM
    S2[Schema: Move to Goal] -->|V2, gamma2| SUM
    S3[Schema: Follow Leader] -->|V3, gamma3| SUM
    S4[Schema: Stay in Formation] -->|V4, gamma4| SUM
    SUM[Weighted Vector Sum] --> NORM[Normalize to Unit Length]
    NORM --> CMD[Final Velocity Command]
    CMD --> ACT[Robot Actuators]

    style S1 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000
    style S2 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000
    style S3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000
    style S4 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000

    style NORM fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#000
    style CMD fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ACT fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Boids Flocking — Neighborhood Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOCAL_SENSE[Local Sensing Disk of Agent i]
        CENTER[Agent i at origin]
        N1[Neighbor j1]
        N2[Neighbor j2]
        N3[Neighbor j3]
        N4[Neighbor j4]
        R[R-sense radius]
    end

    CENTER -.->|separation repulsion| N1
    CENTER -.->|cohesion attraction| N2
    CENTER -.->|alignment matching| N3
    CENTER -.->|separation repulsion| N4

    style CENTER fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style N1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style N2 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff,stroke:#000

```

**Reading Guide:** Agent $i$ only "sees" agents inside the dashed circle of radius $R_{sense}$. Agents outside are completely ignored — this **locality** is the scalability secret.

## 4.5 Stigmergy — Indirect Communication via Environment

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A1 as Agent 1
    participant ENV as Environment / Pheromone Field
    participant A2 as Agent 2
    participant A3 as Agent 3

    A1->>ENV: Deposit pheromone tau at node A
    A1->>A1: Return to nest
    A2->>ENV: Sense tau at node A
    A2->>A2: Probability of choosing A increases
    A2->>ENV: Reinforce pheromone at A
    A3->>ENV: Sense stronger tau at A
    A3->>A3: Path A becomes dominant

    Note over A1,A3: NO direct agent-to-agent message ever sent!
```

**Engineering Insight:** Stigmergy allows coordination **without wireless communication**, which is critical in environments with severe RF jamming (e.g., disaster zones, mine tunnels, or extraterrestrial surfaces).

## 4.6 Emergence — Local Rules to Global Behavior Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1. Designer Specifies Local Rules] --> B[2. Program into Each Agent]
    B --> C[3. Agents Sense Locally]
    C --> D[4. Agents Act Locally]
    D --> E[5. Local Interactions Compound]
    E --> F[6. Global Pattern Emerges]
    F --> G[7. Designer Verifies vs Goal]
    G -->|Mismatch| A
    G -->|Match| H[8. Deployment]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define bottom-up design approach in co-operative robotics. Mention any two advantages over top-down design.**

**Model Answer:**
A **bottom-up design approach** in co-operative robotics is a distributed methodology in which the desired global behavior of a multi-robot system emerges from local interactions between autonomous agents following simple, predefined rules, rather than being explicitly commanded by a central planner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Advantages (any two for full marks):**
> 1. **Scalability** — adding more agents does not require redesigning the control architecture.
> 2. **Fault tolerance** — failure of a few agents does not collapse the entire mission; the swarm self-heals.
> 3. **Lower communication overhead** — only local, neighbor-level messages are needed.
> 4. **Robustness to dynamic environments** — no global map to keep updated.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is stigmergy? How does it enable indirect coordination in a robotic swarm?**

**Model Answer:**
**Stigmergy** is a mechanism of indirect coordination in which agents communicate by **modifying the shared environment**, which in turn influences the behavior of other agents. The term was coined by French biologist Pierre-Paul Grassé (1959) to describe termite mound construction.

In a robotic swarm, stigmergy is typically implemented using **virtual pheromones** — digital markers (e.g., RFID tags, floor-projected markers, or shared memory maps) that are deposited, sensed, and gradually evaporated over time. Robots deposit pheromone intensity $\tau_{ij}$ on path $(i,j)$ after traversing it, and other robots preferentially follow paths with higher $\tau$ values:

$$
P_{ij} = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha \, [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{k} [\tau_{ik}]^\alpha \, [\eta_{ik}]^\beta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Stigmergy allows coordination **without explicit agent-to-agent communication**, making it ideal for RF-denied environments and ultra-low-power swarm deployments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions have an **internal choice**. The two alternatives must be attempted separately if the question is split. Below, we provide Question A and Question B as full **14-mark** model papers with sub-parts of 7+7.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Q.A (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Reynolds Boids model in detail. Derive the three steering vectors and discuss how their weighted combination produces emergent flocking behavior.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Reynolds Boids model (1987) is the canonical example of bottom-up design. Each agent (boid) computes three local steering vectors — **separation, alignment, cohesion** — based on its sensed neighbors, and the linear combination of these vectors produces emergent flocking.

**Three Steering Vectors (3 Marks):**

1. **Separation Vector** (collision avoidance):
$$
\vec{v}_{sep,i} = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\lVert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \rVert^2}
$$
This produces a repulsive force that scales as $1/r^2$, i.e., stronger for closer neighbors.

2. **Alignment Vector** (velocity matching):
$$
\vec{v}_{align,i} = \frac{1}{\lVert \mathcal{N}_i \rVert}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i
$$
This steers the agent to match the average velocity of its neighbors.

3. **Cohesion Vector** (centroid seeking):
$$
\vec{v}_{coh,i} = \frac{1}{\lVert \mathcal{N}_i \rVert}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i
$$
This steers the agent toward the centroid of its neighbors.

**Weighted Combination (2 Marks):**
$$
\vec{v}_{desired,i} = w_1 \vec{v}_{sep,i} + w_2 \vec{v}_{align,i} + w_3 \vec{v}_{coh,i}
$$
After saturation against $v_{max}$, the position is updated as $\vec{r}_i(t+1) = \vec{r}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)\Delta t$.

**Emergent Flocking Discussion (1 Mark):**
At equilibrium, the *alignment* term forces all velocities to be equal, the *cohesion* term forces clustering, and the *separation* term prevents collapse. The net result is a bounded, moving cluster — exactly the **flocking** behavior observed in bird murmurations. No leader exists; the global order arises purely from local rules.

#### Q.A (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast the Subsumption Architecture (Brooks) and Motor Schema Theory (Arkin). Use a neat block diagram and a comparison table.**

**Model Solution:**

**Subsumption Architecture (3 Marks):**
Brooks (1986) proposed organizing control as a hierarchy of **Augmented Finite State Machines (AFSMs)**, with higher layers able to *inhibit* or *suppress* the outputs of lower layers. Each layer is a complete behavior in itself. There is **no world model**, and **no central planner** — the system is purely reactive.

| Feature | Subsumption | Motor Schemas |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Coordination | Hard arbitration (winner takes all) | Soft vector summation (cooperation) |
| World Model | None | Optional, often absent |
| Inventor / Year | Brooks, 1986 | Arkin, 1987 |
| Combination Rule | Suppression / Inhibition | $\vec{V}_{final} = \frac{\sum \gamma_k \vec{V}_k}{\Vert \cdot \Vert}$ |
| Trajectory Smoothness | Piecewise, jerky | Smooth, continuous |
| Layer Count | Fixed, predefined | Open-ended, dynamic |
| Tuning | Topology of wires | Continuous gain values $\gamma_k$ |
| Best For | Discrete tasks (forage, follow) | Continuous tasks (navigation, formation) |

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 and 4.3 diagrams.)*

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Subsumption is more **computationally efficient** and easier to implement on tiny microcontrollers, but produces **discontinuous** behavior. Motor Schemas yield **smoother trajectories** suitable for navigation, but require vector math at every control cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** subsumption's *suppression* with *inhibition* — they have different operational meanings.
> - **Do NOT state** that subsumption has a world model — Brooks explicitly **rejected** world models. Saying so will cost you 2 marks.
> - In the comparison table, **avoid vague adjectives** like "better" — use precise engineering terms (computational complexity, trajectory smoothness, sensor dependency).

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

#### Q.B (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the Probabilistic Finite State Machine (PFSM) approach to bottom-up swarm control. How is it used to model foraging behavior in a co-operative robotic system?**

**Model Solution:**

**PFSM Definition (2 Marks):**
A **Probabilistic Finite State Machine** is a stochastic state machine used to model agent behavior in a swarm. Each agent occupies one of $M$ discrete states at any time, and transitions between states are governed by probabilities $p_{ij}$. The transition matrix $\mathbf{P}$ must be row-stochastic:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{M} p_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, i
$$

The **steady-state distribution** $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ satisfies $\boldsymbol{\pi}^T \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}^T$, guaranteeing long-term predictability.

**Foraging Application (3 Marks):**
Foraging is the canonical PFSM task. A typical 4-state foraging PFSM is:

```
[Search]  --p12-->  [Find]  --p23-->  [Pickup]  --p34-->  [Return]  --p41-->  [Search]
   ^                                                                              |
   |______________________________________________________________________________|
                                  p42 (failure)
```

- **Search** — random walk in arena looking for items
- **Find** — item detected, transition with probability $p_{12}$
- **Pickup** — gripper activated, $p_{23}$
- **Return** — homing to base, $p_{34}$
- On **failure** (e.g., gripper miss), agent reverts to *Search* with probability $p_{42}$

**Why PFSM is Powerful (2 Marks):**
- **Decentralized** — no agent knows the global state
- **Scalable** — adding more agents linearly increases throughput
- **Robust** — agent stuck in a state only wastes its own cycles
- **Tunable** — designer adjusts $p_{ij}$ values to optimize throughput

**Mathematical Metric — Foraging Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{forage} = \frac{N_{items,collected}}{N_{agents} \times T_{total}}
$$

The optimal $p_{ij}$ values are found empirically or via reinforcement learning.

#### Q.B (b) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the design pipeline for a bottom-up swarm system. Explain how the designer reverse-engineers the local rules to obtain a desired global behavior.**

**Model Solution:**

**The Design Pipeline (4 Marks):**
The bottom-up design pipeline consists of seven stages (refer to Section 4.6 diagram):

1. **Goal Specification** — Define the desired global behavior (e.g., "cover 95% of the arena in 10 minutes").
2. **Agent Specification** — Define the hardware and software capabilities of a single agent.
3. **Local Rule Hypothesis** — Guess the local rules that *might* produce the global goal.
4. **Simulation** — Run the local rules in a multi-agent simulator (e.g., ARGoS, Gazebo).
5. **Measurement** — Quantify the global behavior using metrics (coverage, time, energy).
6. **Tuning** — Adjust weights $w_k$, gains $\gamma_k$, or transition probabilities $p_{ij}$.
7. **Deployment** — Transfer to physical robots and validate in real-world trials.

**Reverse Engineering of Local Rules (3 Marks):**
The reverse-engineering process is iterative:

- **Top-down goal** → Break into sub-goals → Each sub-goal corresponds to a **local behavior** → Map behaviors to **sensors and actuators** → Tune interaction parameters.

**Example:** Suppose the global goal is *"spread out uniformly across the arena"*. The designer identifies the sub-goal *"agents must move away from dense regions"*. The local rule is then a **repulsion-from-neighbors** behavior with strength inversely proportional to neighbor count:

$$
\vec{F}_{repel} = -k \cdot \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{centroid}}{\lVert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{centroid} \rVert}
$$

This single local rule, replicated in every agent, produces global uniform dispersion — a textbook reverse-engineering success.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B):**
> - In the **PFSM answer**, students often forget to mention the **row-stochastic constraint** $\sum p_{ij} = 1$. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** if omitted.
> - For the **pipeline question**, do not just list stages — **explicitly mention the iterative loop** (simulation → measurement → tuning → re-simulation). Examiners look for evidence of feedback understanding.
> - In the **reverse-engineering** discussion, the term **"stigmergy"** is a high-value keyword. Use it whenever environment-mediated coordination is discussed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this checklist before the exam — it covers 90% of the high-yield content:**

- [ ] **Bottom-up = local rules + emergence**, NOT central planning.
- [ ] Three sub-families: **Behavior-Based (B), Swarm Intelligence (S), Reactive (R)** — mnemonic **B-S-R**.
- [ ] Reynolds' Boids: **Separation, Alignment, Cohesion** — three steering vectors, linearly combined with weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$.
- [ ] **Separation** scales as $1/r^2$ — stronger for closer neighbors.
- [ ] **Alignment** is velocity matching with neighbors.
- [ ] **Cohesion** is steering toward the local centroid.
- [ ] **Saturation** must be applied: $\lVert \vec{v} \rVert \leq v_{max}$.
- [ ] **Brooks' Subsumption (1986)** — layered AFSMs, hard arbitration, no world model, suppression + inhibition.
- [ ] **Arkin's Motor Schemas (1987)** — soft vector summation, $\vec{V}_{final} = \frac{\sum \gamma_k \vec{V}_k}{\Vert \cdot \Vert}$, smooth trajectories.
- [ ] **PFSM (Krieger & Billeter, 2000)** — stochastic transitions, row-stochastic $\mathbf{P}$, used for foraging.
- [ ] **Stigmergy (Grassé, 1959)** — environment-mediated indirect communication; virtual pheromones.
- [ ] **ACO transition probability:** $P_{ij} = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum [\tau]^\alpha [\eta]^\beta}$.
- [ ] **Pheromone evaporation:** $L_i(t) = L_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ or discrete $L_i(t+1) = (1-\rho)L_i(t)$.
- [ ] **Locality constraint** $r_{comm} \ll r_{arena}$ is the scalability guarantee.
- [ ] **Equilibrium condition** for flocking: all $\vec{v}_i$ equal AND all $\vec{r}_i$ in a bounded cluster.
- [ ] **Emergence** is the macro-pattern arising from micro-interactions — not pre-programmed.
- [ ] **Advantages over top-down:** scalability, fault tolerance, lower comm overhead, dynamic environment robustness.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** harder to predict, harder to verify formally, emergent bugs possible.
- [ ] **Design pipeline:** Goal → Agent Spec → Local Rule Hypothesis → Simulation → Measure → Tune → Deploy (iterative loop).
- [ ] **Reverse engineering** of local rules: top-down goal → sub-goals → local behaviors → sensor/actuator mapping → interaction tuning.
- [ ] **Canonical local rule → global behavior mappings:** Sep+Align+Coh → flocking; Wander+Aggregate → clustering; Forage+Return+Pheromone → adaptive path optimization.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** Amazon Kiva warehouses, agricultural swarms, Mars rover formations, disaster-response rubble exploration.
- [ ] **Tuning rule of thumb:** separation weight $w_1$ is usually the **largest** to prevent collision; cohesion $w_3$ is moderate; alignment $w_2$ is the smallest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain bottom-up design," **always start with the bird-flock analogy** (1 sentence), then formalize with the emergence equation $\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{F}(B_i, I_{local}, \Pi_{comm})$, then give **two concrete examples** (boids + stigmergy), and finally state the **limitations**. This four-beat structure is what separates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Top-Down Design Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Top-Down Design Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Top-Down Design Approach** in Cooperative Robotics is a hierarchical, goal-directed engineering methodology in which the global mission objective of a multi-robot system (MRS) is decomposed, planned, and prescribed by a higher-level authority (either a central planner or a designated leader), and subsequently refined into elementary motion primitives that are assigned to and executed by individual robots within the team.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Top-down design approaches treat the multi-robot system as a single unified entity at the highest abstraction layer. The collective goal is **explicitly known a priori**, the task decomposition tree is **constructed offline**, and the allocation of sub-tasks to individual agents is performed using **deterministic or optimization-based methods** such as auction theory, integer linear programming, or market-based mechanisms.

Mathematically, the top-down philosophy asserts that a global mission $\mathcal{M}$ can be partitioned into a set of atomic tasks:

$$\mathcal{M} \;=\; \big\{ \tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3, \dots, \tau_N \big\}$$

These tasks are then mapped onto a heterogeneous robot team $\mathcal{R} = \{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_K\}$ through an explicit allocation function:

$$\mathcal{A} : \mathcal{M} \times \mathcal{R} \;\longrightarrow\; \{0, 1\}^{N \times K}$$

where the entry $\mathcal{A}(\tau_i, R_j) = 1$ implies that robot $R_j$ has been assigned task $\tau_i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **orchestra conductor standing at the podium**. Before a single note is played:

1. The conductor (top-level planner) reads the entire symphony score (global mission).
2. The score is broken down into sections — violins, cellos, brass, percussion (hierarchical task decomposition).
3. Each musician (robot) is given a specific part (task allocation).
4. The conductor synchronizes timing, tempo, and dynamics (coordination control).
5. Each musician then only focuses on playing their assigned notes (low-level execution).

The orchestra never decides on its own what to play — the **structure flows from the top down**. If the conductor fails, the music collapses. This is the essence of a top-down cooperative robotic system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Top-down design is the **antithesis** of bottom-up (behavior-based/emergent) design. In bottom-up, no robot knows the global goal; intelligence *emerges* from local interactions. In top-down, the global goal is **explicitly encoded** at the highest layer.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Top-Down Approaches

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Implication |
|---|----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Global State Awareness** | A planner maintains world knowledge of all robots and obstacles |
| 2 | **Offline Pre-Planning** | Task graphs and paths are computed *before* execution begins |
| 3 | **Deterministic Allocation** | Each robot is bound to a specific task contract |
| 4 | **Optimality-Driven** | Cost, time, and energy functions are minimized globally |
| 5 | **Predictable Behavior** | The mission trajectory can be verified before deployment |
| 6 | **Single Point of Failure** | Vulnerable if the central planner crashes |
| 7 | **Scalability Limit** | Computationally expensive as robot count $K$ grows |

## 1.4 Historical and Academic Context

The modern formulation of top-down task allocation in MRS is rooted in the seminal works of **Parker (1998)** on ALLIANCE, **Gerkey & Matarić (2002)** on MURDOCH and market-based architectures, and **Korsah, Stentz & Dias (2013)** on dynamic task allocation. These works remain standard references in cooperative robotics curricula worldwide, including the KTU 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Stack of Top-Down Design

The top-down architecture in cooperative robotics is conventionally organized into **five vertical abstraction layers**, each consuming the output of the layer above it.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-Layer Top-Down Stack (KTU Board Favourite)**
> 
> 1. **Mission Layer** — Defines the global objective (e.g., "search and map a disaster zone").
> 2. **Task Layer** — Decomposes the mission into discrete sub-tasks (waypoints, search regions).
> 3. **Allocation Layer** — Assigns sub-tasks to robots via optimization or auction.
> 4. **Coordination Layer** — Synchronizes trajectories, resolves conflicts, manages formations.
> 5. **Execution Layer** — Converts plans into motor commands, executes primitives.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Top-Down Allocation

### 2.2.1 Cost Matrix Representation

Given $N$ tasks and $K$ robots, the **cost matrix** $\mathbf{C}$ encodes the cost incurred if robot $R_j$ executes task $\tau_i$:

$$\mathbf{C} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_{11} & c_{12} & \cdots & c_{1K} \\ c_{21} & c_{22} & \cdots & c_{2K} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ c_{N1} & c_{N2} & \cdots & c_{NK} \end{bmatrix}_{N \times K}$$

Typical cost components include distance, energy expenditure, time-to-completion, and risk.

### 2.2.2 Utility Function for a Single Bidder

In market-based top-down systems, robot $R_j$ computes a **scalar utility** for each task it can perform:

$$U_j(\tau_i) \;=\; \alpha \cdot G_j(\tau_i) \;-\; \beta \cdot C_j(\tau_i) \;-\; \gamma \cdot P_j(\tau_i)$$

where:
- $G_j(\tau_i)$ = expected **gain** (reward) for completing the task.
- $C_j(\tau_i)$ = computational / energetic **cost** of execution.
- $P_j(\tau_i)$ = **risk penalty** (probability of failure × severity).
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting constants, with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.

### 2.2.3 Global Optimal Assignment Objective

The central planner solves the optimization:

$$\mathcal{A}^{*} \;=\; \arg\min_{\mathcal{A}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{K} c_{ij} \cdot \mathcal{A}(\tau_i, R_j)$$

subject to:
- Each task assigned to exactly one robot: $\sum_{j} \mathcal{A}(\tau_i, R_j) = 1 \;\;\forall i$
- Each robot receives at most one task (Hungarian case): $\sum_{i} \mathcal{A}(\tau_i, R_j) \leq 1 \;\;\forall j$

### 2.2.4 Consensus-Based Leader-Follower Formation

In top-down **formation control**, a designated leader $R_L$ dictates the reference trajectory, and followers $R_i$ maintain offsets $\mathbf{d}_i$ in the leader's frame:

$$\mathbf{x}_i^{ref}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{x}_L(t) \;+\; \mathbf{R}(\theta_L(t)) \cdot \mathbf{d}_i$$

where $\mathbf{x}_L(t)$ is the leader's position, $\theta_L(t)$ is its heading, and $\mathbf{R}(\theta_L)$ is the 2D rotation matrix:

$$\mathbf{R}(\theta_L) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_L & -\sin\theta_L \\ \sin\theta_L & \phantom{-}\cos\theta_L \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.2.5 Consensus Convergence (Centralized Estimator)

For state estimation in a top-down data-fusion framework, the centralized estimator uses a weighted average:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t) \;=\; \left(\sum_{j=1}^{K} \mathbf{W}_j \right)^{-1} \sum_{j=1}^{K} \mathbf{W}_j \cdot \mathbf{x}_j(t)$$

where $\mathbf{W}_j$ is the inverse covariance (information matrix) of robot $R_j$.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** All formulas below are *high-yield* — they appear regularly in KTU board examinations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Mission Decomposition | $\mathcal{M} = \{\tau_1, \dots, \tau_N\}$ | Splits global objective into atomic tasks |
| 2 | Cost Matrix | $c_{ij}$ = cost of $R_j$ doing $\tau_i$ | Input to allocation algorithms |
| 3 | Utility Function | $U_j(\tau_i) = \alpha G - \beta C - \gamma P$ | Market-based bidding mechanism |
| 4 | Global Allocation Optimum | $\mathcal{A}^{*} = \arg\min \sum c_{ij} \mathcal{A}_{ij}$ | Hungarian / ILP solution |
| 5 | Hungarian Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(K^3)$ | Time cost for $K$-robot assignment |
| 6 | Leader-Follower Offset | $\mathbf{x}_i^{ref} = \mathbf{x}_L + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{d}_i$ | Formation trajectory reference |
| 7 | Centralized Estimator | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\sum \mathbf{W}_j)^{-1} \sum \mathbf{W}_j \mathbf{x}_j$ | Optimal sensor fusion |
| 8 | Consensus Iter. (Discrete) | $x_i[k+1] = \sum_j a_{ij} x_j[k]$ | Distributed agreement to leader value |
| 9 | Auction Convergence | $\|\mathbf{U}^{(t+1)} - \mathbf{U}^{(t)}\| \leq \epsilon$ | Termination of bid rounds |
| 10 | Fault Tolerance Ratio | $\phi = 1 - \frac{K_f}{K}$ where $K_f$ = failed robots | Degradation after leader loss |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Top-Down Design

Top-down approaches are deployed in real-world cooperative robotics when:

- **Determinism is critical**: e.g., search-and-rescue UAV swarms, military reconnaissance.
- **Pre-mission planning is feasible**: warehouse fleets with known layouts.
- **Optimality matters more than robustness**: minimum-energy exploration missions.
- **Communication infrastructure is reliable**: tethered or networked deployments.
- **Regulatory certification is required**: top-down logs are auditable (aviation, autonomous trucking).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Use Cases:** Amazon Robotics (Kiva systems), Google Waymo truck platoons, NASA Mars rover teams, and agricultural weeding swarms (e.g., Naio Technologies) all employ variants of top-down task allocation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Task Allocation via the Hungarian Method

We derive the centralized allocation result for a square $K \times K$ cost matrix (equal number of tasks and robots). This derivation is **exam-critical**.

### Step 1 — Define the Cost Matrix

Let $\mathbf{C} = [c_{ij}]$ where $c_{ij} \geq 0$ is the cost of assigning robot $R_j$ to task $\tau_i$.

$$\mathbf{C} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_{11} & c_{12} & \cdots & c_{1K} \\ c_{21} & c_{22} & \cdots & c_{2K} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ c_{K1} & c_{K2} & \cdots & c_{KK} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Row Reduction

Subtract the minimum element of each row from every element in that row. This makes each row contain at least one zero.

$$c_{ij}^{(1)} \;=\; c_{ij} \;-\; \min_{k} \{ c_{ik} \}, \quad \forall i, j$$

**Logic:** Adding a constant to an entire row does not change the optimal assignment, because the constant adds the same offset to any feasible permutation.

### Step 3 — Column Reduction

Subtract the minimum element of each column (from the already row-reduced matrix) from every element in that column.

$$c_{ij}^{(2)} \;=\; c_{ij}^{(1)} \;-\; \min_{k} \{ c_{kj}^{(1)} \}, \quad \forall i, j$$

**Logic:** Similarly, a constant added to a column does not alter the optimal matching, since exactly one element is chosen per column in any feasible permutation.

### Step 4 — Cover All Zeros with Minimum Lines

Draw the minimum number of horizontal and vertical lines needed to cover all zero entries. Let this count be $\ell$.

### Step 5 — Test Optimality

- If $\ell = K$ (number of lines equals matrix dimension), an **optimal assignment exists**.
- The assignment is found by selecting one zero per row and column such that no two share a row or column.

### Step 6 — Augment if $\ell < K$

Find the smallest uncovered element $m = \min \{ c_{ij}^{(2)} \}$. Subtract $m$ from every uncovered element and add $m$ to every doubly-covered element. Repeat from Step 4.

### Step 7 — Total Cost Computation

Once the optimal assignment permutation $\sigma^{*}$ is found, the minimum total cost is:

$$C_{total}^{*} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{K} c_{i, \sigma^{*}(i)}$$

### Step 8 — Computational Complexity

Each iteration of the Hungarian method takes $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$, and the method converges in at most $K$ iterations, yielding:

$$T_{Hungarian} \;=\; \mathcal{O}(K^3)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Utility Function in Market-Based Top-Down Auction

In a centralized sequential auction, the planner broadcasts task $\tau_i$. Each robot responds with a bid $b_j(\tau_i)$. The planner awards the contract to the lowest bidder.

### Step 1 — Define the Marginal Cost

Robot $R_j$ estimates the additional cost it would incur by taking on task $\tau_i$, given its current workload $W_j$:

$$c_j(\tau_i \mid W_j) \;=\; \frac{d_j(\tau_i)}{v_j} \cdot w_d \;+\; E_j(\tau_i) \cdot w_e \;+\; \frac{1}{1 - W_j / W_j^{max}} \cdot w_s$$

where:
- $d_j(\tau_i)$ = travel distance to task
- $v_j$ = robot's nominal velocity
- $E_j(\tau_i)$ = energy to execute task
- $W_j^{max}$ = maximum workload capacity
- $w_d, w_e, w_s$ = weighting constants

### Step 2 — Form the Bid

The bid is the sum of marginal cost plus a small profit margin $\eta_j$:

$$b_j(\tau_i) \;=\; c_j(\tau_i \mid W_j) \cdot (1 + \eta_j)$$

### Step 3 — Form the Utility

The utility that the planner gains by accepting robot $R_j$'s bid is:

$$U_j(\tau_i) \;=\; R(\tau_i) \;-\; b_j(\tau_i)$$

where $R(\tau_i)$ is the planner-defined reward for completing $\tau_i$.

### Step 4 — Award Condition

The planner awards the contract to:

$$j^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{j \in \mathcal{J}} \; U_j(\tau_i)$$

where $\mathcal{J}$ is the set of robots that submitted bids.

### Step 5 — Convergence Guarantee

The auction converges when the bid values stabilize across rounds:

$$\big\vert b_j^{(t+1)}(\tau_i) - b_j^{(t)}(\tau_i) \big\vert \;\leq\; \epsilon, \quad \forall j \in \mathcal{J}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (typically $10^{-3}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Leader-Follower Formation Error Dynamics

Consider a leader $R_L$ at position $\mathbf{x}_L = [x_L, y_L]^T$ and a follower $R_i$ that must maintain a rigid offset $\mathbf{d}_i = [d_{ix}, d_{iy}]^T$ in the leader's local frame.

### Step 1 — Reference Position for Follower

$$\mathbf{x}_i^{ref}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{x}_L(t) \;+\; \mathbf{R}(\theta_L(t)) \cdot \mathbf{d}_i$$

Expanding the rotation:

$$\mathbf{x}_i^{ref}(t) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x_L + d_{ix}\cos\theta_L - d_{iy}\sin\theta_L \\ y_L + d_{ix}\sin\theta_L + d_{iy}\cos\theta_L \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Define Tracking Error

$$\mathbf{e}_i(t) \;=\; \mathbf{x}_i(t) \;-\; \mathbf{x}_i^{ref}(t)$$

### Step 3 — Control Law (Proportional Feedback)

A simple top-down control law drives the error to zero:

$$\mathbf{u}_i(t) \;=\; -K_p \, \mathbf{e}_i(t) \;-\; K_d \, \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i(t)$$

where $K_p, K_d > 0$ are PD gains.

### Step 4 — Stability Condition

The closed-loop error dynamics $\ddot{\mathbf{e}}_i + K_d \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i + K_p \mathbf{e}_i = 0$ are stable if and only if:

$$K_p > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad K_d > 0$$

yielding characteristic roots with strictly negative real parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU boards:** Many cooperative robotics questions ask for "conditions under which the leader-follower formation remains stable." The answer is exactly the inequality above.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralized vs Hierarchical Top-Down:** Both fall under the top-down umbrella, but differ in robustness.

| Property | Pure Centralized | Hierarchical (Multi-tier) |
|----------|------------------|----------------------------|
| Decision locus | Single computer | Tiered authority chain |
| Robustness to planner failure | **Very Low** | Moderate |
| Optimality of allocation | **Global optimum** | Near-optimal |
| Scalability to $K > 50$ | Poor | Moderate |
| Communication overhead | $O(K)$ per cycle | $O(\sqrt{K})$ per cycle |
| Example framework | MURDOCH | ALLIANCE / L-ALLIANCE |
| KTU exam weightage | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Layer Top-Down Architectural Stack

This Mermaid diagram maps the canonical five-layer top-down cooperative robotics architecture, showing data and control flow from the highest mission layer down to the lowest execution layer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeM[Mission Planner] --> nodeT[Task Decomposer]
    nodeT --> nodeA[Task Allocator]
    nodeA --> nodeC1[Coordinator Robot 1]
    nodeA --> nodeC2[Coordinator Robot 2]
    nodeA --> nodeC3[Coordinator Robot N]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE1[Motion Planner Robot 1]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE2[Motion Planner Robot 2]
    nodeC3 --> nodeE3[Motion Planner Robot N]
    nodeE1 --> nodeL1[Low Level Controller Robot 1]
    nodeE2 --> nodeL2[Low Level Controller Robot 2]
    nodeE3 --> nodeL3[Low Level Controller Robot N]
    nodeL1 --> nodeF1[Wheel Motor Robot 1]
    nodeL2 --> nodeF2[Wheel Motor Robot 2]
    nodeL3 --> nodeF3[Wheel Motor Robot N]
    nodeA -. feedback .-> nodeT
    nodeE1 -. state .-> nodeC1
    nodeE2 -. state .-> nodeC2
    nodeE3 -. state .-> nodeC3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent **downward command flow**, while dashed arrows represent **upward state feedback**. The Task Allocator sits at the critical bottleneck of the system.

## 4.2 Market-Based Auction Task Allocation Flow

This diagram illustrates the sequential single-task auction mechanism, which is a **top-down favorite** in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Mission Initiated]) --> nodePlan[Central Planner Decomposes Mission]
    nodePlan --> nodeBCast[Planner Broadcasts Task Tau I]
    nodeBCast --> nodeBid1[Robot 1 Computes Bid]
    nodeBCast --> nodeBid2[Robot 2 Computes Bid]
    nodeBCast --> nodeBid3[Robot N Computes Bid]
    nodeBid1 --> nodeEval[Planner Evaluates All Bids]
    nodeBid2 --> nodeEval
    nodeBid3 --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodeDec{Is Convergence Reached?}
    nodeDec -->|No| nodeReask[Planner Requests Revised Bids]
    nodeReask --> nodeBid1
    nodeReask --> nodeBid2
    nodeReask --> nodeBid3
    nodeDec -->|Yes| nodeAward[Planner Awards Contract to Best Robot]
    nodeAward --> nodeExec[Winning Robot Executes Task]
    nodeExec --> nodeCheck{All Tasks Complete?}
    nodeCheck -->|No| nodeBCast
    nodeCheck -->|Yes| nodeEnd([Mission Complete])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Top-Down Multi-Robot System

This diagram provides the **functional block-level view**, which is required when physical drawings are infeasible.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeGO[Global Objective] --> nodeKS[Knowledge Store]
    nodeGO --> nodeTP[Task Planner Module]
    nodeKS --> nodeTP
    nodeTP --> nodeTA[Task Allocator Module]
    nodeTA --> nodeCC[Central Coordinator]
    nodeCC --> nodeR1[Robot 1 Subsystem]
    nodeCC --> nodeR2[Robot 2 Subsystem]
    nodeCC --> nodeRN[Robot N Subsystem]
    nodeR1 --> nodeS1[Sensors Robot 1]
    nodeR2 --> nodeS2[Sensors Robot 2]
    nodeRN --> nodeSN[Sensors Robot N]
    nodeS1 -. feedback .-> nodeCC
    nodeS2 -. feedback .-> nodeCC
    nodeSN -. feedback .-> nodeCC
    nodeCC -. telemetry .-> nodeKS
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Task Decomposition Tree

This is the **standard KTU-style tree diagram** showing how a single "Search and Rescue" mission explodes into executable primitives.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot[Search and Rescue Mission] --> nodeSub1[Area Coverage Task]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSub2[Victim Detection Task]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSub3[Supply Delivery Task]
    nodeSub1 --> nodeSub1A[Zone Alpha Mapping]
    nodeSub1 --> nodeSub1B[Zone Beta Mapping]
    nodeSub2 --> nodeSub2A[Thermal Scan]
    nodeSub2 --> nodeSub2B[Audio Triangulation]
    nodeSub3 --> nodeSub3A[Payload Transport]
    nodeSub3 --> nodeSub3B[Drop Primitive]
    nodeSub1A --> nodePrim1[Move To Waypoint]
    nodeSub1B --> nodePrim1
    nodeSub2A --> nodePrim1
    nodeSub3A --> nodePrim1
    nodePrim1 --> nodeMotor[Send Wheel Velocity Command]
```
