- The nonholonomic constraint means a desired orientation cannot be *set* directly; it must be *achieved* through translation that rotates the wheel.

**Why $\sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$ helps (1.5 marks):**
- The function $\sin(\cdot)$ is **odd** and **bounded** between $-1$ and $+1$, automatically wrapping the angle difference.
- It is the *gradient* of the periodic Lyapunov function $V_{\text{ori}} = \tfrac12\sum a_{ij}(1 - \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j))$, so the closed-loop is a **gradient flow on $\mathbb{T}^N$**.
- It guarantees $\dot V_{\text{ori}} \le 0$ globally with no singularities at $\theta_i = \theta_j \pm \pi$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the distributed consensus-based control law for a team of $N$ unicycle-type wheeled mobile robots to achieve **position rendezvous** under an undirected connected communication graph $\mathcal{G}$. Clearly state the projection arguments that convert a holonomic consensus law into a nonholonomic one.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Using a suitable Lyapunov function, prove asymptotic convergence of all robot positions to a common meeting point. Comment on the role of the algebraic connectivity $\lambda_2(L)$ in the convergence rate.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark): Holonomic consensus template.*
For a holonomic agent the natural consensus law is the negative gradient of $V_{\text{pos}} = \tfrac12\sum a_{ij}\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2$:

$$\dot x_i^{\text{ideal}} = -k_p \sum_j a_{ij}(x_i - x_j) = k_p \sum_j a_{ij}(x_j - x_i).$$

*Step 2 (1 mark): Unicycle tangent and normal vectors.*
Define $e_i = [\cos\theta_i,\ \sin\theta_i]^T$ and $e_i^\perp = [-\sin\theta_i,\ \cos\theta_i]^T$, which form an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^2$ attached to robot $i$.

*Step 3 (2 marks): Decomposition of the ideal velocity.*
The unicycle can only realise motion along $e_i$, so we write

$$\dot x_i^{\text{ideal}} = (\dot x_i^{\text{ideal}}\cdot e_i)\, e_i + (\dot x_i^{\text{ideal}}\cdot e_i^\perp)\, e_i^\perp .$$

*Step 4 (2 marks): Conversion to unicycle inputs.*

$$v_i = k_p \sum_{j=1}^N a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)\cdot e_i,$$

$$\omega_i^{\text{pos}} = k_p \sum_{j=1}^N a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)\cdot e_i^\perp .$$

This realises the holonomic consensus law *along* the wheel axis and uses the orthogonal component to *steer* the wheel toward better alignment.

*Step 5 (1 mark): Final statement.*
Substituting $(v_i, \omega_i^{\text{pos}})$ into the unicycle kinematics $\dot x_i = v_i e_i$ gives the closed-loop rendezvous dynamics.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1.5 marks): Define the centre-of-mass.*
$\bar x = (1/N)\sum_{i=1}^N x_i$. Differentiate: $\dot{\bar x} = (1/N)\sum v_i e_i$.

*Step 2 (1.5 marks): Use the relative coordinate $z_i = x_i - \bar x$.*

$$\dot z_i = v_i e_i - \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_k v_k e_k.$$

*Step 3 (1.5 marks): Choose the disagreement energy.*
$V = \tfrac12\sum_{i,j} a_{ij}\Vert z_i - z_j\Vert^2 = X^T (L \otimes I_2) X \ge 0$, with equality iff $z_1 = \cdots = z_N$.

*Step 4 (1.5 marks): Differentiate and substitute.*

$$\dot V = -k_p \sum_{i,j} a_{ij}\bigl((x_i - x_j)\cdot e_i\bigr)^2 \le 0.$$

The skew-symmetric cross-term in $\omega_i$ cancels in the time-derivative (verify by expanding and using $e_i \cdot e_i^\perp = 0$).

*Step 5 (1 mark): Conclusion.*
By **LaSalle's invariance principle**, all trajectories converge to the largest invariant set where $\dot V \equiv 0$, which forces $(x_i - x_j)\cdot e_i = 0$ for all $i,j$. Combined with the unicycle constraint, this yields the **agreement subspace**: $x_1 = x_2 = \cdots = x_N$. The convergence rate is governed by $\lambda_2(L) k_p$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Design a distributed control law for **axial alignment** of $N$ nonholonomic wheeled mobile robots. Use the heading-based Lyapunov function $V_{\text{ori}} = \tfrac12\sum a_{ij}\bigl[1 - \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j)\bigr]$ and derive the controller as a gradient flow on the torus $\mathbb{T}^N$.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Extend the result to the **combined position rendezvous + axial alignment** problem. State the combined Lyapunov function and prove that the total closed-loop energy is non-increasing for any connected communication graph.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1.5 marks): State the orientation dynamics.*
For a unicycle, $\dot\theta_i = \omega_i$, so the heading is a single-input, single-output system.

*Step 2 (1.5 marks): Compute the gradient of $V_{\text{ori}}$:*

$$\frac{\partial V_{\text{ori}}}{\partial \theta_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij}\,\sin(\theta_i - \theta_j).$$

*Step 3 (2 marks): Define the gradient-descent controller.*

$$\omega_i^{\text{ori}} = -k_\theta \frac{\partial V_{\text{ori}}}{\partial \theta_i} = k_\theta \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij}\,\sin(\theta_j - \theta_i).$$

*Step 4 (1 mark): Closed-loop time-derivative.*

$$\dot V_{\text{ori}} = \sum_i \frac{\partial V_{\text{ori}}}{\partial \theta_i}\,\dot\theta_i = -k_\theta \sum_i \Bigl(\sum_j a_{ij}\sin(\theta_i - \theta_j)\Bigr)^2 \le 0.$$

*Step 5 (1 mark): Conclusion via LaSalle.*
The set $\mathbb{T}^N$ is compact and $\dot V_{\text{ori}} \le 0$. The largest invariant set with $\dot V_{\text{ori}} \equiv 0$ is $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \cdots = \theta_N \pmod{2\pi}$. **Axial alignment is achieved.**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1.5 marks): Combined control law.*

$$u_i = \begin{bmatrix} v_i \\ \omega_i \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} k_p \sum_j a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)\cdot e_i \\[4pt] k_p \sum_j a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)\cdot e_i^\perp + k_\theta \sum_j a_{ij}\sin(\theta_j - \theta_i) \end{bmatrix}.$$

*Step 2 (1.5 marks): Combined Lyapunov function.*

$$V = V_{\text{pos}} + V_{\text{ori}} = \tfrac12 \sum_{i,j} a_{ij}\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2 + \tfrac12 \sum_{i,j} a_{ij}\bigl[1 - \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j)\bigr] \ge 0.$$

*Step 3 (1.5 marks): Compute $\dot V_{\text{pos}}$.*
Following Section 3.1, $\dot V_{\text{pos}} = -k_p \sum a_{ij}\bigl((x_i - x_j)\cdot e_i\bigr)^2 \le 0$.

*Step 4 (1 mark): Compute $\dot V_{\text{ori}}$.*
From Part (a), $\dot V_{\text{ori}} = -k_\theta \sum_i \bigl(\sum_j a_{ij}\sin(\theta_i - \theta_j)\bigr)^2 \le 0$.

*Step 5 (1.5 marks): Combined result and conclusion.*

$$\dot V = \dot V_{\text{pos}} + \dot V_{\text{ori}} \le 0.$$

The only equilibrium satisfying $\dot V \equiv 0$ is the **agreement configuration** $\{x_1 = \cdots = x_N\} \cap \{\theta_1 = \cdots = \theta_N\}$. By LaSalle's principle, the closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable** at the agreement configuration for any connected undirected graph $\mathcal{G}$, with $k_p, k_\theta > 0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the nonholonomic constraint (–2 marks):** Students often write $\dot x_i = v_i$ and $\dot y_i = 0$, treating the unicycle as a *holonomic* particle. Always use $\dot x_i = v_i\cos\theta_i$, $\dot y_i = v_i\sin\theta_i$.
> 2. **Using $\theta_i - \theta_j$ instead of $\sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$ (–1.5 marks):** Linear differences break down near $\pm\pi$ and fail the global Lyapunov proof. The sine form is *the* accepted KTU-style answer.
> 3. **Ignoring graph connectivity (–1 mark):** A consensus controller may be defined but **not converge** if $\lambda_2(L) = 0$ (disconnected graph). Always state "for a connected undirected graph $\mathcal{G}$".
> 4. **Forgetting the projection step (–2 marks):** The derivation must explicitly show the decomposition $\dot x^{\text{ideal}} = (\cdot) e_i + (\cdot) e_i^\perp$. This is the *crux* of the WMR consensus derivation.
> 5. **Conflating position and orientation consensus (–1.5 marks):** These are *two separate Lyapunov sub-energies* with *two separate proofs*. Marks are awarded for the separation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Rendezvous** = position consensus: $\lim \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert = 0$.
- [ ] **Axial alignment** = orientation consensus on $S^1$: $\lim (\theta_i - \theta_j) = 0 \pmod{2\pi}$.
- [ ] **Unicycle model** is the *canonical* WMR: $\dot x = v\cos\theta$, $\dot y = v\sin\theta$, $\dot\theta = \omega$.
- [ ] **Nonholonomic constraint** $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$ forbids sideways motion.
- [ ] **Projection trick:** decompose the ideal holonomic velocity into $e_i$ and $e_i^\perp$ components.
- [ ] **Linear velocity** $v_i = k_p \sum a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)\cdot e_i$ drives rendezvous.
- [ ] **Angular velocity (position part)** $\omega_i^{\text{pos}} = k_p \sum a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)\cdot e_i^\perp$ steers the wheel.
- [ ] **Angular velocity (orientation part)** $\omega_i^{\text{ori}} = k_\theta \sum a_{ij}\sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$ aligns headings.
- [ ] **Combined law** adds the two angular contributions.
- [ ] **Lyapunov function** $V = V_{\text{pos}} + V_{\text{ori}}$ with $V_{\text{pos}} = \tfrac12\sum a_{ij}\Vert x_i-x_j\Vert^2$ and $V_{\text{ori}} = \tfrac12\sum a_{ij}(1-\cos(\theta_i-\theta_j))$.
- [ ] **Algebraic connectivity** $\lambda_2(L) > 0 \iff$ graph connected $\iff$ rendezvous achievable.
- [ ] **Convergence rate** is governed by $\lambda_2(L)k_p$ (position) and the smallest non-zero eigenvalue of the orientation graph Laplacian (orientation).
- [ ] **LaSalle's invariance principle** on the compact set $\mathbb{T}^N$ closes the asymptotic stability proof.
- [ ] **Graph Laplacian** $L = D - A$ is symmetric positive semi-definite for an undirected graph.
- [ ] **Neighbour set** $\mathcal{N}_i = \{j : a_{ij} = 1\}$ — every WMR uses *only* this set.
- [ ] **Stability condition** is $k_p, k_\theta > 0$ (any positive gains work for a connected graph).
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** truck platooning, warehouse fleets, agricultural swarms, drone formations, AUVs.
- [ ] **The $\sin(\cdot)$ trick** handles the $S^1$ topology of headings automatically.
- [ ] **The projection** $e_i, e_i^\perp$ is the bridge from holonomic consensus theory to nonholonomic WMRs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Formation Control of Multiple Wheeled Mobile Robots with a Virtual Leader
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distributed Formation Control of Multiple Wheeled Mobile Robots with a Virtual Leader

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Formation Control with a Virtual Leader** is a cooperative control strategy in which a group of $n$ wheeled mobile robots (WMRs), each equipped with only **local** sensing and neighbour-to-neighbour communication, are driven to maintain a prescribed geometric **formation shape** by tracking a *virtual leader* (a mathematically defined reference trajectory rather than a physical agent).

The desired formation is specified by a set of **constant offset vectors** $\{d_i \in \mathbb{R}^2 \vert i = 1, 2, \dots, n\}$, such that every follower $i$ must satisfy

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \left( p_i(t) - p_l(t) - d_i \right) = 0$$

where $p_i(t) = [x_i(t),\, y_i(t)]^T$ is the position of robot $i$ and $p_l(t) = [x_l(t),\, y_l(t)]^T$ is the position of the virtual leader. Critically, the control law of each robot is **distributed**: it depends only on its own state and the states of its neighbours in the communication graph $G$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A School of Fish Swimming in Formation**

Imagine a school of golden fish moving through a pond. There is **no conductor fish** telling each fish exactly where to swim. Instead, each fish observes only its immediate neighbours (those within a small radius) and adjusts its speed and turning rate to:

1. **Match the average direction** of its visible neighbours (cohesion/alignment).
2. **Maintain a comfortable spacing** from those neighbours (separation).
3. **Track a common group reference** — say, the centroid's motion — which plays the role of the *virtual leader*.

Replace "fish" with "wheeled robot" and "centroid direction" with a "virtual leader trajectory", and you have the essence of distributed formation control. No robot has global information; the desired geometric shape **emerges** purely from local interactions.

### 1.3 Why a *Virtual* Leader?

> [!TIP]
> A **virtual leader** is an *imaginary* agent whose state $(x_l, y_l, \theta_l)$ is generated mathematically (e.g., from a reference path generator) and is broadcast (or relayed through neighbours) to one or more "pinned" robots. It removes the need for a physical leader robot, simplifying deployment and avoiding a single point of failure.

| Component | Real Leader (Physical) | Virtual Leader (Mathematical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware** | Requires an actual robot | Pure software reference |
| **Failure mode** | Can crash / disconnect | Inherently robust |
| **Implementation** | State must be communicated | State can be generated locally |
| **Flexibility** | Limited by leader dynamics | Arbitrary smooth trajectories possible |

### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Quantities

- **Standard metric:** *Gain constants* $k_1, k_2, k_3 > 0$ that govern convergence rate — typically tuned in the range **$k \in [0.1,\, 5.0$]** for indoor mobile robots.
- **Communication radius:** Each robot has a finite sensing/communication range; only robots within this range become *neighbours*.
- **Graph connectivity:** The communication topology must be **connected** (and ideally contain a spanning tree rooted at the virtual leader) for the closed-loop formation to be stable.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Unicycle Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR) Model

The fundamental plant in this topic is the **non-holonomic unicycle-type WMR**. Its configuration vector is

$$q_i = \begin{bmatrix} x_i \\ y_i \\ \theta_i \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x_i, y_i)$ is the wheel-axle midpoint in an inertial frame and $\theta_i$ is the heading angle. The **kinematic model** is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_i &= v_i \cos(\theta_i) \\
\dot{y}_i &= v_i \sin(\theta_i) \\
\dot{\theta}_i &= \omega_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $v_i$ is the linear (forward) velocity and $\omega_i$ is the angular velocity — the **two control inputs** available to robot $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This system is **non-holonomic**: it has **3 configuration variables but only 2 inputs**, and it cannot be stabilised by any smooth, time-invariant, state-feedback law (Brockett's necessary condition). This is *why* backstepping-based formation control is the canonical solution.

### 2.2 Virtual Leader Dynamics

The virtual leader obeys the same unicycle kinematic model but with **prescribed** (reference) inputs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_l &= v_l \cos(\theta_l) \\
\dot{y}_l &= v_l \sin(\theta_l) \\
\dot{\theta}_l &= \omega_l
\end{aligned}
$$

with $v_l > 0$ assumed (leader does not stop, ensuring controllability of the formation).

### 2.3 Formation Error Coordinates

To analyse tracking, errors are expressed in the **leader's body frame** (rotated by $\theta_l$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} e_{ix} \\ e_{iy} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_l) & \sin(\theta_l) \\ -\sin(\theta_l) & \cos(\theta_l) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_i - x_l - d_{ix} \\ y_i - y_l - d_{iy} \end{bmatrix} \\
e_{i\theta} &= \theta_i - \theta_l
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $e_{ix}$ is the **along-track** error (longitudinal), $e_{iy}$ the **cross-track** error (lateral), and $e_{i\theta}$ the **heading** error.

### 2.4 Error Dynamics

Differentiating and substituting the unicycle + leader models yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{e}_{ix} &= -v_l + v_i \cos(e_{i\theta}) + \omega_l \, e_{iy} \\
\dot{e}_{iy} &= v_i \sin(e_{i\theta}) - \omega_l \, e_{ix} \\
\dot{e}_{i\theta} &= \omega_i - \omega_l
\end{aligned}
$$

These three coupled nonlinear ODEs are the **plant for the formation controller**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for Distributed Formation Control**

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Value** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $q_i$ | Config. of robot $i$ | $[x_i,\, y_i,\, \theta_i]^T$ | m, m, rad |
| $q_l$ | Virtual leader config | $[x_l,\, y_l,\, \theta_l]^T$ | m, m, rad |
| $v_i, \omega_i$ | Linear/angular velocity of $i$ | Control inputs | m/s, rad/s |
| $d_i$ | Desired offset of $i$ from leader | $[d_{ix},\, d_{iy}]^T$ | m, m |
| $e_{ix}, e_{iy}$ | Along/cross-track errors | Rotated by $\theta_l$ | m, m |
| $e_{i\theta}$ | Heading error | $\theta_i - \theta_l$ | rad |
| $a_{ij}$ | Adjacency weight (edge $i \to j$) | $a_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ for binary graph | dimensionless |
| $b_i$ | Pinning gain to leader | $b_i = 1$ if $i$ senses leader, else $0$ | dimensionless |
| $k_1, k_2, k_3$ | Controller gains | $k_1, k_2, k_3 > 0$ | s$^{-1}$, m$^{-1}$, s$^{-1}$ |
| $V$ | Lyapunov function | $\frac{1}{2} \sum (e_{ix}^2 + e_{iy}^2) + \frac{1}{k_2} \sum (1 - \cos e_{i\theta})$ | dimensionless |
| $\dot{V}$ | Lyapunov derivative | $-k_1 \sum e_{ix}^2 - \frac{k_3}{k_2} \sum \sin^2 e_{i\theta} \leq 0$ | dimensionless |

> **NOTE:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ used in formulas denotes absolute value; rendered via `\vert` to preserve table layout.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Distributed formation control with a virtual leader is the **workhorse algorithm** behind:

* **Autonomous platooning of trucks** on highways (SAE Level 4 convoys).
* **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva-style robots) coordinating in fleets.
* **Agricultural swarms** for coordinated sowing / harvesting.
* **Drone light-shows** (with extensions to 3-D and aerial vehicles).
* **Underwater AUV teams** for oceanographic surveying.

The virtual-leader framework is **scalable**, **fault-tolerant**, and **communication-efficient** — exactly the properties demanded by Industry 4.0 deployments.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Unicycle Kinematic Model from Rolling Constraints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step justification of the unicycle model** (so a KTU examiner is satisfied that you *derived*, not merely stated, it).

A standard two-wheel differential-drive WMR has wheel radius $r$, wheel-base $b$, with wheel angular velocities $\omega_R$ (right) and $\omega_L$ (left).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Rolling without slipping at the wheel contact:} \quad 
v &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \qquad \omega = \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \\
\text{(ii) Resolving velocity into Cartesian components:} \quad 
\dot{x}_i &= v_i \cos\theta_i, \qquad \dot{y}_i = v_i \sin\theta_i \\
\text{(iii) Pure rotation about the axle centre:} \quad 
\dot{\theta}_i &= \omega_i \\
\text{(iv) Combining into vector form:} \quad 
\dot{q}_i &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & 0 \\ \sin\theta_i & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_i \\ \omega_i \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **non-holonomic constraint** (no lateral slipping of the wheel) is algebraically:

$$
\dot{x}_i \sin\theta_i - \dot{y}_i \cos\theta_i = 0
$$

This is the classic Pfaffian form $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ and is the *reason* smooth time-invariant feedback linearisation fails (Brockett's theorem).

### 3.2 Derivation: Error Dynamics in Leader Body Frame

Let the *rotated* error coordinates be denoted $e_{ip} = [e_{ix}, e_{iy}]^T$ and the rotation matrix $R(\theta_l)$ be

$$
R(\theta_l) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_l & \sin\theta_l \\ -\sin\theta_l & \cos\theta_l \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Differentiate } e_{ip}: \quad 
\dot{e}_{ip} &= \dot{R}(\theta_l)\begin{bmatrix} x_i - x_l - d_{ix} \\ y_i - y_l - d_{iy} \end{bmatrix} + R(\theta_l)\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_i - \dot{x}_l \\ \dot{y}_i - \dot{y}_l \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{(ii) Use } \dot{R}(\theta_l) &= \omega_l \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta_l & \cos\theta_l \\ -\cos\theta_l & -\sin\theta_l \end{bmatrix} = \omega_l \, J \, R(\theta_l),\ \text{where } J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{(iii) Substitute robot + leader kinematics:} \quad 
\dot{e}_{ip} &= \omega_l J e_{ip} + R(\theta_l)\begin{bmatrix} v_i \cos\theta_i - v_l \cos\theta_l \\ v_i \sin\theta_i - v_l \sin\theta_l \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{(iv) Use angle-sum identities:}\quad 
R(\theta_l)\begin{bmatrix} v_i \cos\theta_i \\ v_i \sin\theta_i \end{bmatrix} &= v_i \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_i - \theta_l) \\ \sin(\theta_i - \theta_l) \end{bmatrix} = v_i \begin{bmatrix} \cos e_{i\theta} \\ \sin e_{i\theta} \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{(v) And } R(\theta_l)\begin{bmatrix} v_l \cos\theta_l \\ v_l \sin\theta_l \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} v_l \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{(vi) Assemble:} \quad 
\dot{e}_{ix} &= -v_l + v_i \cos e_{i\theta} + \omega_l e_{iy} \\
\dot{e}_{iy} &= v_i \sin e_{i\theta} - \omega_l e_{ix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally, $\dot{e}_{i\theta} = \dot{\theta}_i - \dot{\theta}_l = \omega_i - \omega_l$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Backstepping Control Law

We use a Lyapunov-based backstepping procedure. **Step 1** treats $v_i$ as a *virtual* input to the $(e_{ix}, e_{iy})$ subsystem.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Choose a candidate Lyapunov function:} \quad 
V_1 &= \frac{1}{2}(e_{ix}^2 + e_{iy}^2) \\
\text{(ii) Pick } v_i \text{ to render } \dot{V}_1 \text{ negative along the } e_{ix} \text{-direction:} \quad 
v_i &= v_l \cos e_{i\theta} + k_1 e_{ix} \\
\text{(iii) Substitute back:} \quad 
\dot{V}_1 &= e_{ix}\dot{e}_{ix} + e_{iy}\dot{e}_{iy} \\
&= e_{ix}(-v_l + (v_l \cos e_{i\theta} + k_1 e_{ix})\cos e_{i\theta} + \omega_l e_{iy}) + e_{iy}((v_l \cos e_{i\theta} + k_1 e_{ix})\sin e_{i\theta} - \omega_l e_{ix}) \\
&= -k_1 e_{ix}^2 + v_l e_{ix}(\cos^2 e_{i\theta} - 1) + k_1 e_{ix} e_{iy} \sin e_{i\theta} + v_l e_{iy} \cos e_{i\theta} \sin e_{i\theta} \\
\text{(iv) Extend to a full Lyapunov that also penalises heading error:} \quad 
V &= \frac{1}{2}(e_{ix}^2 + e_{iy}^2) + \frac{1}{k_2}(1 - \cos e_{i\theta}) \\
\text{(v) Differentiate and substitute } \omega_i: \quad 
\omega_i &= \omega_l + k_2 v_l \frac{\sin e_{i\theta}}{e_{i\theta}} e_{iy} + k_3 \sin e_{i\theta} \quad \text{(for } e_{i\theta} \neq 0\text{)} \\
\text{(vi) After algebra (use } \sin^2 e_{i\theta}/e_{i\theta} \leq \vert e_{i\theta}\vert \text{):} \quad 
\dot{V} &= -k_1 e_{ix}^2 - \frac{k_3}{k_2} \sin^2 e_{i\theta} \leq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $\dot{V} \leq 0$ and $V$ is radially unbounded, **LaSalle's invariance principle** guarantees asymptotic convergence: $e_{ix}, e_{iy}, e_{i\theta} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

### 3.4 The Distributed Control Law (Graph-Theoretic Form)

For *true* distributed implementation, each robot $i$ uses only neighbour information. Define:

* **Adjacency matrix** $A = [a_{ij}]$ with $a_{ij} = 1$ iff robot $i$ can sense robot $j$.
* **Pinning matrix** $B = \text{diag}(b_1, \dots, b_n)$ with $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicating access to the virtual leader.
* **Desired inter-robot offset** $d_{ij} = d_i - d_j$.

The **distributed formation control law** for a *fully-actuated* point-mass surrogate (then adapted to unicycle via Section 3.3) is:

$$
u_i = -k_1 \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}\big((p_i - p_j) - d_{ij}\big) - k_2 b_i \big((p_i - p_l) - d_i\big)
$$

The closed-loop dynamics form the *consensus + pinning* equation:

$$
\dot{p} = -(L \otimes I_2) \, p - (B \otimes I_2)\,(p - p_l \mathbf{1} - d)
$$

where $L$ is the graph Laplacian. **Stability** requires that $(L + B)$ be **positive definite**, which is guaranteed whenever the leader is *pinned* to at least one robot and the underlying undirected graph is connected.

### 3.5 Connection of the Two Frameworks

> [!TIP]
> The fully-actuated distributed law above is the *conceptual skeleton*. For unicycle robots, we **wrap** Section 3.3's backstepping map around it: replace $p_i - p_l$ by the rotated error $e_{ip}$, and feed $v_i, \omega_i$ through the non-holonomic input transformation. This gives a *distributed*, *non-holonomic*, *virtual-leader* formation controller — exactly what KTU Module 4 demands.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Formation Controller

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the signal-flow architecture for **one follower robot $i$** in the formation. The blocks represent functional processing stages; arrows represent data dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Suite: IMU + Wheel Encoders + LIDAR] --> B[State Estimator: x_i, y_i, theta_i]
    C[Neighbour Communication Module: Receive q_j from N_i] --> D[Neighbour State Buffer]
    B --> E[Error Computation Block]
    D --> E
    F[Virtual Leader Reference Generator: x_l, y_l, theta_l] --> G[Pinning Selector: b_i]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Rotated Error Coordinates: e_ix, e_iy, e_itheta]
    H --> I[Backstepping Control Law: v_i, omega_i]
    I --> J[Wheel Velocity Mapper: omega_L, omega_R]
    J --> K[DC Motor Drivers and Wheels]
    K --> L[Robot i Motion in Workspace]
    L --> A
    L -.-> M[Visualisation: Formation Shape on RViz]
    M -.-> N[Supervisor Monitoring Station]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (sensor → estimator → robot → sensor) is the *physical* feedback loop.
- The **inner loop** (neighbour comms → error block → backstepping) is the *cooperative* feedback loop.
- The **virtual leader block** is shared — it may reside on a base station or be replicated identically on every robot.

### 4.2 Communication Topology Graph (Pinning Illustration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VL[Virtual Leader: q_l]
    R1[Robot 1: pinned b_1 = 1]
    R2[Robot 2]
    R3[Robot 3]
    R4[Robot 4]
    R5[Robot 5]
    VL -. pin link .-> R1
    R1 --- R2
    R2 --- R3
    R3 --- R4
    R4 --- R5
    R1 --- R3
    R2 --- R4
    R3 --- R5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this graph matters:** Robot 1 is the only one directly "pinned" to the virtual leader ($b_1 = 1$, all others $b_i = 0$). Despite this, the formation is stable because the **graph Laplacian + pinning matrix** $L + B$ is positive definite — there exists a spanning tree rooted at the leader.

### 4.3 Hierarchical Control Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 3 Mission Planner: Formation shape selection] --> L2[Level 2 Formation Coordinator: Virtual leader trajectory generation]
    L2 --> L3[Level 1 Distributed Formation Controller: Backstepping on each robot]
    L3 --> L4[Level 0 Low-level Servo: Wheel torque and velocity loops]
    L4 --> L5[Physical Plant: Unicycle WMRs]
    L5 -. feedback .-> L4
    L3 -. feedback .-> L2
    L2 -. feedback .-> L1
```

> **Engineering insight:** Each level operates at a different *time-scale*: mission planning (seconds), formation coordination (100 ms), distributed control (10 ms), low-level servo (1 ms). This hierarchical decomposition is what makes the algorithm deployable on real hardware with finite bandwidth.
