topics.title: Cooperative Surveillance with Multiple UAVs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT862 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cooperative Surveillance with Multiple UAVs # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cooperative Surveillance with Multiple UAVs** is the coordinated deployment of two or more Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) — equipped with heterogeneous on-board sensors (EO/IR cameras, LiDAR, SAR, RF receivers) — that collaborate through shared information, distributed task allocation, and consensus-based decision-making algorithms to monitor, detect, classify, and track targets (static or dynamic) over a predefined **Region of Interest (ROI)** while satisfying kinematic, communication, and energy constraints. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the *Co-operative Robotics* syllabus, cooperative surveillance is treated as a **Multi-Agent Perceptual Task** that fuses three distinct research streams: 1. **Cooperative Perception** — joint estimation of the environment state by sharing sensor data. 2. **Cooperative Task Allocation** — distributed assignment of sub-regions / sub-targets across the UAV team. 3. **Cooperative Motion Planning** — collision-free trajectory generation while maintaining communication connectivity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Multi-UAV surveillance is examined under the umbrella of *Cooperative Decision Making and Control*. Students must clearly distinguish between **centralised**, **decentralised**, and **distributed** cooperative architectures because question papers frequently test the architectural trade-offs. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **The Beach Lifeguard Analogy:** Imagine a crowded beach. A *single* lifeguard on a tall tower has a great overview but limited zoom and cannot simultaneously watch both swimmers near the rocks *and* the far end of the bay. Now consider a *team* of five lifeguards: - Each lifeguard (UAV) covers a **different sub-zone**. - When one spots a struggling swimmer, he **radios (communicates)** the position to the others. - The others immediately re-orient themselves to **box-in and continuously track** the swimmer. - The team dynamically **re-allocates zones** when a lifeguard swaps out for a break. This is exactly how cooperative UAV surveillance works: **partition + share + fuse + re-plan**. ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Surveillance Missions | Mission Type | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Area Coverage** | Sweep a fixed ROI exhaustively | Disaster mapping, border patrol | | **Target Search** | Locate unknown targets of interest | Search & Rescue (SAR) | | **Target Tracking** | Maintain persistent lock on moving targets | Suspect vehicle tracking | | **Event Monitoring** | Detect rare/anomalous events | Wildfire ignition, crowd density | | **Perimeter Surveillance** | Continuous patrol along a boundary | Prison, military base security | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** The mission performance is quantitatively judged by **Coverage Ratio** (fraction of ROI visited over time), **Detection Probability** $P_d$, **False Alarm Rate** $P_{fa}$, and **Track Continuity** (mean number of simultaneous UAVs locking the target). ## 1.4 Sensor Coverage Geometry A single UAV-mounted sensor is characterised by: - **Footprint / Field of View (FoV):** Typically $2\alpha$ angular width (azimuth) by $\beta$ (elevation). - **Ground Sampling Distance (GSD):** $\text{GSD} = \dfrac{H}{f} \cdot p$, where $H$ is altitude, $f$ is focal length, $p$ is pixel pitch. - **Maximum Detection Range $R_d$:** The slant range beyond which $P_d$ falls below a threshold. The **swath width** $W$ on the ground for a downward-looking sensor is: $$W = 2 H \tan(\alpha)$$ This is the fundamental geometric quantity used by KTU examiners when computing required UAV count. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Pillars of Cooperative UAV Surveillance The cooperative surveillance stack is organised into **four logical layers**, each of which can appear as an exam sub-question. ### Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Sensing) - Each UAV $i \in \{1, \dots, N\}$ carries sensor suite $\mathcal{S}_i$ with intrinsic model $z_i = h_i(x) + v_i$, where $v_i$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise with covariance $R_i$. - Sensor returns can be **range-bearing** (LiDAR/radar), **bearing-only** (camera), or **range-only**. ### Layer 2 — Communication Layer - Information exchange governed by an **adjacency matrix** $\mathcal{A} = [a_{ij}]$ where $a_{ij}=1$ if UAVs $i$ and $j$ are within communication range. - The **graph Laplacian** $L = D - \mathcal{A}$ (with $D$ the degree matrix) drives consensus dynamics. - For KTU purposes, assume **undirected connected graph** unless stated otherwise. ### Layer 3 — Information Fusion Layer - Centralised: a ground station fuses all measurements (optimal but fragile). - Decentralised: each UAV maintains its own estimate, exchanged with neighbours using **Consensus on Information (CI)** or **Consensus on Measurements (CM)**. - Distributed: hybrid — no single fusion centre, but estimates converge to the centralised optimal under mild conditions. ### Layer 4 — Decision & Control Layer - Task allocation via Hungarian algorithm, auction-based methods, or market-based approaches. - Path planning using **Voronoi partitioning**, **traveling salesman tour**, or **receding horizon control**. ## 2.2 Cooperative Target Tracking — Mathematical Model Let the target state be $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ evolving as: $$x_{t+1} = F x_t + G w_t, \qquad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$ UAV $i$ measures $z_i = H_i x_t + v_i$, with $v_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_i)$. ### Information Filter Form (preferred for decentralisation) The **information matrix** $Y = P^{-1}$ and **information vector** $y = P^{-1}\hat{x}$ update as: $$\hat{x}_{t+1|t} = F \hat{x}_{t|t}$$ $$Y_{t+1|t} = \left(F P_{t|t} F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top}\right)^{-1}$$ $$Y_{t+1|t+1} = Y_{t+1|t} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$$ $$y_{t+1|t+1} = y_{t+1|t} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} \left(z_i - H_i \hat{x}_{t+1|t}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Information Filter for Multi-UAV?** Because the **additive** form of the information update allows each UAV to **independently contribute** its measurement information to a global sum. This is the key mathematical enabler of decentralised cooperative tracking. ## 2.3 Voronoi Partition-Based Coverage For *area coverage* missions, divide the ROI $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ into **Voronoi cells** $\mathcal{V}_i$: $$\mathcal{V}_i = \left\{ q \in \Omega : \left\Vert q - p_i \right\Vert \leq \left\Vert q - p_j \right\Vert, \forall j \neq i \right\}$$ Each UAV is responsible for monitoring its own cell. The **Centroidal Voronoi Tessellation (CVT)** — where each UAV sits at the centroid of its cell — minimises the average squared distance from any point in the ROI to the nearest UAV, giving the **optimal static coverage configuration**. ## 2.4 Consensus-Based Distributed Estimation Each UAV $i$ maintains local estimate $\hat{x}_i(k)$ updated as: $$\hat{x}_i(k+1) = \hat{x}_i(k) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij} \left(\hat{x}_j(k) - \hat{x}_i(k)\right) + \text{measurement correction}$$ The first term drives the estimates toward consensus; the second term injects new sensor data. Under a connected graph and proper step-size $\epsilon$, all $\hat{x}_i$ converge to the centralised optimum. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Swath width | $W = 2H\tan(\alpha)$ | metres, $H$ = altitude, $\alpha$ = half-FoV | | 2 | GSD | $\text{GSD} = \dfrac{Hp}{f}$ | metres / pixel | | 3 | Required UAVs (parallel sweep) | $N \geq \dfrac{L_{ROI}}{W} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_{passes}}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Target motion | $x_{t+1} = F x_t + G w_t$ | state vector | | 5 | Measurement model | $z_i = H_i x_t + v_i$ | per UAV | | 6 | Kalman predict | $\hat{x}_{t+1\mid t} = F\hat{x}_{t\mid t}$ | mean estimate | | 7 | Kalman covariance predict | $P_{t+1\mid t} = F P_{t\mid t} F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top}$ | covariance update | | 8 | Kalman gain | $K_i = P H_i^{\top}(H_i P H_i^{\top} + R_i)^{-1}$ | per UAV | | 9 | Information update | $Y_{t+1} = Y_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_i H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$ | additive form | | 10 | Consensus update | $\hat{x}_i(k+1) = \hat{x}_i(k) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}(\hat{x}_j - \hat{x}_i)$ | $\epsilon \in (0, 1/\Delta_{\max})$ | | 11 | Voronoi cell | $\mathcal{V}_i = \{q: \Vert q - p_i\Vert \leq \Vert q - p_j\Vert\}$ | geometric partition | | 12 | Probability of detection | $P_d(R) = 1 - \exp\left(-\kappa R^{-\eta}\right)$ | empirical, $\kappa,\eta > 0$ | | 13 | Cramer–Rao Lower Bound (single UAV) | $\text{CRLB} = \left(\sum_i H_i^{\top}R_i^{-1}H_i\right)^{-1}$ | cooperative tracking accuracy | | 14 | Communication connectivity | $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ | Fiedler eigenvalue, $\lambda_2 > 0 \Rightarrow$ connected | | 15 | Mission completion time | $T_{\text{mission}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{ROI}}}{N \cdot W \cdot v_{\text{UAV}}}$ | for parallel sweep | > [!NOTE] > **Vertical Bar Convention in Tables:** In the formulas above, all absolute value or norm symbols use $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity. Do not use the bare `|` pipe character inside table cells — it will break rendering. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Disaster Response:** Earthquake / flood zone mapping using UAV swarms with cooperative photogrammetry. - **Defence:** Border surveillance using heterogeneous UAV swarms (high-altitude wide-area + low-altitude close-look). - **Smart Agriculture:** Cooperative multi-spectral imaging for crop health assessment. - **Traffic Monitoring:** Distributed UAV network replacing expensive fixed gantry cameras. - **Wildlife Conservation:** Cooperative anti-poaching surveillance over large reserves. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Required Number of UAVs for Full Area Coverage **Problem Setup:** A rectangular ROI of dimensions $L \times B$ must be surveyed using UAVs flying at altitude $H$ with sensor half-FoV $\alpha$. UAVs fly at constant ground speed $v$ in parallel tracks (lawnmower pattern) with no overlap and no gap. **Step 1 — Express the swath width covered by one UAV track.** The sensor FoV projects a rectangular strip on the ground. With the sensor looking straight down, the swath width $W$ is given by: $$W = 2 H \tan(\alpha)$$ This is because the two rays defining the FoV make angle $\alpha$ on either side of the nadir, and both reach the ground at horizontal distance $H\tan(\alpha)$. **Step 2 — Count the number of parallel tracks to cover the breadth $B$.** For a *gapless* coverage in the lateral direction: $$n_{\text{tracks}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{B}{W} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{B}{2H\tan(\alpha)} \right\rceil$$ **Step 3 — Determine number of UAVs needed in parallel (per pass).** If we allow one UAV per track and want to complete the entire mission in a single pass (i.e., all UAVs fly simultaneously along parallel tracks), the number of UAVs equals: $$N_{\text{UAV}} = n_{\text{tracks}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{B}{2H\tan(\alpha)} \right\rceil$$ **Step 4 — Account for finite mission time constraint.** If the mission must be completed in time $T_{\text{max}}$, the length each UAV must traverse is: $$\ell = \sqrt{L^2 + (n_{\text{tracks}} W)^2} \approx L + n_{\text{tracks}} \cdot W \quad \text{(with turn ignored)}$$ The time required is: $$T = \dfrac{\ell}{v}$$ If $T > T_{\text{max}}$, additional UAVs are placed in staggered formation along the same tracks: $$N_{\text{total}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T}{T_{\text{max}}} \right\rceil \cdot n_{\text{tracks}}$$ **Step 5 — Final compact formula.** The general KTU-ready closed-form expression: $$N_{\text{UAV}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{B}{2H\tan(\alpha)} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{v \cdot T_{\text{max}}} \right\rceil$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Cooperative Information Filter Step **Step 1 — Convert Kalman filter to information form.** The Kalman filter posterior update is: $$P_{t+1\mid t+1}^{-1} = P_{t+1\mid t}^{-1} + H^{\top} R^{-1} H$$ The information matrix is the inverse covariance. Define $Y_{t+1\mid t+1} = P_{t+1\mid t+1}^{-1}$ and similarly for prediction. Then: $$Y_{t+1\mid t+1} = Y_{t+1\mid t} + H^{\top} R^{-1} H$$ **Step 2 — Extend to multiple UAVs.** If each UAV $i$ provides a measurement $z_i$ with Jacobian $H_i$ and noise covariance $R_i$, and assuming the measurements are conditionally independent given the state: $$Y_{t+1\mid t+1} = Y_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$$ **Step 3 — Corresponding information vector update.** The information vector $y = Y\hat{x}$ updates as: $$y_{t+1\mid t+1} = Y_{t+1\mid t} \hat{x}_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1}\left(z_i - H_i \hat{x}_{t+1\mid t}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Predicted information (from prediction step).** Applying matrix inversion lemma to the Riccati recursion: $$\begin{aligned} P_{t+1\mid t} &= F P_{t\mid t} F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top} \\ Y_{t+1\mid t} &= \left(F P_{t\mid t} F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top}\right)^{-1} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Distributed realisation.** In a decentralised setup, each UAV $i$ broadcasts to neighbours the pair $(y_i, Y_i)$. The receiver performs: $$\begin{aligned} Y_{\text{merged}} &= Y_{\text{self}} + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} Y_j \\ y_{\text{merged}} &= y_{\text{self}} + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} y_j \end{aligned}$$ This is the basis of **Channel Filter / Covariance Intersection** methods for cooperative estimation. ## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Two UAVs track a target moving on a 1-D line with state $x_t = [p_t, \dot{p}_t]^{\top}$. $$\begin{aligned} F &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta T \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta T^2/2 \\ \Delta T \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = \sigma_q^2 \\ \Delta T &= 1\text{ s}, \quad \sigma_q = 0.1 \end{aligned}$$ UAV 1 measures **position only**: $H_1 = [1, 0]$, $R_1 = 0.5^2$. UAV 2 measures **velocity only**: $H_2 = [0, 1]$, $R_2 = 0.3^2$. Initialise $P_0 = \text{diag}(4, 1)$. Compute the cooperative information matrix after one prediction–update cycle assuming the measurement residual terms cancel (i.e., only the covariance update is required). **Step 1 — Predicted covariance.** $$\begin{aligned} P_{1\mid 0} &= F P_0 F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} (0.1)^2 \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2.5\times 10^{-6} & 5\times 10^{-5} \\ 5\times 10^{-5} & 10^{-3} \end{bmatrix} \\ &\approx \begin{bmatrix} 5.0000 & 1.0001 \\ 1.0001 & 1.0010 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Predicted information matrix.** $$Y_{1\mid 0} = P_{1\mid 0}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0000 & 1.0001 \\ 1.0001 & 1.0010 \end{bmatrix}^{-1}$$ Using the formula for the inverse of a $2\times 2$ matrix: $$Y_{1\mid 0} = \dfrac{1}{\det(P)} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0010 & -1.0001 \\ -1.0001 & 5.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\det(P) = (5.0000)(1.0010) - (1.0001)^2 \approx 5.0050 - 1.0002 = 4.0048$$ $$Y_{1\mid 0} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.2499 & -0.2497 \\ -0.2497 & 1.2485 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Information contribution from UAV 1 (position sensor).** $$H_1^{\top} R_1^{-1} H_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} (4) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Information contribution from UAV 2 (velocity sensor).** $$H_2^{\top} R_2^{-1} H_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \left(\dfrac{1}{0.09}\right) \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 11.1111 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Cooperative information matrix (sum).** $$Y_{1\mid 1} = Y_{1\mid 0} + H_1^{\top}R_1^{-1}H_1 + H_2^{\top}R_2^{-1}H_2$$ $$Y_{1\mid 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2499 + 4 & -0.2497 + 0 \\ -0.2497 + 0 & 1.2485 + 11.1111 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.2499 & -0.2497 \\ -0.2497 & 12.3596 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Final cooperative posterior covariance.** $$P_{1\mid 1} = Y_{1\mid 1}^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.2370 & 0.00479 \\ 0.00479 & 0.0815 \end{bmatrix}$$ Compare with single-UAV (only UAV 1): $$P_{\text{single}} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0000 + 4 & 1.0001 \\ 1.0001 & 1.0010 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1667 & -0.1667 \\ -0.1667 & 0.8335 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \text{ (approximate)}$$ The cooperative result is **strictly better** (lower covariance) — confirming the value of multi-UAV tracking. ## 3.4 Derivation: Cramer–Rao Lower Bound for Cooperative Estimation For an unbiased estimator $\hat{x}$ of state $x$, the covariance satisfies: $$\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{x} - x)(\hat{x} - x)^{\top}\right] \succeq \mathcal{I}^{-1}(x)$$ where $\mathcal{I}(x)$ is the **Fisher Information Matrix (FIM)**: $$\mathcal{I}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top}(x) R_i^{-1} H_i(x)$$ For linear-Gaussian models the KF covariance **equals** the CRLB, making the FIM the natural performance metric for cooperative surveillance. The **Posterior CRLB (PCRLB)** for a moving target with process noise: $$\mathcal{I}_{t+1} = \left(Q_{\text{eff}}^{-1} + \mathcal{I}_{t+1}^{\text{meas}}\right)^{-1}$$ where $Q_{\text{eff}}$ captures the predicted uncertainty growth. Recursively computing $\mathcal{I}_t$ gives the theoretical **best achievable tracking accuracy** of the cooperative UAV team. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define cooperative surveillance and list three advantages of using multiple UAVs over a single UAV for surveillance missions. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Cooperative surveillance is the coordinated use of multiple UAVs equipped with onboard sensors that communicate, share information, and jointly execute perception, tracking, and decision-making tasks over a region of interest. **Three advantages:** 1. **Extended spatial coverage** — multiple UAVs partition a large ROI and complete sweeps in parallel, reducing mission time by a factor of approximately $N$. 2. **Improved estimation accuracy** — cooperative information fusion lowers the CRLB; the posterior covariance equals $\left(\sum_i H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i\right)^{-1}$, which is strictly smaller than any single $H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$. 3. **Fault tolerance and robustness** — if one UAV fails or its communication drops, neighbours can re-allocate tasks, making the mission resilient. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark per advantage — keep the answer crisp and structured.)* ### Question 2: Differentiate between centralised, decentralised, and distributed cooperative surveillance architectures with one example limitation of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Architecture | Description | Limitation | |---|---|---| | **Centralised** | All raw measurements sent to a single ground station that performs fusion | Single point of failure; high bandwidth demand | | **Decentralised** | Each UAV maintains its own estimate, no fusion centre exists | Slower convergence to global optimum; requires consensus iterations | | **Distributed** | Hierarchical or hybrid; nodes share only summary statistics | Implementation complexity; partial observability between subgroups | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **`(a)` (7 Marks)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **Information Filter** formulation for cooperative multi-UAV target tracking. Derive the additive information update law. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the information variables.** The information matrix is the inverse of the covariance: $$Y_t \triangleq P_t^{-1}, \qquad y_t \triangleq P_t^{-1} \hat{x}_t$$ **Step 2 — Recall Kalman recursion.** The standard Kalman predict and update are: $$P_{t+1\mid t} = F P_{t\mid t} F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top}$$ $$P_{t+1\mid t+1}^{-1} = P_{t+1\mid t}^{-1} + H^{\top} R^{-1} H$$ **Step 3 — Invert the prediction covariance.** Using the Woodbury matrix identity, the predicted information matrix is: $$Y_{t+1\mid t} = \left(F Y_{t\mid t}^{-1} F^{\top} + G Q G^{\top}\right)^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Extend to $N$ UAVs with independent measurements.** Since measurements $z_i$ from different UAVs are conditionally independent, the joint measurement information is the sum of individual contributions: $$\begin{aligned} Y_{t+1\mid t+1} &= Y_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i \\ y_{t+1\mid t+1} &= Y_{t+1\mid t} \hat{x}_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1}\left(z_i - H_i \hat{x}_{t+1\mid t}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Significance for cooperative tracking.** The additive structure means that in a decentralised setup, each UAV only needs to broadcast its $(y_i, Y_i)$ contribution. The receiving node computes: $$Y_{\text{fused}} = Y_{\text{self}} + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} Y_j, \qquad y_{\text{fused}} = y_{\text{self}} + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} y_j$$ This is the **Channel Filter** approach. Diagram of the information flow is as shown in Section 4.2 of these notes. --- **`(b)` (7 Marks)** A rectangular ROI of **4 km × 2 km** must be surveyed in **less than 30 minutes** using UAVs flying at altitude **500 m** with sensor half-FoV $\alpha = 30°$ and constant ground speed **20 m/s**. Determine the minimum number of UAVs required, assuming parallel lawnmower sweep with no overlap. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the swath width.** $$W = 2 H \tan(\alpha) = 2 \times 500 \times \tan(30°) = 1000 \times 0.5774 = 577.4 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Number of parallel tracks to cover breadth $B = 2$ km = 2000 m.** $$n_{\text{tracks}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{2000}{577.4} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.464 \rceil = 4 \text{ tracks}$$ **Step 3 — Time to traverse length $L = 4$ km = 4000 m at $v = 20$ m/s.** $$T_{\text{single pass}} = \dfrac{4000}{20} = 200 \text{ s} \approx 3.33 \text{ min}$$ Since $T_{\text{single pass}} = 3.33 \text{ min} < T_{\text{max}} = 30 \text{ min}$, only one UAV per track is needed (no staggering). **Step 4 — Total UAVs.** $$N_{\text{UAV}} = 4 \times 1 = \boxed{4 \text{ UAVs}}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) **`(a)` (7 Marks)** Explain the **Voronoi tessellation** approach for cooperative area coverage. Show that the Centroidal Voronoi Tessellation (CVT) is optimal in the sense of minimising the mean squared distance from ROI points to the nearest UAV. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Voronoi cell definition.** For UAV positions $P = \{p_1, \dots, p_N\}$, the Voronoi cell of UAV $i$ is: $$\mathcal{V}_i(P) = \{q \in \Omega : \Vert q - p_i\Vert \leq \Vert q - p_j\Vert, \forall j \neq i\}$$ **Step 2 — Coverage cost function.** Define the cost as the integrated squared distance from each ROI point to its assigned UAV: $$\mathcal{H}(P) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \int_{\mathcal{V}_i} \Vert q - p_i\Vert^2 \, dq$$ **Step 3 — Lloyd iteration for minimisation.** Setting $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial p_i} = 0$ and using the divergence theorem: $$p_i^{\star} = \dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{V}_i\vert} \int_{\mathcal{V}_i} q \, dq$$ That is, the optimal UAV position is the **centroid** of its Voronoi cell. The Lloyd algorithm iteratively recomputes the Voronoi partition and moves each UAV to its cell's centroid until convergence. **Step 4 — Convergence guarantee.** The Lloyd iteration monotonically decreases $\mathcal{H}(P)$ because each centroidal move reduces the second-moment integral. Convergence is assured for convex ROIs; for non-convex regions, constrained Lloyd variants are used. **Step 5 — Real-world significance.** CVT-based deployment guarantees **maximum uniform coverage** in static-surveillance scenarios. It is the basis of **optimal camera placement**, **sensor network layout**, and **multi-UAV persistent surveillance**. --- **`(b)` (7 Marks)** Discuss the **Consensus on Information (CI)** algorithm for decentralised state estimation in cooperative UAV surveillance. State the update law and convergence conditions. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Local information pair at UAV $i$.** UAV $i$ maintains a local information vector $y_i(k)$ and information matrix $Y_i(k)$ at iteration $k$. **Step 2 — Consensus update.** $$\begin{aligned} y_i(k+1) &= y_i(k) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}\left(y_j(k) - y_i(k)\right) + i_i^{\text{new}} \\ Y_i(k+1) &= Y_i(k) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}\left(Y_j(k) - Y_i(k)\right) + I_i^{\text{new}} \end{aligned}$$ where $i_i^{\text{new}} = H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1}(z_i - H_i \hat{x})$ is the new measurement information from UAV $i$ and $I_i^{\text{new}} = H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$ is its information matrix contribution. **Step 3 — Convergence conditions.** - The communication graph must remain **connected** at all times (i.e., Fiedler eigenvalue $\lambda_2(L) > 0$). - The step-size $\epsilon$ must satisfy $0 < \epsilon < 2 / \lambda_{\max}(W)$ where $W$ is the weight matrix of the consensus protocol. **Step 4 — Convergence result.** Under the above conditions, $y_i(k) \to y^{\star}$ and $Y_i(k) \to Y^{\star}$ for all $i$, where $(y^{\star}, Y^{\star})$ is the centralised posterior information. Thus every UAV asymptotically obtains the **optimal cooperative estimate** without any fusion centre. **Step 5 — Practical considerations.** CI is robust to intermittent communication, packet loss, and dynamic topology. The trade-off is convergence time — typically $O(N^2)$ iterations are needed for $N$ UAVs. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cooperative Surveillance Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Kalman and Information filters** — Students often write the Kalman update when the question asks for information form. Memorise the additive structure: $Y_{t+1} = Y_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_i H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$. > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling operator in UAV count** — Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ for integer UAV count; fractional UAVs do not exist. Marks are routinely lost for writing $N = 3.46$ UAVs. > 3. **Mixing FoV angle conventions** — Some texts use the full FoV $2\alpha$; KTU questions consistently use the **half-angle** $\alpha$. State your convention clearly. > 4. **Skipping the consensus step-size constraint** — When proving convergence of CI, you MUST specify $0 < \epsilon < 2/\lambda_{\max}$, otherwise the proof is incomplete. > 5. **Ignoring graph connectivity** — Forgetting to state that the communication graph must be connected invalidates the cooperative estimation result entirely. > 6. **Unit mismatches** — Swath width $W$ in metres vs. ROI in km. Convert before plugging into the formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cooperative Surveillance with Multiple UAVs:** - **Definition:** Multi-UAV coordinated monitoring using shared information; spans perception, communication, fusion, and decision layers. - **Four Architectures:** Centralised, Decentralised, Distributed, Hierarchical — know advantages and single point-of-failure implications. - **Swath width formula:** $W = 2H\tan(\alpha)$ — half-FoV convention. - **GSD formula:** $\text{GSD} = Hp / f$. - **UAV count formula:** $N = \lceil B / W \rceil \cdot \lceil L / (v T_{\text{max}}) \rceil$. - **Target motion model:** $x_{t+1} = F x_t + G w_t$. - **Measurement model:** $z_i = H_i x_t + v_i$, noise $\sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_i)$. - **Information filter** uses additive updates: $Y_{t+1} = Y_{t+1\mid t} + \sum_i H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$. - **Posterior estimate:** $\hat{x}_{t+1} = Y_{t+1}^{-1} y_{t+1}$. - **Voronoi cells:** $\mathcal{V}_i = \{q : \Vert q - p_i\Vert \leq \Vert q - p_j\Vert, \forall j \neq i\}$. - **Centroidal Voronoi Tessellation (CVT):** Each UAV at the centroid of its cell minimises mean squared distance. - **Lloyd iteration:** Monotonically decreasing cost $\mathcal{H}(P)$; converges for convex ROIs. - **Consensus update:** $\hat{x}_i(k+1) = \hat{x}_i(k) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}(\hat{x}_j - \hat{x}_i)$. - **Convergence condition:** Graph connected ($\lambda_2(L) > 0$) and step-size $0 < \epsilon < 2/\lambda_{\max}(W)$. - **CRLB / FIM:** $\mathcal{I}(x) = \sum_i H_i^{\top} R_i^{-1} H_i$; cooperative accuracy strictly improves as $N$ increases. - **Channel Filter / Covariance Intersection:** Robust distributed fusion that avoids double-counting. - **Applications:** Border patrol, disaster response, agriculture, SAR, wildlife conservation, traffic monitoring. - **Performance metrics:** Coverage ratio, detection probability $P_d$, false alarm rate $P_{fa}$, track continuity, posterior covariance trace. - **Key advantage of multi-UAV:** Spatial coverage parallelism, accuracy improvement via fusion, fault tolerance through re-allocation. - **Exam hot topics:** (1) Derive UAV count for a given ROI; (2) Show equivalence of Kalman and Information forms; (3) Prove CVT optimality; (4) State CI convergence conditions.
